- How To Deal With a Toxic Family: Kind: captions Language: en - [Narrator] There's been a huge decline in mental health around the world, which is why we are so committed to creating more content than we ever have. Thanks so much for being a part of our journey. Do you have a toxic family and you're not sure what to do? Having a toxic family member can be detrimental, because family is supposed to be defined by love and support. This is a betrayal of the highest order, and something, if left untreated, that will haunt someone for the rest of their lives. It's difficult to even admit a family member is toxic, and the treatment isn't easy. Although we do recommend reaching out to a professional for help, here are a few tips to ponder while you decide what to do. Number one: make clear boundaries for yourself. We know that it sounds simple to say, hey I like this, and I don't like that, and you're right, it does seem straightforward, so why does this toxic person continue to be able to cross those boundaries? It might be that, due to fear, being worn down, or not realizing that it's happening, you haven't made the boundaries clear. The problem with toxic family members is not only that they know you well, but they exercise that knowledge regularly to get at you. They know where all the thin spots are. Clarifying a boundary is like adding an extra fortification, or making your house out of brick rather than straw, and filling in all the gaps. So you need to be super clear with yourself on not only what your boundaries are, but why. Give it some backup. Know that your own limit is because toxic people will overstep that boundary repeatedly, so you'll need to determine for yourself how much you'll put up with before you know it's time to walk. They might rush you before the cement has been set properly, so don't worry about failing the first few times. With practice, you'll be able to hold your own. These boundaries will also help to remind you that you have not only the need but the right to protect yourself from these attacks. This means when you cut a call off from your body-shaming mother or block the number to that sibling who keeps cursing at you or demeaning your life, you can understand that it's right for you. You've had enough. Number two: distance. We mean either emotionally or physically. Because physically isn't always realistically possible, emotional distance involves cutting off the gifts you've been giving. What gifts, you ask. The gift of you, the gift of knowing anything meaningful about you, like your interests, goals, secrets, or memories. Those were things you previously shared with them, and they responded by repeatedly, maliciously using that information to hurt you. They have proven themselves undeserving, so you get to stop the flow. It's okay to say you don't wish to talk on a subject, or not answer a question. If you must speak with them, you can have cordial contact, also known as keeping them at an arms' length. This is where conversations and emotions are purposefully superficial, pleasant, and all about them, since toxic people are usually all about themselves anyhow. You can remind yourself this is being done on purpose for your own protection and sanity. You're not giving in to them. If possible, you can have low contact, where you interact only at large occasions like weddings. Then there's also no contact, when even low contact has become unbearable. So don't worry about, what if they've changed? Once toxic family members catch on to what's happening, they'll redouble their efforts to force poison down your throat, letting you know that nothing has changed. In fact, they're worse than before. The most important thing to remember in all of this is that it's not your fault that they did this to you, despite what they're screaming at you. Their repeated abuse, despite all your efforts to appease them or talk to them, is effectively a foot shoving you against the exit, so take the exit and let them deal with the outcome themselves. Number three: don't argue or justify. You know the drill. It's been a blissful week of peace from that condescending, gaslighting family member, but then a call or email will come, and it'll be something that seems to require a conversation. So you, being compassionate, ask them what's going on, and it devolves into drama and arguments. Alert: you might wanna fight back and provide proof to support yourself. Admiral Ackbar says, "It's a trap." Oh yeah, he knew what was up. This toxic family member has reeled you in to a staged argument they know and will ensure you can't win. Their goal is to make you feel low, so they can proclaim superiority. Battles need to be chosen carefully. Determine what arguments and battles are actually for real need to go to war, and which ones are drama and self-made pseudo emergencies. These emergencies are false and solvable by themselves, but they wanna feel the power puppeteering you to their bidding. It's okay to decline being suckered in. You can then conserve your energy for other things, like your own life. Number four: hold strong to yourself. You need to be your own anchor and lighthouse. Having that strong, solid sense of self means accepting and understanding that no one, including yourself, is perfect, and that you still know who you are, regardless of snags along the way. We understand you can feel shame or guilt, but knowing yourself lets you know what to do about it, or even if it's warranted. Did you purposefully and consciously act with malicious intent? When it comes to that toxic family member, chances are pretty good that you didn't. There is nothing shameful or bad about simply being yourself. You do not need the approval of that toxic family member. Maybe ignore all of those well done, son movies. You only need the approval of yourself for yourself. All those degrading, mean things said by toxic people are usually projections of their own shortcomings that they aren't mature or wise enough to deal with. They instead do the cheap, cowardly move, and dump it on others. Their incompetence is not your fault, nor your problem. The rock that is you knows it doesn't matter that a mistake happened. It's not going to destroy you. In fact, it lets you know where you get to uncover more knowledge. And number five: find support. No man is an island, and people work better together. Having and building that support net to catch you is important, even necessary. These are the people you know you can lean on when your I toxic family's attempts to recapture you get hard. Support can also come from a therapist or program, as well as new friends who have nothing to do with your family. Healing and breaking away from abuse is a process that takes time. Be forgiving and patient with yourself. This journey will have you looking deep into your core, and more closely at others. A breakage needs to happen before you're able to start rebuilding, and you know you have the strength to do it. Nobody, including you, deserves abuse, and you are worthy of so much more. What other advice do you think could be helpful? Are you trying any of the tips we talked about? Discuss and share in the comments. Thanks so much for watching and we'll see you soon. Take care.

I know&nbsp; some people like to have a laundry day when&nbsp;&nbsp; they do it all, but it really helps me to feel&nbsp; on top of things if I do it every single day,&nbsp;&nbsp; and many families need to. With all the clothes&nbsp; and the towels and the sheets, I need to put a&nbsp;&nbsp; load on every single morning, so I have a little&nbsp; routine that I do. I put a load of laundry on at&nbsp;&nbsp; breakfast time, then after the school run, I'll&nbsp; put it in the tumble dryer or hang it up to dry,&nbsp;&nbsp; then while dinner is cooking, me or Matt will&nbsp; fold it, and then we take it up with us when we&nbsp;&nbsp; take the boys upstairs for a bath, and sometimes&nbsp; we manage to get it put away, sometimes it sits&nbsp;&nbsp; there for a day, but it just really helps our&nbsp; flow of laundry if we do it every single morning.&nbsp; And the next tip is one I've said a thousand times&nbsp; before on this channel, but it's still such a&nbsp;&nbsp; helpful one, and that is to get everything ready&nbsp; the night before. Your future self will thank you&nbsp;&nbsp; for this, it just really helps with the morning&nbsp; overwhelm if you are prepared, so just think&nbsp;&nbsp; about what you've got going on, what lunches&nbsp; you have to prepare, the snacks, the clothes.&nbsp;&nbsp; Every night, we will lay out our children's&nbsp; clothes on the banister in three little piles,&nbsp;&nbsp; it's what we've always done, and we even do it&nbsp; on the weekend, so even if we're just laying out&nbsp;&nbsp; a tracksuit for them to put on, it just really&nbsp; helps us feel organized. I also plan my outfit,&nbsp;&nbsp; I'll also think about the snacks and who needs&nbsp; lunches, have they got clubs on, who's got karate?&nbsp;&nbsp; There's so much to think about, and I feel like&nbsp; I'm better at doing that the night before, because&nbsp;&nbsp; the morning is just always going to be chaos. And if you do struggle with mornings,&nbsp;&nbsp; you might want to consider getting up&nbsp; before your kids do, because we all know&nbsp;&nbsp; you can get so much done in just an hour&nbsp; when there are no kids running around,&nbsp;&nbsp; and it can also be a really nice calm, quiet&nbsp; start to the day if you get up before the kids,&nbsp;&nbsp; or even if I get up just 20 minutes before&nbsp; the kids and I manage to get my clothes on&nbsp;&nbsp; and do my skincare routine, I already&nbsp; feel like I've had a headstart.&nbsp; Obviously, if you're going to wake up before the&nbsp; kids, you do need to go to bed at a reasonable&nbsp;&nbsp; hour. For instance, I didn't wake up before&nbsp; the kids during the whole lockdown because I&nbsp;&nbsp; was actually staying up late to get work done, so&nbsp; obviously you can't burn the candle at both ends,&nbsp;&nbsp; but if you can go to bed a&nbsp; little bit earlier, then it&nbsp;&nbsp; might actually help to wake up before them. And another tip is to prepare things like lunches&nbsp;&nbsp; and snacks the night before, or the morning of.&nbsp; So I actually like to do this in the morning,&nbsp;&nbsp; and especially when the kids were homeschooling,&nbsp; I found it so helpful for them to have snack boxes&nbsp;&nbsp; or snack baskets. So first thing in the morning,&nbsp; I'd just put all the healthy snacks that I was&nbsp;&nbsp; happy for them to have in their baskets which had&nbsp; their names on it, I'd also put a drink as well,&nbsp;&nbsp; and then throughout the day, I didn't have to&nbsp; hear my kids shouting "Snack" 20 times a day,&nbsp;&nbsp; they could just go to their little boxes, choose&nbsp; what they wanted to eat, and just help themselves.&nbsp; And my next tip is don't sit down. I know this&nbsp; sounds harsh, but I know as soon as I sit down&nbsp;&nbsp; on the couch, I will not be getting up&nbsp; again, I will not be being productive,&nbsp;&nbsp; so I try to get everything done before we actually&nbsp; sit down on the couch. I'll clean the kitchen,&nbsp;&nbsp; I'll put the dishwasher on, I'll sort out the&nbsp; things for the next day as well, and before&nbsp;&nbsp; we go upstairs to the bath, we do a big family&nbsp; speed-clean, because there is nothing worse than&nbsp;&nbsp; taking the kids up to the bath, putting them all&nbsp; to bed, and then coming downstairs to a huge mess.&nbsp; So we've started doing a family tidy-up, and&nbsp; then Matt and I will also spend about 10,&nbsp;&nbsp; 20 minutes tidying up the kitchen so it's all nice&nbsp; and clean, and sometimes we'll spend that time&nbsp;&nbsp; catching up as well, and then once everything's&nbsp; done, I will actually finally sit down in the&nbsp;&nbsp; evening, and honestly, it's the best feeling.&nbsp; When you come downstairs to a nice, clean kitchen,&nbsp;&nbsp; I feel like it's a really nice start to the day. And another top tip is to delegate, especially if&nbsp;&nbsp; you feel like you do everything in your house,&nbsp; it's worth sitting down with your family and&nbsp;&nbsp; talking about how you can share the tasks out more&nbsp; evenly. Maybe your partner could be doing more,&nbsp;&nbsp; maybe you could give your kids some chores,&nbsp; because there are age-appropriate chores&nbsp;&nbsp; for every age. So at toddler level, they could be&nbsp; at least just taking their wrappers to the bin and&nbsp;&nbsp; putting it in the bin, maybe they could help feed&nbsp; a pet. Older kids can make their beds, they can&nbsp;&nbsp; unload the dishwasher, set the table, my boys love&nbsp; to run around with the cordless vacuum as well,&nbsp;&nbsp; so there are things that they could be&nbsp; doing, and you could set up a chore chart,&nbsp;&nbsp; I'll link ours in the description, so at&nbsp; least there's a reward, and they'll feel&nbsp;&nbsp; like they'll achieve something at the end of it. And when Matt and I first got married and had&nbsp;&nbsp; Fraser, I remember I used to get really resentful&nbsp; of him if he didn't just offer to do something&nbsp;&nbsp; if I was holding Fraser or felt like I was&nbsp; doing everything, and I'd think, "Why is he not&nbsp;&nbsp; offering to burp him or thinking about it&nbsp; more?" But I realized, if I just ask him,&nbsp;&nbsp; he would just do it, so then I actually just&nbsp; started asking him to do things, not nagging,&nbsp;&nbsp; but I think he just... It didn't even come into&nbsp; his head, so it's worth just talking to them or&nbsp;&nbsp; sitting down with them before you get resentful. I touched on this point earlier, but something&nbsp;&nbsp; that really helps us is having set tidy up times.&nbsp; So before we walk out the door in the morning at&nbsp;&nbsp; 8:30, we'll run around and tidy up just things&nbsp; like if there's pajamas or toothbrushes out,&nbsp;&nbsp; just put all of that away, and then&nbsp; also before we go upstairs for bath,&nbsp;&nbsp; we'll do a little speed clean. When the kids&nbsp; were younger, we used to put on the Tidy Up Song&nbsp;&nbsp; and run around tidying up to that, but it just&nbsp; kind of like... The kids, they know it's part&nbsp;&nbsp; of the routine now, and they don't mind helping. I also wanted to say that it's okay to accept help&nbsp;&nbsp; when you're offered it, it doesn't make you any&nbsp; less of a super mom if you do accept help. When&nbsp;&nbsp; I had Fraser, if a family member or friend would&nbsp; offer me help, I'd be like, "Oh no, it's okay,&nbsp;&nbsp; I can do it all," but now I think, why did&nbsp; I do that? Because they love to help anyway,&nbsp;&nbsp; and it would actually just make my life so&nbsp; much easier. I also, when I had our third baby,&nbsp;&nbsp; I had a cleaner every two weeks just to help keep&nbsp; the house disinfected. I was like, "Even if it's&nbsp;&nbsp; a complete mess, I know that things have actually&nbsp; been cleaned," and it made all the difference, so&nbsp;&nbsp; if it's something that one, you don't like doing,&nbsp; or that you can afford, then why not get help?&nbsp; My next tip is to find a way of&nbsp; organizing that really works for you,&nbsp;&nbsp; and that you like. There are so many benefits to&nbsp; having an electronic diary, but if something that&nbsp;&nbsp; you're not going to stick to, then don't try and&nbsp; force it. So personally, I really like to have&nbsp;&nbsp; an electronic diary for everything to do with&nbsp; work, because videos and things change so often,&nbsp;&nbsp; if I actually wrote all that down in my written&nbsp; planner, I'd be changing it quite often, but I&nbsp;&nbsp; really like my written planner for everything to&nbsp; do with the kids, family birthdays, meal planning,&nbsp;&nbsp; and all of that, so I actually have two, and I'd&nbsp; also really recommend making loads and loads of&nbsp;&nbsp; lists, and I have two apps that I wanted to&nbsp; recommend to you guys that I really like using.&nbsp; The first one is called Trello, I find it&nbsp; very easy to use. You can move lists around,&nbsp;&nbsp; and you can create as many lists as you want,&nbsp; so I'll often have one for Christmas presents&nbsp;&nbsp; to get the kids, video ideas, food shopping to&nbsp; get, stuff like that. And there's also another&nbsp;&nbsp; really good free app called Google Keep, and&nbsp; so if anything pops into my head, I'll just&nbsp;&nbsp; write it down in one of those apps, and it just&nbsp; works wonders. I think I realized as soon as I&nbsp;&nbsp; had my second child that I couldn't just think,&nbsp; "Oh okay, there's a birthday happening in two&nbsp;&nbsp; weeks' time," like keep it in my head. If I don't&nbsp; write things down, I just completely forget them,&nbsp;&nbsp; especially now that I've got the three&nbsp; of them, so just write everything down.&nbsp; And my next one is a huge one, and that is to meal&nbsp; plan and to meal prep. Now, I've made so many meal&nbsp;&nbsp; prep videos on my channel before, you've probably&nbsp; seen them, but it just saves us so much time, and&nbsp;&nbsp; money as well, because you only get what you need,&nbsp; but our little routine is on a Sunday evening,&nbsp;&nbsp; Matt and I will talk about what meals we want to&nbsp; have the following week. We normally plan just&nbsp;&nbsp; six meals, because we'll tend to get a takeaway&nbsp; at least once a week, so we'll talk about&nbsp;&nbsp; the six meals we want to have, from that, we'll&nbsp; then make an ingredients list of everything&nbsp;&nbsp; that we don't already have, and then on Monday,&nbsp; Matt will actually go to the shops and get it.&nbsp; We used to do an online shop before the pandemic,&nbsp; but during the pandemic, you were limited to only&nbsp;&nbsp; getting 80 items for a food shop online and&nbsp; we would always need more than that, and we&nbsp;&nbsp; were finding there was a lot of replacements&nbsp; happening, so Matt will actually just go,&nbsp;&nbsp; and that is what works for us. It just means that&nbsp; we eat healthily, we feel organized. Every day,&nbsp;&nbsp; we're just like, "Oh, what's on the menu tonight?"&nbsp; And it just makes life feel a lot easier.&nbsp; And another foodie tip is to batch cook whenever&nbsp; possible. So batch cooking is just making&nbsp;&nbsp; more than you actually need, so we will often&nbsp; double or triple a recipe so that we can then&nbsp;&nbsp; put it in the freezer or have it the next&nbsp; night. So if we're making chili one night,&nbsp;&nbsp; we'll double the recipe and we'll make 10 portions&nbsp; rather than five portions, and it just means&nbsp;&nbsp; then we can have a night off another day. So it's&nbsp; really worth doing, make the most of your freezer&nbsp;&nbsp; as well, have some freezer meals to hand, and also&nbsp; things like the slow cooker and the air fryer are&nbsp;&nbsp; your friend, and I use them as much as I can. And my next tip is to designate a day when you&nbsp;&nbsp; do the bulk of your cleaning. Now obviously, as&nbsp; a busy family, we have to be cleaning every day,&nbsp;&nbsp; we have a morning routine and an evening routine&nbsp; for cleaning. If you haven't seen those videos,&nbsp;&nbsp; I'll link them in the description and you can&nbsp; check them out, but I do choose one day...&nbsp;&nbsp; Actually during the pandemic, it has been the&nbsp; weekend, when I clean the bathrooms, I change the&nbsp;&nbsp; sheets on the bed and stuff like that, and I find&nbsp; that it's a bit easier to let things go during the&nbsp;&nbsp; week, because I know that morning, that day, that&nbsp; I'm going to focus on the bulk of the cleaning.&nbsp; Something else I love to do is a 10-minute&nbsp; tidy-up. If there is a very messy room or a very&nbsp;&nbsp; messy cupboard and I'm just dreading it, I'll set&nbsp; a timer on my phone and just focus on that task&nbsp;&nbsp; for the 10 minutes, and you'll be amazed how much&nbsp; you can get done in that time, and it also really&nbsp;&nbsp; speeds me up, because I know there's a timer. You can also spend just 10 minutes decluttering&nbsp;&nbsp; something, it might be a drawer or a&nbsp; cupboard as I say, and it's really effective,&nbsp;&nbsp; and just decluttering in general can just&nbsp; help to clear your mind. If you're feeling&nbsp;&nbsp; like there's just so much stuff in your house,&nbsp; spend some time decluttering, and you'll feel&nbsp;&nbsp; so much better for it. I actually created a&nbsp; 28-day challenge for February, and each day,&nbsp;&nbsp; you focus on decluttering something different,&nbsp; so if you want to check out that challenge,&nbsp;&nbsp; I will also link that in the description. And it's&nbsp; also worth, whenever you're doing a cleaning task&nbsp;&nbsp; or a decluttering task, something like that, to&nbsp; set yourself a little reward at the end. So if&nbsp;&nbsp; you're like, "I'm going to clean this room, but&nbsp; once I've done it, I'm going to have a cup of tea&nbsp;&nbsp; and let myself go on Instagram for 15 minutes,"&nbsp; those little rewards really keep me motivated.&nbsp; Next up, I wanted to talk about quiet time and&nbsp; how I used quiet time to help me stay organized.&nbsp;&nbsp; So when my children were babies, they would&nbsp; have a one or two-hour nap in the afternoon,&nbsp;&nbsp; but as soon as they turned three years old,&nbsp; they would stop napping, and then it was very&nbsp;&nbsp; difficult, because I felt like the things&nbsp; that I got done during their nap time was&nbsp;&nbsp; just impossible to get done. I used to get dinner&nbsp; ready, tidy up, answer emails, and all of that,&nbsp;&nbsp; and then all of a sudden, I just couldn't do it. So I started to let them have quiet time around&nbsp;&nbsp; the time when it would actually be nap time, so I&nbsp; would either let them play in their room quietly,&nbsp;&nbsp; have a bit of screen time, or I'd put on&nbsp; their favorite show, like Umizoomi, say,&nbsp;&nbsp; and they could just chill out, because they were&nbsp; probably a bit tired anyway, but it also meant&nbsp;&nbsp; then that I could get a few things done, and&nbsp; they just got used to it. I'd be like, "Okay,&nbsp;&nbsp; we're going to have a bit of quiet time now,"&nbsp; and then they would just chill out and they'd&nbsp;&nbsp; be able to get stuff done, and obviously you don't&nbsp; want your kids to be on the screens all the time,&nbsp;&nbsp; but I remember as kids, we used to sit in front&nbsp; of the TV and watch a bit of TV and stuff,&nbsp;&nbsp; so don't feel guilty if you absolutely need to&nbsp; get some stuff done, you can have quiet time.&nbsp; And my next tip is toy rotation. So rather&nbsp; than just have all of your kids' toys out&nbsp;&nbsp; all of the time, you can hide some&nbsp; away in the garage or in the attic,&nbsp;&nbsp; and then rotate your toys, and every now and&nbsp; then, bring some out of the attic or garage,&nbsp;&nbsp; and then put some more away. And you're probably&nbsp; thinking, "How is toy rotation going to help me&nbsp;&nbsp; be more organized?" But when you bring the new&nbsp; toys out, it's more interesting for the kids, and&nbsp;&nbsp; it keeps them occupied for a little bit longer. So I found when I got one of their car garages out&nbsp;&nbsp; of the attic or whatever, it was instantly like&nbsp; they had a new toy and it was fun to play with,&nbsp;&nbsp; whereas if they just had all their toys all the&nbsp; time, they would just get bored of it, and in&nbsp;&nbsp; a weird way, not play with anything, so it might&nbsp; be something to try if you're finding they're not&nbsp;&nbsp; as interested in the toys all the time. And the last tip I wanted to share with you&nbsp;&nbsp; is probably the most important one, and that&nbsp; is to make time for self-care to help minimize&nbsp;&nbsp; stress. Having a chaotic family life is actually&nbsp; quite normal, and having a messy house can also be&nbsp;&nbsp; the sign of a very happy, lived-in house,&nbsp; but the biggest issue is the stress that&nbsp;&nbsp; we feel and the pressure that we're under,&nbsp; so if you can make half an hour or an hour&nbsp;&nbsp; to have some self-care, maybe it's have a bath,&nbsp; do a hobby you love, or do some exercise, or&nbsp;&nbsp; go out for a walk, it can make a huge amount of&nbsp; difference. If I ever go for like a 30-minute run,&nbsp;&nbsp; I just come back feeling ready to take on family&nbsp; life and everything that goes with it again,&nbsp;&nbsp; it just gives me a little bit of head space and&nbsp; then I can enjoy quality time with the kids again.&nbsp; Anyway, those are all the tips that I wanted to&nbsp; share with you guys, I hope you really liked them.&nbsp;&nbsp; Let me know in the comments below which one&nbsp; was your favorite and what you're going to try,&nbsp;&nbsp; and thank you so much, I'll see&nbsp; you in my next one. Bye, guys.

- Having it all - for working mothers everywhere | Julie Ellison | TEDxDerryLondonderryWomen: Kind: captions Language: en i recently saw a clip of the late great ruth bader ginsburg talking about high when she worked in columbia law school during the 1970s and her son was 10 years old her son's skull would ring at least once a month to ask her to come in to talk about his lively behavior now she went every month when she was asked but after a while she grew weary and the next time the phone rang rang she said look my son has two parents i suggest you start alternating your calls now the school did start alternating the calls but the interesting thing about it was the calls reduced from more than once a month to less than once a semester and ruth bader ginsburg's explanation for that was that the school was much more reluctant to disturb a man at his work than they were to disturb a woman now when i first saw that clip i shared it online immediately with the caption for working mothers everywhere because i thought it captured something of what society still expects from working mothers but when i sat back and thought about it a little bit more i wondered whether the problem is really what society expects from working mothers or whether now 50 years on the issue is more about what working mothers expect from themselves you see now that i am a working mother i've realised that i've tried to carry out my job as though i don't have children and i've tried to parent my children as though i don't have a job and that's presented challenges that i just wasn't prepared for i have three children i have an amazing daughter who's four and then twin girls who are just fabulous and they've just turned two and i've been a barrister now for nine years a career i love but just to rewind a little bit i initially trained and worked as a pharmacist and throughout my twenties i saw many successful women who were juggling careers with family lives apparently without any difficulty and as a woman in my twenties i really didn't give much thought to what that really was like because i had no intention of having children just yet i returned to university when i was 28 and a couple of years after that it came to choosing which career path and law i would take and my father who is also a lawyer and who had instilled in me from a very early age that i could be anything i wanted to be and i mean i think he probably told that to me every day he sat me down and told me to consider carefully whether i really wanted to be a barrister because i would be self-employed with all the financial uncertainty that would bring and if i was successful in years to come it would be very difficult to juggle my career with a family life now clearly perhaps because i'm something of an optimist or perhaps because my dad instilled in me too well the idea that i could be anything i wanted to be i carried on into a career at the bar regardless and five years later in 2016 i had my first daughter and two years after that i had my twins now those first few months are hard the sleepless nights the dirty nappies the constant worry about whether or not you're getting it right but i find that since returning to work and as my children are growing older i'm now facing a new struggle which is that i don't really know what type of mother i'm going to be you see for people like me who are ambitious and driven we want to have it all and women who have come a very long way in terms of equality in the workplace and when their children come along they don't necessarily want to step back to focus on their families but nor do they want to step back from family life to such an extent that they feel like they're not really properly parenting at all a few weeks ago one of my twin daughters hurt her foot and needed to go to the hospital for an x-ray my husband asked whether i wanted him to take her or whether i wanted to go now immediately without hesitation i said i wanted to go because i suspect like many mothers i wanted to be there to hear every minute detail of what the doctor was going to say just like in years to come i want to be able to go to school plays and sports days and if the school rings to discuss one of my child's behavior i want to be the first one to hear it so while now it's largely a matter of choice who the school rings first i still want it to be me my husband is very supportive as cheryl sandberg ceo of facebook and author of lean in recommended he is an equal partner but that doesn't mean i like it when if one of my children fall over and hurt themselves they call for him first and the other day when i was helping my husband out to the car with the children i had a real pang of jealousy when i didn't know that one of my two-year-olds likes to press the doorbell on the way out and while child care is a necessary reality for many working parents i still remember the first time i smelt a stranger's perfume on my baby's head and the feeling of fear that i had that after spending eight or nine hours in somebody else's arms my baby was going to start loving me a little bit less and start to love them instead you see those feelings i wasn't prepared for i knew that being a working mother was going to be difficult looking after tiny little people making sure they're well cared for on top of what is a stressful job was never going to be easy but i didn't realize quite high emotionally tough and at times confusing it could be and although we talk jokingly about mummy guilt i think there's room for a much deeper discussion about the internal conflict that many working mothers and parents face to allow us to really work out how we're going to enjoy both work and home in 2019 a bristol and essex university study found only 27.8 percent of women were in full-time work or self-employed three years after childbirth that's compared to 90 percent for new fathers and now while i know that there is a host of complex reasons behind that sort of statistic i wonder if for women in particular who want to carry on in their job or need to that they're feeling like the compromises they're making in one way or the other are simply too great we need to recognize that you can't lean in fully both at work and at home all of the time inevitably i think that leads to people taking on too much and women in particular placing unrealistic expectations on themselves and ultimately i think that leads to problems with mental health and takes a toll on relationships so what are we going to do about it well i read recently that when you're trying to spin all the plates whether they be work plates or home plates you need to work out which ones are china and which ones are plastic you need to work out what your priorities are and then start letting those plastic plates drop or better yet don't even start spinning them in the first place so in terms of my work that might mean that for the next few years i don't accept every invitation to a work dinner or every opportunity to broaden my cv and on the home front i'm going to have to get comfortable with the idea that i'm not going to be there every day when my children come home from school and i might have to miss the occasional sports day or school play and while those things are going to be hard i think perhaps even most more importantly i need to practice letting go of the little things that every day make the mental burden that little bit more hard to bear so who cares if my children go out without their hair brushed if they go out without their um matching outfits on and i'm definitely never going to be one of those mothers that packs school lunches that are organic and muddled into little penguins and if i do forget to do something or if i can't be there for my children i'm going to be honest and open with them about why that is and it's because i'm trying to be a good working mother and set an example for them and i am only human and it's okay for them to really know that my mother worked full-time whenever i was a child she was a college lecturer she also looked after my elderly grandmother and she was one of the first amongst the first generation of women who had the opportunity to do that and she made it work when i look back in my childhood i don't remember all the times that she wasn't there i remember the times that she was and what i really learned is that you can be a great mother with an incredible bond with your children and work at the same time but if i'm going to put that into practice and be happy i'm going to have to realize that there will be compromises and there will be sacrifices but i'm going to have to stop judging myself if things aren't perfect all of the time working parents simply can't be everything to everyone and we need to start having a real open vulnerable conversation inside and outside of the workplace about how we're going to make it work you see i think that the only way that we can truly have it all is when we stop trying to do it all so well i don't know exactly what type of mother i'm going to be by letting some plastic plates drop i hope that i can show my precious daughters the fulfillment that comes from having a career i love while raising them there is a short window of 15 or so years when our children are most dependent on us those years will be hard work but if we work hard to let go of some of the expectations we place on ourselves my hope is working parents everywhere can embrace and enjoy them

- How To Manage Wedding Seating Arrangements For Divorced Parents? - Wedding Trends Now: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] How to manage wedding seating arrangements for divorced parents? Managing wedding seating arrangements for divorced parents can be a delicate task. It is important to create a comfortable atmosphere for everyone involved. Let's break down some effective strategies to tackle this situation. At the wedding ceremony, it is generally best to see both divorced parents in the front row. This is their child's special day, and they should be honored. To help ease any potential tension, consider using family members, such as siblings or grandparents, as buffers between parents who may not get along. This simple step can help reduce discomfort. Using reserved seating cards with individual names can also clarify the seating order. This pre-arrangement helps minimize confusion and tension on the big day. When it comes to the reception, the seating arrangements depend largely on the relationship between the divorced parents. If they have a friendly relationship, seating them at the same table with siblings and other family members can work well. This arrangement can promote a positive atmosphere. However, if the parents do not get along, it is better to seat them at separate tables with some distance between them. This separation helps avoid awkwardness and allows each parent to enjoy the celebration. Placing each parent with their close friends or family members can also help them feel more comfortable. Having a conversation with the parents before the wedding about seating arrangements can be beneficial. This discussion allows you to address any concerns they may have about fairness or sensitivity. Open communication can help ease worries and ensure everyone feels respected. When planning the top table seating, traditional arrangements may not suit divorced or remarried parents. Consider alternative seating plans that accommodate everyone. For instance, positioning the maid of honor or best man as a buffer between divorced parents can help maintain harmony during the reception. In summary, here are some key points to keep in mind. See both parents in the front row at the ceremony. Use family members as buffers if necessary and utilize reserved seating cards for clarity. At the reception, seat parents together if they get along, otherwise separate them with distance. Place each parent near their close friends or family to ensure comfort. Lastly, communicate openly with parents about seating to minimize misunderstandings. These strategies can help create a smooth and enjoyable wedding day experience for everyone involved.

- Mental Health Minute: Divorce & Children: Kind: captions Language: en when a marriage ends it may be tempting to wish a former spouse would simply disappear but that may be exactly what children fear the most a new study from arizona state university says when children are exposed to conflict between divorced parents many children fear that they will be abandoned by one or both parents children who are exposed to fighting between parents who are married or cohabitating worry about their parents separating experts say that while abandonment is unlikely it seems logical to children the study asked 559 kids between the ages of 9 and 18 about their exposure to conflict researchers questioned children about whether their divorced parents fought in front of them spoke poorly of the other parent or asked children to carry messages children reported worrying about abandonment and experiencing mental health issues even 10 months after observing interpersonal conflict communication between some divorced parents works best when conversations are limited to talking about the children and nothing else kids should not be asked to carry messages between households nor should one parent ask the kids about who the other parent may be dating also do not treat a child like a best friend divorce can fracture people emotionally get some help before your boundaries start to disappear i'm bill prasad your mental health and wellness expert you

- Understanding the Impact of Divorce on Children: Emotional, Psychological, and Social Consequences: Kind: captions Language: en understanding the impact of divorce on children emotional psychological and social consequences divorce is not an easy process to go through as a family however sometimes divorce becomes a necessity for the couple as they may have reached a Crossroads in their marriage and it might be better for both of them to separate adults going through a divorce process have to adapt to new changes which include taking numerous decisions relating to financial and emotional aspects this process is even more difficult for children especially when they are very young at a tender age children are not able to understand the new changes that arise from the fundamental fact that they will not be able to live with both parents as one family therefore children must adapt to the immediate change of living with one parent alone and seeing the other parent only occasionally during the designated visit time it becomes even more difficult when a parent decides to emigrate and thus permanently moves out of the country of residents of the children which leads to even fewer personal visits emotionally all these changes take a toll on the child's psyche and parents must strive to make the transition as cordial and friendly as possible studies show that a divorce process can have the following effects on children depending on their age for toddlers And preschoolers because they are so young children do not understand the need to be permanently separated from one parent the concept of two houses can fill children with worry and fear they feel anxious when they are not able to be with both their parents and quite often feel that they are not loved by the absent parent for children of primary school age a divorce process can cause them Stress and Anxiety and often makes them feel guilty about the changes it brings children of this age group often think of guilt and may feel that a divorce is due to their mistakes for shortcomings research shows that children are most affected by contentious divorces which can be reflected in increased behavioral problems poor academic performance and also in children's Health children need to adapt to the new changes and one of the best ways is to keep up with a regular visit schedule and provide them with easy access to both parents subsids and needs a regular schedule helps to bring a needs oriented structure into their lives and children might understand that both parents regularly prioritize their needs parental support and reassurance can avoid many of the likely challenges especially if parents can put their children's needs before anything else parents should consider seeking the expert help which can include medical and psychological support in helping their children solving anxiety problems through professional help has proven to be of great value to Children an - What identical twins separated at birth teach us about genetics - BBC REEL: Kind: captions Language: en-GB My name is Dr Nancy Segal. I'm a psychology professor at California State University, Fullerton. What got me into twin research initially is that I am a fraternal twin and always fascinated with the similarities and differences, mostly differences that my sister and I have. And I used to wonder as a child how this could be if we had the same parents and same environments. And when I began to study psychology at the high school and graduate school level, I learned about genetics and how we all came into the world with predispositions of our own. And that explained why my sister and I were so very, very different. Well, I spent many years at the Minnesota study with twins raised apart, looking at identical and fraternal twins who've been separated at birth. It's very important to include the fraternals because they're the natural control group. And let's think about the differences between those twins before looking at the insights. Identical twins share all their genes having split from a single fertilised egg within the first two weeks after conception. Fraternal twins share half the genes on average. They result when a woman releases two eggs at the same time and they're fertilised by two separate sperm, so they have 50 percent of their genes in common on average, like ordinary siblings. Comparing resemblance in identical twins to fraternal twins gives us a handle on whether or not genetics has an effect. It would if identical twins are more alike than fraternal and they invariably are. Studying twins gives us enormous insights into how we come to be the way that we are. Twin studies are a natural model for looking at genetic and environmental influences on behaviour, and what we are finding is that many more behaviours than we ever would have thought, do have a genetic component to them. Genetics is not everything, but does explain a great deal of why we differ one person to another. Well, let's start with looking at genetic influences on physical traits. We find that height and weight have substantial genetic components. We find that general intelligence has a substantial genetic component, a little bit less than the sum of the physical traits like height and weight and brainwaves. But nevertheless, as does special mental abilities. And then we drop down a bit when we get to job satisfaction. Probably the most surprising findings in the last 20 years or so had been that things like religiosity, how much you invest in religious activities and interests and political attitudes and social attitudes It's very important to appreciate the genes do not work in deterministic ways. They work in probabilistic ways they predispose, but they do not provide the final word. Genes change in expression just because you have a gene doesn't mean it will always be expressed. It takes a certain environment to bring that out. We all have genes that will be expressed given a particular environment. Now, with identical twins, they have the same DNA, but sometimes gene expression can occur in one twin and not the other. And this can create differences between them, and these environmental differences that trigger different gene expression might even start in the womb. The beauty of identical twins raised apart is that they share only their genes and not their environments. So any resemblance between them is tied to their common genes. We find some amazing similarities in identical twins raised apart. Many more than we ever would have anticipated, not just in the more traditional areas like intelligence, personality, physical features like height and weight, but in some more unusual habits, such as a pair of twins who both used to scatter love letters around the house to their wives and both bit their nails down to the nub and both explain their same mixed headache syndrome in exactly the same way as if someone is beating on their head with a hammer. These are very challenging, and you can ask yourself, are they due to just random chance? My answer to that is no, they're not. And the rarer they are, the more I believe it's somehow tied to their genes and the way the genes interact with their environments to produce these kinds of unusual similarities. I think that the people who find it challenging or even disturbing are those who don't fully understand the process. They think the genes work in deterministic ways when in fact they work in probabilistic ways. And so it doesn't mean that we're set in stone, we can't change. It means that we can change. We can alter environments to make behavioural expression different. We can work to prevent disease or to mitigate it, but we can't all be the same.

they were gonna go to divorce and that back and forth it's probably one of the biggest things that affected me I feel like I'm realizing stuff I'd like this is very therapeutic for me right now I'm gonna be honest I really feel like I'm just sitting and talking to my friend about this so the first trimester of my senior year it was pretty much the same thing as my junior year one minute they were going to counseling the next day running divorced and finally at the end of the year closer to Christmas time they decided that they were going to get separated and they were not trying anymore you can either choose to sit there and just be upset about it and cry about it and be sad about it or you can choose to accept it embrace it don't pretend like it didn't happen or that it's not happening know that it's there but be okay with it and whichever one of those options you choose it's still gonna happen your parents are still gonna have these problems they will still get divorced whether you are upset about it or you accept it and you don't let it take over your life and that's something that is really really difficult to do it's so much easier said than done I just remember great relationship with my mom but with any of this being open and tell them how you feel talk to a therapist talk to a friend talk to a family member one of my biggest tips that if possible I really the exact same thing as you are and be there for each other and support each other and also do not compare your situation to anyone else's situation be grateful for what you do have but also out why are you so angry um and then you have to figure out how to redefine your relationship you're no longer married to each other you're co-parents so trying to figure out how can go from spouses to co-parents um effectively and the last thing that I would recommend is trying to take away some of the emotion in conflict in general people tend to say the worst things things that they regret when they're highly emotional highly angry highly sad so try and diffuse some of the emotions something very simple for example is to email the parent instead of talking to them face to face so much or via the phone email has its own issues but it does tend to diffuse emotion and the last and final point that I wanted to make to parents the most important thing you can do is to listen to your child's voice they may not say anything but try and put yourself in your child's shoes and think of the long-term impact that this would have and listen to their inner voice thank [Applause] you

  19. @MrElarain: Something she touches on that kind of undermines a big part of her message is that subsequent studies usually show: Yes, children in high conflict marriages do better after divorce if it leads to a low conflict divorce*. Children are actually still worse off moving from a high conflict marriage to a high conflict divorce. (Note, *NOT abusive. Just conflict.)

So if your justification for splitting is that the kids will be better off, really ask yourself if divorce is going to resolve the conflicts with your co-parent. That doesn't automatically mean divorce is the wrong the choice, but it does mean it's not in the children's best interest necessarily.

Let me tell you, as long as there are no major issues and the man is still willing to work and support the family, never divorce easily or you will surely regret it. Why? Because the reasons women want to divorce often stems from seeing everything wrong with her husbands, complaining that he doesn't earn enough, isn't romantic, and feels like life is just revolving around household chores with no hope. Sometimes it's because of an unreliable friend who stirs things up, or because they envy others lives and think that after a divorce, they can find someone better. But in reality, after a divorce, women only have two choices. Remain single or remarry. If they stay single with children, they bear the full responsibility of making money and raising the children. And if they get sick, there's no one to take care of them. That's when they realize how tough it is to live alone. If they choose to remarry, life gets even harder. I can't let go of the child, so I have to endure this. Why are so many women initiating divorces? Experts point out that under the current economic downturn in China, husbands are struggling to support their family on their own, and women have no choice but to bear the dual economic burden of working and family care. This dual burden on women is especially prominent during periods of social transition leading to frequent conflicts in marriages. Although economic independence enables women to initiate divorces, the decline in quality of life due to high stress also leads many women to regret their decision and wish to remarry. A lawyer explains, "Why are most divorces now initiated by women? Many men believe that it's only natural for women to take on housework after marriage and childirth. But times have changed. Women are now more independent, both mentally and financially. >> In the past decade, the rates of women buying homes and cars have increased by 20%, surpassing that of men. The number of women participating in the workforce is now only 2% behind men. And in the field of graduate education, women even outnumber men by 1.6%. Therefore, the traditional family structure of the home has changed. The vlogger says, >> "Think about it. Women could earn money and live well on their own. So why would they continue a marriage with a man who neither appreciates nor understands them? >> And the latest data released by the Ministry of Civil Affairs supports this view. According to the National Divorce to Marriage Ratio for 2025, the top three provinces are the three northeastern provinces, Halong Jang, Jilin, and Leoing. In Halong Jang, the divorce to marriage ratio reached 63%. Meaning that for every 100 couples who marry, 63 simultaneously divorce. This statistic may seem absurd, but it reflects the consequences of decades of industrial decline, population loss, and hopelessness. In the late 1990s, China experienced a massive wave of layoffs, and the Northeast was hit the hardest. According to data from China's statistical yearbook, between 1998 and 2002, over 40 million people were laid off from stateowned enterprises, with nearly 40% concentrated in the three northeastern provinces. Factories closed, employees were laid off, and many families were left with nothing but cabbage and potatoes on the table. Financial struggles intensified family tensions. Many young people chose to move to the south for work. According to the National Bureau of Statistics, by the end of 2024, Halong Jang's permanent population had decreased by 407,000 compared to the previous year, marking the fourth consecutive year of a net outflow of over 300,000 people. Being separated long-term for work leads to instability in the family. What's more crucial is that women in the Northeast have always been relatively independent, strong, and capable of earning money. According to data from the National Health Commission in 2024, women in the Northeast have an average of 10.4 years of education, above the national average. And the urban employment rate is 68% much higher than in the Southwest. This means that northeastern women are financially stable and confident. They don't rely on men for support and don't need marriage to survive, so they're naturally not afraid of divorce. The second reason is that a lot of toxic positivity has distorted the views of many women hold about marriage and family. A vlogger explains, "In our country, the divorce rate has been increasing for the 15th consecutive year. Behind the rise, the so-called motivational quotes have played a toxic role. For instance, saying like, "A home is a place for love, not reason. If you love me, don't talk about logic." Men are rational. If you don't want him to reason with you, what else can he do? Regardless of whether a man spends money on you, you should have the ability to earn your own. Don't just keep asking. Don't underestimate the influence of these toxic sayings. Now, I find that more toxic sayings are emerging. I saw one that made me laugh. It goes, "Why do women propose divorce now? If she's doing well, will she ask for a divorce? Since women are more likely to propose divorce, it must mean they're unhappy. That the men aren't treating them well. So, what's wrong with her asking for a divorce? This kind of illogical reasoning, I don't know where it comes from." >> The vlogger analyzes, "A big reason why women are asking for divorces now is because of infidelity. In traditional Chinese culture, marriage is based on mutual respect and equality, emphasizing human harmony and responsibility. However, after the Chinese Communist Party took over, it initiated multiple social movements, especially the cultural revolution, which distorted people's ideological views. Traditional family ethics and a sense of responsibility were impacted leading to a gradual weakening of marriage concepts. The five provinces with the lowest divorce rates are Tibet, Guo, Hanan, Gansu, and Shinghai. These areas are mostly inland provinces with a strong traditional culture, low population mobility, and conservative views on marriage. Tibet, in particular, has the lowest divorce rate in the country at just one out of 1,000. Why is Tibet's divorce rate the lowest in the country? The main reason is that the concept of marriage in Tibetan culture is heavily influenced by family and religion. Both marriage and divorce require collective family decisions which reduces impulsive divorces. Experts analyze that Tibet's low divorce rate is due to the local social culture and the emphasis placed on traditional family values and religious beliefs. Tibetan Buddhism has a traditional view of marriage emphasizing stability and longevity. Divorce is seen as shameful in Tibetan society and is subject to societal pressure. Additionally, Tibet social culture is relatively conservative and the importance of family values is high. The traditional family structure and ethical beliefs are deeply rooted in Tibetan society. Furthermore, the simple lifestyle and pure interpersonal relationships in Tibetan communities makes it easier for couples to understand and support each other in daily life. Professor Lu Xiang, a law professor at the China University of Political Science and Law, offers deep insights into marital relationships. >> China's divorce rate is now one of the highest in the world. The main group initiating divorces is young people aged 22 to 35, as they have a much stronger sense of autonomy than their predecessors. They fall in love at first sight and get married, then impulsively divorce when angry, letting their emotions run wild. This kind of love is often a form of narcissism, and narcissistic love never lasts. No one can meet all of your expectations. A lovable person will be loved by everyone. But when they are no longer as lovable as before, you still love them and are willing to find their lovable traits. This might be real love. And to achieve this, one must have a tolerant heart. When you make a mistake, you hope others will forgive you. So, you should also accept the imperfections in others and learn to tolerate them.

sent shockwaves through the royal family and the World At Large but while the public may have fixated on the drama and Scandal of the split what truly captivates is the story of how Diana's life underwent a dramatic transformation in the wake of the divorce from her Newfound Independence to her philanthropic Endeavors Diana's divorce paved the way for a new chapter in her life keep watching to discover 15 reasons why Diana's life changed after her divorce number one loss of Royal status Diana Princess of Wales was one of the most beloved members of the British royal family however after her divorce from Charles she lost her royal title and was no longer addressed as her Royal Highness this change in status had a significant impact on her life losing her royal title meant that Diana no longer had access to certain Privileges and benefits that came with being a member of the royal family she was no longer able to 10 State functions on behalf of the queen or receive diplomatic immunity when abroad this also meant that her security detail was reduced leaving her more vulnerable to Paparazzi and other forms of harassment despite these challenges she continued to work for various causes and inspired millions of people around the world ultimately Diana's loss of Royal status after her divorce was a turning point in her life it forced her to find A New Path and use her influence for good even as she faced new challenges and scrutiny number two increased media attention despite losing her royal status after her divorce from King Charles Princess Diana remained a public figure and was closely monitored by the media this increased media attention affected many aspects of her life including her relationships her charitable work and her personal safety the media's interest in Diana was partly due to her status as a former princess but it was also driven by her personal life her relationships including her brief romance with Dodie Fayed were widely reported in the Press despite the overwhelming attention Diana remained gracious and composed always maintaining her composure in public until her untimely death in 1997 her ability to handle the media with poise and Grace earned her widespread admiration from people all over the world number three Financial Independence becoming financially independent was a significant change for Diana after her divorce Princess Diana had to become financially independent while she received a lump sum settlement and an annual income she had to rely on her own earnings for the rest this major change in her life meant that she had to start working and managing her own finances through various philanthropic Endeavors and public speaking engagements she became more focused on her professional Endeavors Diana's Newfound Financial Independence also gave her the freedom to make choices about her life she was able to travel more and spend time with her children without the restrictions that came with her royal duties she also became more involved in fashion and style which helped to establish her as a style icon number four increased freedom Diana's life took a turn for the better after her divorce no longer bound by the responsibilities and obligations of her marriage she was able to explore her interests and passions in ways that had previously been Out Of Reach before the divorce Diana's life revolved around her family and her husband's interests leaving her with little time or opportunity to explore her own passions however with her Newfound Independence Diana was able to pursue her interests and engage in activities that brought her joy and fulfillment exploring new hobbies including painting and photography and even taking up yoga writing and Hiking classes this newfound Freedom allowed her to discover new passions and ReDiscover herself and allowed her to build deeper relationships with friends and family spending more time with the people she cared about and building a support system of friends who shared her interests Diana's divorce was undoubtedly a difficult experience but It ultimately led to positive change in her life allowing her to reevaluate her life and prioritize her happiness which allowed her to live her life on her terms and allowed her to make decisions about her future without the input of her ex-husband number five change in parenting responsibilities after her divorce Diana found herself thrust into a new role as the primary caregiver for her two sons Diana's role as a parent shifted from being a partner and co-parent to a sole caregiver responsible for her children's daily needs Taking on more responsibilities including managing her children's daily schedules and ensuring their emotional and physical well-being this shift in parenting responsibilities had a significant impact on her life both emotionally and practically it also meant she had to learn new skills and ways of parenting and became more responsible for making decisions about her children's upbringing and well-being despite the challenges Diana found strength and resilience in her new role as a single mother she learned to rely on her own instincts and abilities and she found joy in the special moments she shared with her children number six re-evaluation of her life Diana's divorce may have felt like the end of the world to her but in version of herself instead of Simply surviving she chose to thrive using her experience as a springboard to Lodge herself into a life filled with purpose and passion as she began to shed the layers of important causes one of Diana's most notable accomplishments was her advocacy for the eradication of landmines after visiting Angola in 1997 she Drew International attention to the devastation caused by landmines and called for their worldwide ban her efforts led to the signing of the Ottawa treaty which saw a significant reduction in landmine usage and casualties Diana also worked tirelessly to raise awareness of HIV AIDS and the stigma surrounding the Disease by shaking hands with AIDS patients she helped to break down the prejudices and misconceptions surrounding the illness and was a powerful advocate for those affected by it her humanitarian work was complemented by her Devotion to her two sons Prince William and Prince Harry who she instilled with empathy and compassion traits they've carried forward her Legacy lives on through her sons who continue to support the causes she championed William and Harry have both spoken publicly about mental health and their own struggles with grief and Trauma the Diana award which recognizes young people who make positive impact in their communities was also established in her honor Diana's fashion choices which were daring and After repeatedly giving UPSC exams, I could n't clear them. In the morning, I was taunted somewhere or the other, saying, "The person who ca n't wake up in the morning, what will he achieve in life?" My family found it a very difficult step. They told me, "Remember, when a girl leaves home after marriage, going to her father's house and her husband's house both becomes very difficult. Did he ever hit you? Did he ever touch you? Maybe it wasn't all that. Despite that, I took this decision." It is said that those who walk off the beaten track are rebellious. The reason for that was that if I studied law, I would get a DU hostel for nine years and could stay in the hostel and prepare for coaching. So, from the elite girls college of South Campus, I went to North Campus of Delhi University and that just changed my life. As soon as I went to my first year in Pasona, I felt the college fever. Because I was in an all-girls college that year, I suddenly found a wonderful environment there. As soon as I went there, I decided why not contest the elections. I contested the elections in a hurry and I won. I was the Joint Secretary at Delhi University. He was from my own family and background, belonged to my own state. There was a slight age gap, but I felt that this would be a good match for me. But I was determined that if I had fallen in love, then I would definitely get married. You got married, but after marriage, your system suddenly failed. I had a grid collapse phase. Everyone says that divorce is easy. I feel that no one gets married just to get a divorce. No one gets happy when they get a divorce. Divorce is still a big taboo in our country, and especially for girls like us who have come up from very small towns. Even today, we belong to a rural background. Even today, when I go on holidays, I go to my village and there, even today, the old son-in-law is remembered by name, and my family members cry or wonder what happened to their daughter. Now let's talk about what was the reason for the breakdown of the marriage. Somewhere, I feel like everyone used to hurt me a lot when someone said, "Hey, this person is very career- oriented." Earlier, it used to sound like an insult to me, but now I understand that there is nothing wrong in being career-oriented. You can also be a good wife while keeping your career in mind. You can earn money and you can also run the family. So the reason why things didn't work out with my ex-husband was that I wanted to pursue further studies and there was some kind of manipulation between us that I wasn't getting much support from him. The friendship may or may not remain but at least there will be no regrets in the heart. But destiny had something else in store for us and everyone's level of understanding is different. My marriage wasn't working out and my exams weren't coming up. I decided to apply for my Masters. When I decided to do this, there was very little time left. There was only one university, the University of Cambridge, where I had a form left. All the scholarship forms had been sold. How did I manage to save Rs. 5000, which seemed like a huge amount to me at that time? I had started practicing law slowly and moderately, but it was n't like I had a very flourishing practice. So, how did I save money and fill my entrance form? It took a month and a half. When my result came in January 2019, I was looking around. Those who had got admission in Cambridge had written big posts on the language. They had written big stories. Their families were very happy. The biggest question on me was how would I get so much money? How would I tell these people that I wanted to go? A time came when I disclosed this to my ex-husband. I told him that I did not get any congratulations but I definitely got the message that don't expect me to give you money. Sometimes I felt bad about these things. I made up my mind that no matter what happens, I will definitely do it. This was my rebel that while being in that marriage, I decided that I will take this decision for my future, for my well-being or to make a good career. My family found this a very difficult step. They told me to remember that after marriage, when a girl leaves home, it becomes very difficult to go to her father's house and her husband's house. But I had understood this much that if a girl leaves home financially, I had black and white clothes and some of my files and my favorite books and yes, fridge magnets because I was very fond of travelling. I left all these things and moved out of that house. A year before going to Cambridge, I took a PG. I slept on a mattress on the floor. I did not use any AC because I wanted to come out of that comfort zone. I wanted to remind myself that I should take each and every step. I had to save money because I didn't have any scholarship or any backup, so I earned money and saved it. With the support of my parents, my siblings, and my extended family, I was able to complete my course. I was at a wedding at the time. We often talked about finishing it, but it was very difficult at that time. These emotional concussions and that one over-the-top valminik. But when I went there, my world changed. Did I get a taste of the outside world? As many people say, the answer is no, the atmosphere there changed. Where people used to sit in the library and study all the time, I practically applied my three-four years of working experience there. I networked a lot and worked hard. By the end of 2019, as soon as our next year, 2020, started, Covid arrived. The hardships of Covid were such that the money I used to get from my Indian retainers stopped paying me. I have two learnings and two experiences from there that were very useful to me. I learned that no work is big or small. I started practicing from the Supreme Court and there came a time when I had to pick up people's plates. I worked in an Indian restaurant, I am a vegetarian, people used to eat bones there and I used to pick up their plates. It was not that I felt bad, but I I started making cocktails there as a bartender, I taught yoga to the people there, I kept myself busy with work in this way, I also studied like this. After taking a break of one year, I thought that I would go back and practice law with full concentration. However, when I came to India, everything had changed. Everyone living there had come to India due to Covid, but I spent a few more months there and when I came here, I thought it would be wise that since I had some loans, I should close my old marriage. I had also mortgaged my jewellery worth Rs. 1000. My mother was very sad that this is a woman's jewellery, son, this should not be taken out, but I said, mother, I am so capable, so strong that I can get not just one but 10 sets of jewellery made for myself, but today I need it, so because of those jewellery, that loan and such small supports, I got a Masters in International Law degree. It was a wonderful experience and Cambridge has taught me that you should always live with humility and humility. No matter where you started from or what your end is, you should hold on to your roots. This was my learning there. After coming here, I joined a very prestigious private university as an Assistant Professor of Law. I think it was a very wise and big decision. When it comes to the last phase, which I think was the most decisive phase of my life, that decisive phase was a well-paying job, a very good one. I used to get paid in lakhs there. I decided that I was feeling a little uneasy here. And I am not able to do justice, despite teaching children, despite performing well here, despite having good appraisals, I don't think my nature is such that I will be able to survive here. I used to live in Sonipat. With great courage and a lot of effort, I collected money and built my own office for the first time. I found my own office. At the age of 30, I started my own office, which is named Pranatelli. Ours was a lifeless marriage, that even today we see each other every day in the Supreme Court. We will keep seeing each other, so we parted ways in a very civilized manner. The time comes or the phase comes when we leave everything and go. I thought that after practicing in the Supreme Court and having a Cambridge degree in between and taking a break, when I go back, the profession will welcome me with open arms. When I went back, I understood that it is not as easy as it seemed. Every second, third, fourth day, I would hear subtle undertones in indirect and direct ways, sometimes with Simpati, sometimes with sarcasm. This is his former wife, this is a divorce, oh what has happened to the poor girl, some people really came near her to console her, they talked to her, ladies, gossips of the bar room, gossips of the corridors, and a point came when I strengthened my ears by listening to them. The day the divorce papers were being signed, that day too, I asked myself this question for the last time with my eyes closed and asked myself, can you do this? Did he ever hit you, did he ever abuse you? Maybe it was nothing, despite that I took this decision because there are some problems inside you which you cannot tell whether you want to or not. Marriage is necessary, but divorce shows you a new world. God forbid this should happen to you, but if divorce ever happens to you, then take it on your stride and you should not be sad or worried about what people will say if they say I am divorced. I told my relatives in my village, my family, my distant relatives that such things This happened because I did not want that after a love marriage, fingers should be pointed at my parents or they should be held accountable for this thing. During all this, my family was very supportive. Where since childhood we were told not to make boyfriends, there came a phase in life where parents started accepting everything very naturally. After all this, a very good phase came in life. I got married again. I met a wonderful person. More so specifically, from my women, this is my request or this is my summary of all the good and bad experiences till date. - Budgeting Basics!: Kind: captions Language: en Do you ever feel stressed about money? Unless you’re a prince or a preschooler, you probably said yes. For a lot of people, it always feels like there’s not quite enough to go around. You’re constantly having to make tough decisions about what you can forego, and it never feels like you’re really in control of your finances. Well, when you need a bit of inspiration, look no further than the front of a one dollar bill. I’m talking about this guy: George Washington. Besides being the first president of the United States, General George Washington was the commander of the rag-tag Continental Army during the Revolutionary War. He must’ve felt like he never had enough soldiers to go around, but by making a plan, he was able to lead that army to victory over the greatest fighting force in the world. You can take a cue from George by becoming commander-in-chief of your finances. Each one of these is a soldier in your army, and your battle plan is a budget. I know, the word “budget” makes most people want to go AWOL. In fact, according to a 2016 Gallup poll, only 32% of couples keep a written budget of any kind. But if you were a soldier, would you want to follow a general who was making it up as he went along? A lot of people tend to think of budgeting as prediction--estimating what you’ll make in future months and how you’ll want to spend it. But the most effective budgets work exclusively with present dollars. After all, you can’t give orders to soldiers that don’t exist! So the size of your army is only how much money you currently have in your bank accounts. And as general, your role is to give every last one of those soldiers a job to do. So, if you have a total of $10,000 across all of your accounts, you need to decide how much of that you’re going to spend this month, and how much you’re going to set aside for savings. Before we can decide how much we want to spend, we need to know how much we’re spending now. Print out your last 3 months of bank statements, and put each expense into one of 5 categories: Essentials. These are the things you can’t do without. Rent, utilities, groceries, gas, car payments, health insurance. Anything you need to live or do your job effectively. Security. These are expenses that increase your financial stability, like building emergency funds or paying off debts. Don’t double count credit cards in this category though! If you use a card to buy groceries, that expense should go in “Essentials”. Goals. This is money you set aside to achieve big life goals, like saving up to buy a house, starting your own business, or taking a special vacation. Lifestyle. These are expenses that help you navigate your social world. Personal grooming and apparel, gifts for friends and family, gym memberships, even pet needs can go in here. Discretionary. This category is all about treating yourself. Going out to dinner, renting a movie, buying a video game, or getting a massage. Everyone has their own priorities, but think of this as a good battle plan template, with the most urgent needs at the top, and the more flexible ones towards the bottom. Now that you know how much you generally spend on these categories, you can start assigning your soldiers to next month’s duties. Unless you’re living paycheck to paycheck, you probably have some money left over--but don’t let those soldiers just sit around! Decide what you’re saving them for. Is it an emergency fund or a Hawaiian vacation? Savings that have explicit purposes are a lot harder to steal from. Congratulations! You’ve made your first month’s budget Now how do you make it stick? Number one. Write it down. You can use a spreadsheet, an app, or good old pencil and paper, but no general ever won a war by keeping all their strategy up here. Number two. Update it. Conditions on the ground will change, so restrategize at the beginning of every month--and you’ll have new soldiers to deploy in next month’s battles. Number three. Use advanced technology. Thanks to the dozens of budgeting apps you can download for your phone, no one has to keep a checkbook longhand anymore. This can be especially helpful for discretionary spending: now you can know right away whether you can afford that Weird Al album on vinyl. So how you do know you’re budgeting correctly? Well, the most obvious answer is that you’re less stressed. Imagine enjoying a dinner out without worrying about whether or not you can afford it. Imagine not holding your breath every time you check your account balance. You’d be amazed how much you can improve your mood just by introducing more clarity and control into your finances. Like a lot of good financial habits, the point of budgeting isn’t to have more money… it’s to be happier with the money you have. And that’s our two cents! We want to tell you about the new PBS Digital Studios series: Say It Loud. A celebration of Black History and culture and it's impact on how we live today. Hosts Evelyn From The Internets and Azie Dungey, give you a comedic take on identity and pop culture. From Black pride movements to Black Twitter shenanigans Check out "Say It Loud" in the link in the description below.
