Finance सबरेडिट के एक रेडिट पोस्ट से। यह एक प्लेबुक है कि आपको अपने पैसों के साथ क्या करना चाहिए, जिसे मैं अपनी कमेंट्री के साथ समझाऊंगा। तो चलिए सीधे स्टेप नंबर एक से शुरू करते हैं। एक हाई इनकम अर्नर के तौर पर, आपके पैसे सबसे पहले एक इमरजेंसी फंड बनाने में जाने चाहिए, जिसमें कम से कम तीन से छह महीने के खर्चे हों। रेडिट गाइड सलाह देती है कि आप अपने छह महीने के खर्चे एक हाई यील्ड सेविंग्स अकाउंट या SGOV जैसे ट्रेज़री ETF में रखें लिक्विडिटी और सेफ्टी के लिए। आगे यह भी कहा गया है कि अगर आपके पास पचास हज़ार डॉलर से ज़्यादा कैश का बड़ा बैलेंस है, तो आप Fidelity या Schwab जैसी ब्रोकरेज में एक CMA अकाउंट, यानी कैश मैनेजमेंट अकाउंट पर विचार कर सकते हैं। ये सारे ऑप्शन्स एक जैसे ही हैं। वे चाहते हैं कि आप अपना कैश मनी मार्केट फंड या किसी लिक्विड जगह पर रखें जहाँ आपको 2.5 से 5% का इंटरेस्ट मिले। छह महीने के इमरजेंसी फंड की सलाह काफी अच्छी है। तो, एक हाई अर्नर के तौर पर, अगर आप साल का $150k या उससे ज़्यादा कमा रहे हैं, तो आपका लाइफस्टाइल और खर्चे शायद ज़्यादा होंगे। आप शायद घर की मॉर्गेज, ज़्यादा इंश्योरेंस प्रीमियम, एक अच्छी कार और बच्चों की ट्यूशन फीस दे रहे हों। एक हाई अर्नर के तौर पर नौकरी जाने पर, खर्चों में तुरंत कटौती मुश्किल होती है। इसलिए, जितना ज़्यादा कमाते हैं, इमरजेंसी फंड उतना ही बड़ा होना चाहिए। ट्रेज़री ETF या कैश मैनेजमेंट अकाउंट का सुझाव अच्छा है, क्योंकि HENRYs अक्सर बड़े कैश बैलेंस को रेगुलर सेविंग्स अकाउंट में छोड़ देते हैं, जहाँ सिर्फ ज़ीरो पॉइंट एक या ज़ीरो पॉइंट ज़ीरो ज़ीरो एक परसेंट मिलता है। जबकि वे उसी सेफ्टी और लिक्विडिटी के साथ ढाई से पाँच परसेंट कमा सकते थे। पचास हज़ार डॉलर के इमरजेंसी फंड पर यह एक्स्ट्रा यील्ड हर साल लगभग दो हज़ार डॉलर अतिरिक्त हो सकती है। यहाँ सबसे ज़रूरी पहलू हैं लिक्विडिटी बनाए रखना और साथ ही अपने इमरजेंसी फंड से पैसे भी कमाना। ठीक है, स्टेप नंबर दो, दूसरी जगह जहाँ आपके पैसे जाने चाहिए वो है आपके HSA कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन्स को मैक्स करना, अगर आप एलिजिबल हैं तो। HSA बस एक हेल्थ सेविंग्स अकाउंट होता है। और इस अकाउंट में आप इन्वेस्ट कर सकते हैं और क्वालिफाइड मेडिकल खर्चों के लिए पेमेंट भी कर सकते हैं। अब, इस आर्टिकल ने इसे प्रायोरिटी लिस्ट में नंबर दो पर क्यों रखा है, इसके कुछ कारण हैं। पहला, HSA सबसे ज़्यादा टैक्स एफिशिएंट सेविंग्स व्हीकल है क्योंकि यह ट्रिपल टैक्स बेनिफिट्स देता है। नंबर एक, आपके कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन्स टैक्स डिडक्टिबल होते हैं, तो जिस भी साल आप इसमें कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट करते हैं, यह आपके टोटल इनकम टैक्स को कम कर देता है। नंबर दो, इस अकाउंट की ग्रोथ टैक्स फ्री होती है, बिल्कुल Roth IRA की तरह। और नंबर तीन, क्वालिफाइड मेडिकल खर्चों के लिए विथड्रॉअल्स भी टैक्स फ्री होते हैं। HSA के बारे में सबसे दिलचस्प बात यह है कि आज अपनी जेब से किए गए मेडिकल खर्चे के लिए आप भविष्य में खुद को रीइम्बर्स कर सकते हैं। तो मान लीजिए कि आज आपको दो हज़ार डॉलर का एक मेडिकल खर्चा देना है। HSA फंड्स इस्तेमाल करने के बजाय, अच्छी इनकम होने पर आप इसे अपनी जेब से दे सकते हैं। इससे HSA फंड्स टैक्स फ्री ग्रो करते हैं और पिछले मेडिकल खर्चों के लिए पैसे एक्सेस करने का ऑप्शन भी रहता है। तो जब तक आप रसीद संभाल कर रखते हैं, मान लीजिए कि बीस साल बाद, आपके HSA में रखे दो हज़ार डॉलर बढ़कर आठ हज़ार डॉलर हो गए हैं। आप असल में पूरे आठ हज़ार डॉलर निकाल सकते हैं और बीस साल पुराने मेडिकल खर्चे का इस्तेमाल करके इसे टैक्स फ्री विथड्रॉअल के लिए क्वालिफाई कर सकते हैं, जो काफी पावरफुल है। HSA के इस सुझाव पर मेरा मानना है कि यह बिल्कुल सही है। मैं कहूँगा कि यह स्टेप अगले स्टेप के साथ इंटरचेंजेबल हो सकता है, जो कि 401k और IRA जैसे अकाउंट्स को मैक्स आउट करना है। लेकिन कुछ लोग इसे प्रायोरिटाइज़ करना चाहेंगे, खासकर अगर उनका एम्प्लॉयर HSA में कुछ कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन्स को मैच करता है। यह उन लोगों के लिए भी अच्छा है जो अपनी हेल्थ के हिसाब से एक हेल्थकेयर इमरजेंसी फंड चाहते हैं। अगर आपके पास HSA है तो एक बात जो आपको पक्की करनी है वो यह है कि आप असल में फंड्स को इन्वेस्ट कर रहे हैं। बहुत से लोग बस एक HSA या Roth IRA शुरू कर देते हैं और वे कोई भी फंड इन्वेस्ट नहीं करते, वे बस उसमें पैसे जमा करते हैं और उसका कुछ नहीं करते। तो यह पक्का करें कि आप उस पैसे को लो-कॉस्ट इंडेक्स फंड्स में डाल रहे हैं और इस तरह आप कम से कम HSA की टैक्स एफिशिएंसी का फायदा उठा पाएंगे। स्टेप नंबर तीन रिटायरमेंट कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन्स के बारे में है। मतलब अपने 401k, IRA या Roth IRAs में कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट करना। हमारे चैनल के सिक्सटी-सेवन परसेंट दर्शक US में हैं, जहाँ एम्प्लॉयर स्पॉन्सर्ड 401k प्लान्स होते हैं। अगर आप UK में हैं, तो वहाँ वर्कप्लेस पेंशन होती है। ऑस्ट्रेलिया में इसे सुपरएन्युएशन कहते हैं। कनाडा में RRSPs होते हैं। किसी भी हाल में, आप अपनी सैलरी का एक परसेंट रिटायरमेंट फंड में बचाना चाहेंगे। कई एक्सपर्ट्स का सुझाव है कि अपने पेचेक का लगभग दस से पंद्रह परसेंट रिटायरमेंट के लिए अलग रखें। यह सुनिश्चित करता है कि आप आराम से रिटायर हो पाएंगे। अमेरिका में एम्प्लॉयर मैचेस होते हैं। अगर आपका एम्प्लॉयर 401k मैच ऑफर करता है, तो हमेशा उसका पूरा फायदा उठाएँ। इसे समझाने के लिए, मान लीजिए आप साल का एक लाख डॉलर कमाते हैं। आपका एम्प्लॉयर हर साल आपकी सैलरी का तीन परसेंट तक या आपके तीन हज़ार डॉलर के कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन्स को 401k में मैच कर सकता है। आप इसका फायदा उठाना चाहेंगे क्योंकि यह एम्प्लॉयर की तरफ से फ्री का पैसा है। अमेरिका में एक हायर अर्नर के तौर पर, आपको यह देखना चाहिए कि क्या आपका एम्प्लॉयर मेगा बैकडोर कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन ऑफर करता है। यह आपको तेईस हज़ार पाँच सौ डॉलर प्रति वर्ष की नॉर्मल कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन लिमिट से ज़्यादा कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट करने देता है। हाल ही में, कई कंपनियाँ इसे ऑफर करने लगी हैं, खासकर टेक फील्ड में जहाँ गूगल, माइक्रोसॉफ्ट, मेटा और अमेज़न सभी इसे ऑफर करते हैं। मैं अभी स्क्रीन पर उनकी पूरी लिस्ट छोड़ दूँगा जिसका आप स्क्रीनशॉट ले सकते हैं, लेकिन मैं नीचे इस रिसोर्स का एक लिंक भी दूँगा जिसमें उन कई अलग-अलग कंपनियों की लिस्ट है जो मेगा बैकडोर रॉथ ऑफर करती हैं। आमतौर पर, यह ऐसे काम करता है कि आप नॉर्मल कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन लिमिट से ज़्यादा अपने प्लान में दिए गए एक आफ्टर-टैक्स बकेट में कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट करेंगे। फिर आप अपने 401k के अंदर ही रॉथ में इन-प्लान कन्वर्जन करेंगे या उसे रॉथ IRA में रोल कर देंगे। कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन्स पर पहले ही टैक्स देने से, इस कन्वर्जन पर टैक्स नहीं लगेगा, और यह बहुत पावरफुल है। अगर कन्फ्यूजिंग लग रहा है, तो एक अमेज़न एम्प्लॉई का उदाहरण देखते हैं जो साल का तीन लाख पैंतालीस हज़ार डॉलर कमाता है। उन्हें पूरा एम्प्लॉयर मैच मिलता है और वे अपने मेगा बैकडोर कॉन्ट्रिब्यूशन में और भी ज़्यादा कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट कर पाते हैं। आप देख सकते हैं कि अगर आपकी उम्र पचास साल से कम है, तो आप इस अकाउंट में हर साल उनहत्तर हज़ार डॉलर तक कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट कर सकते हैं, जिसमें मैच भी शामिल है। और यह समय के साथ वेल्थ जेनरेट करने के लिए बेहद पावरफुल है। तो हाँ, अपने रिटायरमेंट अकाउंट्स में कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट करने के अलावा, यह ज़रूर देखें कि क्या आपकी कंपनी किसी भी तरह का मेगा बैकडोर ऑफर करती है। इसके अलावा, बस यह पक्का करें कि आप अपने रिटायरमेंट अकाउंट में लगातार और नियमित रूप से कॉन्ट्रिब्यूट और इन्वेस्ट कर रहे हैं, क्योंकि स्टेप तीन आपकी नेट वर्थ बढ़ाने के लिए बहुत ज़रूरी है। ठीक है, स्टेप चार है पाँच परसेंट या उससे ज़्यादा इंटरेस्ट रेट वाले कर्ज़ चुकाना। मुझे यह बात बहुत पसंद है, क्योंकि इस स्टेप में मन की शांति का भी ध्यान रखा गया है। पहला, रेडिट आर्टिकल कहता है कि अगर आप सेविंग्स खत्म करने का सोच रहे हैं, तो ऐसा सिर्फ दस परसेंट से ज़्यादा इंटरेस्ट रेट वाले कर्ज़ों के लिए करें। पाँच से छह परसेंट इंटरेस्ट रेट वाले कर्ज़ों के लिए, सेविंग्स खत्म करने के बजाय लिक्विडिटी बनाए रखना और एक्स्ट्रा पेमेंट्स करना बेहतर है। दस परसेंट से ज़्यादा वाले कर्ज़ चुकाने पर ROI इतना अच्छा होता है कि सेविंग्स इस्तेमाल करना फायदेमंद है। जब यह पाँच परसेंट के करीब आता है, तब आप उन कर्ज़ों को पूरा चुकाने के लिए अपना समय ले सकते हैं। आपके मॉर्गेज की बात करें तो, आर्टिकल कहता है कि अगर आप डिडक्शन्स आइटमाइज कर रहे हैं, तो सात लाख पचास हज़ार डॉलर से कम के मॉर्गेज को प्रीपे न करें। यह सलाह तभी सही है जब मॉर्गेज सात लाख पचास हज़ार डॉलर से कम हो, आप डिडक्शन्स आइटमाइज कर रहे हों और इंटरेस्ट रेट इतना कम हो कि इन्वेस्टिंग, मॉर्गेज चुकाने से बेहतर रिटर्न दे। जो लोग नहीं जानते, US में सात लाख पचास हज़ार डॉलर से कम बैलेंस वाले मॉर्गेज पर आप चुकाए गए सारे इंटरेस्ट को डिडक्ट कर सकते हैं, बशर्ते आप डिडक्शन्स को आइटमाइज कर रहे हों। आपको खुद पता लगाना होगा कि क्या आपके सिनेरियो में आइटमाइज्ड डिडक्शन्स, सरकार के स्टैंडर्ड डिडक्शन से बेहतर हैं। अगर आप मैरिड फाइलिंग जॉइंटली कर रहे हैं, तो दो हज़ार पच्चीस में आपका स्टैंडर्ड डिडक्शन इकत्तीस हज़ार पाँच सौ डॉलर है। इस मॉर्गेज इंटरेस्ट डिडक्शन से तभी फायदा होगा जब आपके टोटल आइटमाइज्ड डिडक्शन्स स्टैंडर्ड डिडक्शन से ज़्यादा हों। एक उदाहरण के तौर पर, मान लीजिए आप शादीशुदा हैं और आपके पास मॉर्गेज इंटरेस्ट पच्चीस हज़ार, स्टेट और लोकल टैक्स दस हज़ार, और चैरिटेबल डोनेशन पाँच हज़ार हैं। कुल आइटमाइज्ड डिडक्शन चालीस हज़ार डॉलर हुए। क्योंकि चालीस हज़ार, इकत्तीस हज़ार पाँच सौ से ज़्यादा है, आपको टैक्स बचाने के लिए आइटमाइज करना चाहिए और फिर देखें कि स्टेप चार आप पर लागू होता है या नहीं। स्टेप चार में, हाई इंटरेस्ट वाले कर्ज चुकाना आपकी पहली प्रायोरिटी होनी चाहिए। अगर आपके पास दस परसेंट से ऊपर कुछ भी है, तो यहीं से शुरू करना बेस्ट है। स्टेप नंबर पाँच है एक टैक्सेबल ब्रोकरेज अकाउंट में इन्वेस्ट करना। अगर आप हाई अर्नर हैं और स्टेप एक से चार तक कर चुके हैं, तो एक्स्ट्रा सेविंग्स लॉन्ग टर्म ग्रोथ के लिए टैक्सेबल ब्रोकरेज अकाउंट में जा सकती है। किसमें इन्वेस्ट करना है, गाइड आपको वीटीआई और वीओओ जैसे ईटीएफ में इन्वेस्ट करने की सलाह देती है, न कि इंडिविजुअल स्टॉक्स में। इंडिविजुअल स्टॉक्स ज़्यादा रिटर्न के कारण रोमांचक हो सकते हैं, पर वे बहुत रिस्की भी होते हैं। मान लीजिए आपके पास एक हज़ार डॉलर हैं और आप पाँच कंपनियों में दो सौ डॉलर इन्वेस्ट करते हैं। अगर उनमें से कोई एक असाधारण रूप से अच्छा परफॉर्म करती है, जैसे हाल ही में एनवीडिया ने किया। एनवीडिया में आपका दो सौ डॉलर का इन्वेस्टमेंट अकेले छह सौ, आठ सौ या शायद एक हज़ार डॉलर में बदल सकता है, जिससे आपके पोर्टफोलियो गेन्स में काफी बढ़ोतरी होगी। लेकिन इसका दूसरा पहलू ये है, कि अगर आपके पाँच स्टॉक्स में से कोई एक खराब परफॉर्म करता है या दिवालिया हो जाता है, तो आपका नुकसान ओवरऑल मार्केट से ज़्यादा हो सकता है, खासकर बुरे साल में। जैसे, अगर किसी साल मार्केट दस परसेंट नीचे है और आपने ऐसी कंपनियों में इन्वेस्ट किया है जो पचास परसेंट नीचे हैं, तो आप एक वोलेटाइल साल में मार्केट से कम परफॉर्म करेंगे। इंडिविजुअल स्टॉक्स चुनना मार्केट को बीट करने और जल्दी वेल्थ बनाने का एक आकर्षक तरीका हो सकता है। लेकिन अगर आपको रिसर्च पसंद नहीं, या किसी स्टॉक पर ज़्यादा यकीन नहीं, और आप पैसे खोने का रिस्क नहीं लेना चाहते, तो इंडेक्स फंड्स ही आपके लिए बेहतर रहेंगे। इंडेक्स फंड्स एक पैसिव स्ट्रैटेजी है जहाँ आप इसे सेट करके भूल जाते हैं। आप मार्केट या इंडेक्स पर निर्भर रहते हैं, और आपको उनके हिस्टोरिकल एवरेज रिटर्न्स मिलते हैं। S&P 500 में, आपको हर साल आठ से दस परसेंट मिल सकता है। स्टेप नंबर छह: अपने आरएसयू का क्या करना है, अगर आपके पास कोई है तो। आरएसयू का मतलब है रेस्ट्रिक्टेड स्टॉक यूनिट्स, जो एम्प्लॉइज को कंपनी में ज़्यादा समय तक रहने के लिए प्रोत्साहित करते हैं। अगर आप टेस्ला में काम करते हैं और कंपनसेशन पैकेज में चार हज़ार आरएसयू मिलते हैं, तो यह बहुत अच्छा होगा क्योंकि इसकी कीमत एक मिलियन डॉलर से ज़्यादा है। लेकिन आमतौर पर आरएसयू का चार साल का रेस्ट्रिक्टेड वेस्टिंग शेड्यूल होता है, यानी आप एक साल की क्लिफ पर हर साल पच्चीस परसेंट अनलॉक करते हैं। इसका मतलब है कि आपको क्लिफ को पार करने और अपने टेस्ला आरएसयू स्टॉक का पच्चीस परसेंट अनलॉक करने के लिए कम से कम एक साल रहना होगा। फिर उसके बाद, हर महीने आप और ज़्यादा शेयर्स जमा करेंगे और अनलॉक करेंगे। एक साल की क्लिफ इसलिए रखी जाती है ताकि नए एम्प्लॉइज बस अंदर आकर, स्टॉक लेकर, उसे बेचकर, और फिर चले न जाएँ। गाइड कहती है कि आप आरएसयू को वेस्ट होते ही बेच दें, पर मुझे लगता है कि यह आपकी कंपनी और आपको अभी पैसे चाहिए या नहीं, इस पर निर्भर करता है। अगर आप अपनी कंपनी में विश्वास करते हैं और अभी पैसे की ज़रूरत नहीं है, तो शायद आप अपने आरएसयू होल्ड कर सकते हैं, क्योंकि यह वेल्थ बनाने का एक अच्छा तरीका हो सकता है अगर आप उन शेयर्स को होल्ड कर पाते हैं और आपकी कंपनी अच्छा करती है। रेडिट गाइड कहती है कि अगर आपके कुल इन्वेस्टमेंट्स का एक तिहाई से ज़्यादा कंपनी के आरएसयू में लगा है, तो डाइवर्सिफाई करें, जो आमतौर पर बहुत ज़्यादा कंसन्ट्रेटेड होने से बचने के लिए सही है। कुछ मामलों में आप अपनी कंपनी के स्टॉक को होल्ड करना चाहेंगे, खासकर अगर आपकी कंपनी रॉकेट शिप ट्रेजेक्टरी पर है। भले ही आपकी नेट वर्थ अचानक उस कंपनी के स्टॉक का बड़ा हिस्सा बन जाए, फिर भी यह लॉन्ग रन में आपके लिए बेहतर हो सकता है। उदाहरण के लिए, अगर आप एनवीडिया में काम करते हैं और आपके पास आरएसयू थे और इस साल की शुरुआत में, आपको पता था कि एआई बूम ज़ोरों पर था, तो क्या आप सच में उन स्टॉक्स में से एक से डाइवर्सिफाई करना चाहेंगे जिसमें अभी स्टॉक मार्केट में शायद सबसे ज़्यादा मोमेंटम है? शायद नहीं। यदि आपके स्टॉक विकल्प आपकी कंपनी के स्टॉक से खराब हैं, तो विविधता लाने पर विचार करें। आरएसयू को 401k या रॉथ आईआरए जैसे टैक्स-लाभ वाले खातों में स्थानांतरित नहीं किया जा सकता। गाइड का सुझाव है कि उन्हें बेचकर उन पैसों से उन खातों को फंड करें, यदि आपने पहले से नहीं किया है। मैं इससे काफी सहमत हूँ, पर यह आपकी स्थिति पर निर्भर करता है। स्टेप नंबर सात: लॉन्ग-टर्म ग्रोथ के लिए इक्विटीज़ का एक वेल-डाइवर्सिफाइड पोर्टफोलियो रखना। गाइड पचास साल से कम उम्र में अस्सी से सौ परसेंट इक्विटीज़ और कीमती धातुओं या क्रिप्टो जैसे ऑल्टरनेटिव्स में पाँच से दस परसेंट का छोटा एलोकेशन रखने का सुझाव देती है, अगर आप उस रिस्क के साथ कम्फर्टेबल हैं। पचास के बाद, वोलेटिलिटी कम करने और कैपिटल बचाने के लिए सत्तर या शायद साठ परसेंट इक्विटीज़ तक एडजस्ट करना चाहेंगे। यह रेकमेंडेशन काफी सटीक है। मैंने पहले भी शेयर किया है कि आप जितने छोटे होते हैं, उतना ही ज़्यादा रिस्क-ऑन हो सकते हैं, क्योंकि आप वेल्थ बनाने के फेज़ में होते हैं। इस स्टेप के लिए ज़्यादा कमेंट्स नहीं हैं। चलिए स्टेप नंबर आठ पर चलते हैं। स्टेप नंबर आठ: टर्म, डिसेबिलिटी या अम्ब्रेला इंश्योरेंस के ज़रिए इनकम और एसेट्स को प्रोटेक्ट करना। मुझे पर्सनली लगता है कि यह स्टेप ऑप्शनल है, लेकिन अगर आपको इंश्योरेंस चाहिए, तो इस गाइड के अनुसार उनके वेरिएशंस के बारे में बात करते हैं। पहला है टर्म लाइफ इंश्योरेंस। मेरी राय में, यह होल लाइफ से बेहतर है कम प्रीमियम, सीधी शर्तें। गाइड सलाह देती है कि आप अपनी हाउसहोल्ड इनकम का 4-8 गुना लाइफ इंश्योरेंस लें। तो उदाहरण के लिए, छह गुना लेते हैं। मान लीजिए आपकी हाउसहोल्ड इनकम साल की ढाई लाख डॉलर है। तो आपके पास एक लैडर्ड टर्म पॉलिसी हो सकती है जहाँ आप तीस साल के लिए एक मिलियन डॉलर और बीस साल के लिए पाँच लाख के लिए कवर्ड हैं। इस तरह, अगर आप अभी पैंतीस साल के हैं, तो आपके पास पैंसठ साल की उम्र में रिटायरमेंट तक आपको कवर करने वाली एक मिलियन डॉलर की पॉलिसी हो सकती है, और आपके पास एक एक्स्ट्रा पाँच लाख डॉलर होंगे जो आपको पचपन साल की उम्र तक कवर करेंगे, शायद जब आपका मॉर्गेज पूरी तरह से चुका दिया गया हो। तो पैंतीस से पचपन तक, आपके पास कुल डेढ़ मिलियन डॉलर की पॉलिसी होगी, जब आपकी ज़रूरतें सबसे ज़्यादा हों, जैसे बच्चे, मॉर्गेज, वगैरह। और पचपन से पैंसठ तक, आपके पास अभी भी वह एक मिलियन डॉलर का कवरेज होगा, क्योंकि शायद इस समय तक आपके बच्चे कॉलेज चले गए हैं और आपका मॉर्गेज चुका दिया गया है। हालाँकि, पैंसठ साल की उम्र के बाद, आप अपने दम पर हैं। लेकिन टर्म लाइफ इंश्योरेंस आपको मिलने वाले कवरेज के लिए अपेक्षाकृत सस्ता है। तो इस तरह की पॉलिसी के साथ, आपके प्रीमियम महीने में सौ से डेढ़ सौ डॉलर के बीच हो सकते हैं, अह, पंद्रह सौ से दो हज़ार डॉलर प्रति माह की तुलना में जो एक होल लाइफ पॉलिसी हो सकती है। दूसरे प्रकार का इंश्योरेंस है डिसेबिलिटी इंश्योरेंस। अगर आपका प्रोफेशन फिजिकल एबिलिटीज़ पर निर्भर करता है, जैसे आप एक सर्जन हैं, तो ओन ऑक्यूपेशन डिसेबिलिटी पॉलिसी लें। काम नहीं कर सकते तो कमाई प्रोटेक्ट करने के लिए साठ से सत्तर परसेंट इनकम रिप्लेसमेंट का लक्ष्य रखें। ये सब तो इनकम प्रोटेक्शन की बात थी। अब आते हैं तीसरे प्रकार के इंश्योरेंस पर, अम्ब्रेला इंश्योरेंस। ये हाई अर्नर्स के लिए बहुत ही ज़रूरी है, क्योंकि आप जितने ज़्यादा अमीर होते जाते हैं, मुकदमों के लिए उतने ही बड़े टारगेट बन जाते हैं। अम्ब्रेला इंश्योरेंस तब काम आता है जब आपकी रेगुलर ऑटो या होमओनर इंश्योरेंस की लिमिट्स पार हो जाती हैं। मान लीजिए आप किसी गंभीर कार एक्सीडेंट में हैं और कोई आप पर दो मिलियन डॉलर का मुकदमा करता है। आपका ऑटो इंश्योरेंस शायद पाँच लाख डॉलर कवर करेगा, लेकिन इससे आप पर डेढ़ मिलियन डॉलर की ज़िम्मेदारी आ जाएगी। यह आपकी पूरी नेट वर्थ और भविष्य की कमाई को खत्म कर सकता है, लेकिन एक दो मिलियन डॉलर की अम्ब्रेला पॉलिसी उस गैप को भर देगी। गाइड कम से कम एक मिलियन डॉलर के अम्ब्रेला कवरेज की सलाह देती है, लेकिन एक हाई अर्नर के रूप में, आप शायद दो मिलियन डॉलर से ज़्यादा कंसीडर करना चाहेंगे। इस इंश्योरेंस की कॉस्ट काफी सस्ती है। यह अक्सर एक मिलियन डॉलर के कवरेज के लिए सिर्फ दो सौ से चार सौ डॉलर प्रति वर्ष होता है, और हर एडिशनल मिलियन पर शायद सिर्फ सौ या दो सौ डॉलर प्रति वर्ष और लगेंगे। यह निश्चित रूप से रखना अच्छा है। अगर आप हाई अर्नर हैं, तो आपको इनमें से किसी इंश्योरेंस की ज़रूरत नहीं है, लेकिन कुछ लोगों को इन्हें रखना पसंद होता है। यह हाई इनकम अर्नर्स के लिए एक कंप्लीट आठ-स्टेप फाइनेंशियल प्लेबुक थी, जिसे महत्व के क्रम में प्राथमिकता दी गई है। इस फ्रेमवर्क में मुझे पसंद है कि यह अंदाज़ा मिटा देता है कि अगला डॉलर कहाँ जाए, और उस रेडिट उपयोगकर्ता को धन्यवाद जिसने इसे संकलित किया। एक हाई अर्नर होना सिर्फ बहुत सारा पैसा कमाना नहीं है, बल्कि यह उस पैसे को सही तरीके से मैनेज करके, उसे लंबे समय तक चलने वाली और टिकाऊ वेल्थ में बदलने के बारे में है। आप अभी किस स्टेप पर काम कर रहे हैं? नीचे कमेंट्स में बताएं। अगर आप मेरे इस वीडियो में इंटरेस्टेड हैं कि अमीर लोग पैसे के बारे में कैसे सोचना पसंद करते हैं, तो आप यहाँ यह वीडियो देख सकते हैं। यह टॉप एक परसेंट और उससे आगे के लोगों की मानसिकता पर एक अच्छा वीडियो है। मैं आप लोगों से उस वीडियो में या इस चैनल पर भविष्य के किसी वीडियो में मिलूँगा। यहाँ फिर से आने के लिए धन्यवाद। पीस।

  3. @johnmichaelmorrow1589: This is a great guide for above average income but, for people with truly high income this guide is a ticking time bomb for taxes. Most people will save for retirement then retire and spend down their accounts into their 80s and then die and leave their home to heirs. This advice is adequate for that person; however, for truly high income they will never spend all their money. They might spend 25k/m or 300k/yr while netting over 500k+ in income. In that range they will likely hit retirement with well over 10M in assets. When they retire they can replace their income using only 3% or less of their portfolio meaning they will probably die leaving over 40M to their heirs. If you own real estate and individual stocks their is a step up in basis and the heirs pay no income or capital gains taxes to receive those dollars, but if they have 10M plus in retirement accounts those could be double taxed meaning estate taxes and income taxes on those accounts. This could leave over 2/3rds of retirement accounts to the IRS at death not to mention RMDs which force that retiree into the highest tax bracket during retirement. You might say to avoid that just spend down retirement accounts first before brokerage accounts to avoid the tax trap at death; however, selling individual stocks is a 23.8% maximum CG tax and liquidating retirement accounts is a 37% maximum federal income tax (not including state taxes) no one will willing pay 37% over 23.8% meaning the retirement accounts never get touched and largely go the IRS at death. Once you reach the point that you are able to save over about 100k/yr you take the employee match and that’s it. no more dollars to tax deferred accounts. Hope this helps anymore making over about 400k-500k/yr.

- The One Simple Budgeting Method That Changed My Life: Kind: captions Language: zh 有一个简单的预算方法， 可以帮你理清财务。 我已经用了快十年了， 那就是像经营企业一样经营自己。 在今天的视频里， 我们就来聊聊这是什么意思， 我还会和大家分享我今天将 要介绍的具体预算方法， 以及你们该如何实践。 我个人对这个方法深信不疑， 因为你想想， 如果你像经营一家盈利 的企业一样经营自己， 那么每个月底会发生什么呢？ 你就会有剩余的利润， 也就是净利润， 这样你才能把你的“企 业”长久地经营下去。 能盈利的企业之所以能持续经营， 就是因为它们赚的钱 总是比支出的多。 所以， 像经营企业一样管理财务的第一步， 是至少要了解你自己 的个人财务状况。 根据莱斯大学的一项心理学研究， 了解自己的财务状况是人们维持甚 至改善个人财务的最大原因之一。 很震惊吧！ 我知道， 但你听听这项研究就明白了。 这位教授就个人对其金融资产、 负债和消费模式的了 解程度之间的关系， 进行了三项不同的研究， 并基本得出结论， 引述如下：“对自身财务状 况更高程度的自我认知， 会转化为对过去理财成败的反馈， 降低对当前财务优劣势的不确定性， 并明确未来的前进方向。 ”。 长话短说，这应该不让你意外。 你越是关注一件事， 你自然就会做得越好， 不确定性也会随之减少。 当然，除非你是我， 并且正在试着找女朋友， 在这种情况下， 投入更多关注并不等 于能获得更多成功。 所以我通常还是喜欢专注于理财， 因为你越关注它， 情况通常就会变得越好。 说点正经的， 通常有朋友来问我：“嘿， 我怎样才能更好地管 理我的个人财务？ ”我总是给他们提出 完全相同的挑战。 那就是至少连续三个月， 追踪他们的收入和支出， 要精确到元。 那些真正成功改善财务状况的人， 都是能够坚持完成这个挑战的人， 而且他们还会以一定的频率， 查看自己的银行账户、 信用卡账单和在线账户。 通常那些每周至少查一次 或更频繁查账的人…… 他们的个人理财能力普遍更好。 这也有道理， 因为这样你就能确切地知 道自己每天的财务状况。 我个人是每天或每隔一天 就查看我的银行账户， 这让我在个人…… 财务生活方面感到非常安心。 事实上， 如果你看过我其他一些视频， 你应该知道我有一个 出了名的财务总表， 我会在每个月底用它来统 计我所有的收入和支出。 这其实是为什么每个月的最后一天对 我来说都很有趣的一个重要原因。 比如今天是十月三十号， 明天是十月三十一号， 是万圣节没错，但它也是月底， 我可以统计我的收入和支出了。 我真正喜欢做的是， 在晚上结束时拿一瓶健怡可乐。 然后我就会查看我所有不同的账户， 把所有东西都记录下来， 看看过去一个月里各项情况如何。 然后我再看看从今年早 些时候到现在的进展， 我的净资产是否在增长，我的…… 投资是否在增长，等等等等。 这绝对是我最喜欢的日子之一。 我一想到月底能有这么多乐趣， 就真的会流口水。 不过， 有些人并不像我这样享受理财。 我给一些朋友布置了那个挑战， 就是为期三个月的消 费追踪挑战等等， 如果他们花二十美元买了杯 Peet's 咖啡， 或者偶尔花一百美元买了件名牌 T 恤， 他们就会觉得很尴尬。 他们就是…… 普遍害怕面对自己的财务状况。 这种对财务状况感到尴 尬的感觉非常普遍， 它甚至可能会让你不知所措， 以至于因为对花销感到内疚而 无法真正去追踪你的支出。 如果你正在看这个视 频并且你就是这样， 你只需要知道这完全 是源于心理作用， 而且有几种方法可以克服它。 很多人害怕面对的一件事就 是他们自己的财务现实， 因为…… 直面你的财务现实可能是 一件非常可怕的事情。 你可能有两万美元的债务， 你可能有学生贷款， 你的信用卡可能有很高的未付余额， 或者你的银行账户余额可能是负数。 而且你知道，如果你去查银行账户， 那个数字就真的会摆在你面前， 让你无法回避。 然而， 我认为你能做的最好的事情之一， 就是直面你的债务， 直面你现在所面临的现实。 你需要从现在开始，追踪你的进展。 你现在可能负债两万五千美元， 这感觉肯定很不好受， 但如果你从现在开始追踪， 我敢保证， 只要你在接下来的几 个月里持续追踪， 并且取得了一些进展， 那就会成为你继续前进的巨大动力。 在过去的六到七年里， 我每年都在做记录。 即使在刚开始我一年只挣 三万到四万美元的时候， 我意识到只要我在进步， 我就走在正确的轨道上。 现在回看六年前， 看看我当时的情况， 然后意识到自己已经走了这么远， 真的很有趣。 我认为通过这种记录， 你会得到一份像是财 务日记一样的东西。 这会对你非常有帮助， 在你的人生道路上建立自信。 所以，如果你也想理好财， 我会这么做。 我会做四件事。 第一， 我肯定会从这里开始追踪你的支出。 这会是一个相当需要 手动操作的过程。 你可以查看过去三个月的信 用卡账单和银行对账单。 然后大致算出你在每个 类别上的平均花费。 第二， 每周至少查看一次网 银和信用卡余额， 以便及时掌握财务状况， 越频繁越好。 第三，我会开始做预算， 我们很快就会用到我使用 的那个具体的预算方法。 第四， 我会学习将你的消费与你 的自我认同分离开来。 一开始，这最后一点真的很难， 特别是当你清楚地知道自己目前 的消费状况并为此感到尴尬时。 然而，如果你练习记录支出和收入， 慢慢地你就会更像一个观察者。 你开始，像是，观察…… 你是如何花钱的， 但你几乎是从第三方或第 三人称的视角来看待它。 你只是在非常客观地 看待自己的消费。 这种感觉有点难以言表， 但当我花钱时， 我几乎不觉得是我自己在花钱。 我只是从一个很远的距离看着自己， 就像一个外星人俯视着地球， 看着汉弗莱，看他花了多少钱。 我有点像是从一个客观 的角度来看待这件事。 所以当我花了一些钱， 我并不会感觉太糟糕。 我只会想：“哦，酷。 我花二十美元买了杯 Peet's 咖啡。 ”希望你也能达到一个对自己的消费 几乎或完全没有情绪波动的境界。 这样你就能定期地客观 审视自己的消费了。 好了， 一旦你对自己的财务状况 有了非常清晰的认识， 我们就可以开始介绍那个改 变了我人生的预算方法了， 我个人喜欢称之为我人生的损益表， 或者基本上就是一张利润和亏损表。 损益表通常是企业用来查看其在任 何特定月份的盈利情况的报表。 好的， 现在我们来看一个具体的范例。 在最上面这里， 大家可以看到这家公司当月所实 现的销售收入是$3,367。 接着往下看，他们还有销货成本， 这其中包括了材料、进口、 外包、 库存和运费等等——这些 总计是$1,400。 这样一来， 他们所获得的毛利就达到 了$1,967.20。 他们还有一些其他的运营开销， 比如员工汤姆的薪水、玛丽的薪水、 日常的电话费和办公文具费。 所有这些开销总计是$1,185， 所以他们月底最终实现的净 利润是$782.20。 所以，从视觉上来看， 这张报表是从上到下看的。 在最上面， 你可以看到他们赚了多少收入， 然后随着损益表往下看， 你可以看到他们为了维 持生意花了多少钱， 也就是他们的销货成本。 最后， 任何其他的可变费用都在最下面。 所以，对这家公司来说， 月底有了这$782.20的利润， 他们就可以拿这笔利润， 并且在使用上有很多的灵活性。 他们可以再投资到生意上， 或者也可以把钱取出来买一 台PlayStation 4。 这里的重点是， 他们每个月都有一笔剩余 的利润可以用于未来。 那么，这跟我们有什么关系呢？ 或者说，跟你我有什么关系？ 这正是改变了我人生轨迹的方法。 所以，我们再来看看这张损益表， 然后把它简化成三、四个类别。 好的，对于个人损益表， 顶部的“收入”项保持不变。 你可能有薪水或工资， 所以应知道每月这笔钱是多少。 对于“销货成本”， 我们将其替换为一个更贴切的类别， 叫做“固定开销”。 这些是你生活中不可 或缺的必要支出。 例如，房租、 保险、交通、 医疗保健等都属于此类。 对于这张损益表中的“费用”部分， 我们把它换成“可支配开销”。 这些是你在每个月底 所有的非必要开销。 比如外出吃饭、购物、 娱乐，还有你打电子游戏的习惯。 最后，“净利润”这一项， 我们就完全保留不变。 如果你觉得这看起来很复杂， 别担心， 我会在下方的说明区提供一 个现成的模板供你下载。 我基本上就是重新创 建了这张损益表， 但把它们做成了空白单元格， 这样你就可以填上自己的信息了。 所以，这就是我们要用的预算方法。 我们要为我们生活的每一 个月都创建一张损益表。 然后希望， 我们每个月底都能有 一些净利润剩下。 这个预算方法其实和所谓的 “零基预算法”很相似， 那是在个人理财圈里 很流行的一种方法。 在那种方法里， 你会把月收入放在最上面， 然后列出你所有的开销。 理想情况下，在每个月底， 你会剩下零美元， 这样每一块钱的去向就都有了着落。 然而，在损益表法中， 不同之处在于你每个 月底会剩一些钱， 而对于这笔剩下来的钱， 你可以决定要怎么用它。 你可以把它存起来用于大额消费， 可以为退休储蓄， 或者也可以把它当作下 个月要用的灵活资金。 在我看来， 做这些损益表的最好之处在于， 在有了两、三、 四个月的数据之后， 你就会算出你在每个类别 上的平均花费是多少。 这样你就可以看着某个类别， 比如娱乐或购物， 然后对自己说：“好吧， 我在这方面花太多钱了吗？ ”然后去质疑并找出是否可以在 某些类别上减少或削减开支。 理想情况下， 在你个人生活的损益表中， 你每个月底都会有一些净利润。 这会让我们有灵活性去分配这笔钱。 并且是策略性地分配这笔钱。 所以这就是我们下一步要讨论的， 也就是我们应该如何分配 每个月得到的净利润？ 如果大家还记得的话， 我曾在频道上做过一个视频， 叫做《你拿到薪水后， 钱应该花在的七个地方》。 那是一个非常深入的视频， 详细讲解了你每次拿到薪水时 究竟应该如何分配你的钱。 不过，在这个视频里， 我会做一个快速的回顾和精简。 我们要做的是，在每个月底， 我们应该有剩余的钱或净利润， 然后我们想用这笔钱来 巩固我们的财务基础。 一旦我们的财务基础稳固了， 我们就要开始追求财富的增长。 那么，关于我们的财务基础， 我们不妨这样来看。 在最底层， 你至少要确保支付你 债务的最低还款额， 比如你的信用卡、 学生贷款和汽车贷款。 原因是， 如果你没有打好这第一层基础， 也就是支付债务的最低还款额， 它可能会让整个结构崩溃， 因为你的信用分数可 能会被彻底毁掉。 只要错过一笔某种债务的还款， 就可能让你的分数暴 跌高达一百八十分。 这简直太疯狂了， 因为它会损害你未来…… 比如贷款买车、 贷款买房或为你生活中任何 想买的东西贷款的机会。 所以，在债务问题上， 我们需要做到完美还款。 但假设我们已经做到了这些， 那么下一个层面就是 建立一笔应急基金。 我个人认为， 你的应急基金里至少应该 存有六个月的生活费。 这意味着如果你每个月的生 活开销是$2,000， 你就应该至少存下$12,000。 确保你把这笔钱存放在一 个高收益储蓄账户里。 因为由于现在的利率， $12,000以你能拿到的百分 之四到四点五的无风险利率计算， 每年至少能多赚$500。 如果你在寻找高收益账户的资源， 我会在下面附上一些链接， 方便大家参考。 在这两个基础层面都搞定之后， 我认为有两条不同的路可以走， 而且我觉得这两条路是可以互换的。 第一条是， 你可以还清所有现有的债务， 除了你的房贷。 那个可以排除在外。 如果你这么做， 你将能过上一种基本上无债的生活， 并获得内心的平静和很大的灵活性。 第二个选择是， 开始向退休账户存钱， 比如罗斯IRA、 IRA和401(k)。 在这两个选项中，我认为第一， 还清债务能有效巩固你的财务基础； 而第二，向退休基金存钱并投资， 则是为了随着时间推移， 逐步增加你的财富。 然而，在资金分配上， 情况就变得有些棘手了。 我无法确切知道哪一个最适合你， 毕竟每个人的情况都略有不同。 就我个人而言，如果你问我， 我会努力还清我所有的债务， 包括我的学生贷款和信用卡。 然后， 我唯一可能排除在外的债务是房贷， 但这绝对取决于你的利率。 好的，如果你已经走到了这一步， 希望你的财务基础已经稳固， 或许也开始为退休存钱了， 那么你接下来可以做的事情 就是投资一个应税账户。 在应税账户里， 你就是努力去实现最佳的复利效应， 并试图增加你的净资产。 你可能会购置像房地产或 另类投资这样的资产， 但这里的关键是， 要投资你深刻理解的东西， 而不要仅仅因为朋友 告诉你才去投资。 无论是什么， 确保你有一种方法来追踪你的投 资以及它们随时间推移的表现， 因为从长远来看， 这会让你成为一个更好的投资者。 很多人甚至都走不到 购置资产这一步， 因为他们糟糕的理财能力让他们 一直卡在巩固财务基础的阶段。 如果你开始像经营一家 公司一样经营自己， 我至少可以保证你会在财 务生活中看到一些进步。 你将能够直面你可能 身处的财务现实。 而这才是真正重要的， 只要你在朝着你的财务目标前进， 那就是一种胜利。 如果你觉得这个视频有价值， 请务必订阅，并看看我的免费模板。 我今天谈到的那个预算方法， 会免费链接在下方。 如果你想了解更多资金分配知识， 请看我的下一个视频…… 关于《你拿到薪水后， 钱的七个去处》。 我们下个视频再见。 好的，就这样。

- Joel Leon: The beautiful, hard work of co-parenting | TED: Kind: captions Language: en My name is Joel, and I'm a co-parent. So, growing up, I never heard the term "co-parent." I heard a lot of other things, though, for starters, "absentee father," "sperm donor" -- that's a good one -- "deadbeat dad" and, my personal favorite, "baby daddy." "Baby daddy," for those not in the know, refers to an individual who helps to conceive a child but does little else. Baby daddy is also someone who is not married by law to the mother of said child. Growing up, I thought "co-parent" was reserved primarily for white families that starred in Netflix prime-time dramas. (Laughter) It still kind of does. But it wasn't used to explain the role of a parent. Right? Either you had kids or you didn't, and no one in my social circles or at our dinner table was having complex conversations about the role fathers played in that conversation, right? A more balanced, open, loving approach to parenting was not something we were discussing within our social circles. A majority of the time, the fathers I knew of growing up were barely present or just completely nonexistent. "Co-parent" wasn't a term I heard or saw where I grew up, where I came from. I come from the hood. That hood would be Creston Avenue, 188th in the Bronx. And for -- one person, that's what's up. Appreciate that. For a lot of us in that hood, there was only one person you could already turn to for food, shelter, warmth, love, discipline: our mothers. My mother, who I playfully call "Linda T," was my first example of real love and what showing up as a healthy co-parent looked like. She was a strong, determined single mother, a woman who would have benefited greatly from having a secure and stable partner as a co-parent. So I vowed whenever I got married, my boo and I would be together forever. You know? (Laughs) We'd share the same bed and home, we'd sleep under the same covers, we'd argue at IKEA -- normal stuff. My partner would feel seen and loved, and our children would grow up in a two-parent household. However, things rarely ever end up how we plan them. Our daughter Lilah has never known a household with both of her parents living together under one roof. Her mother and I were never married. We dated on and off for several months before we found out she was pregnant. Up until then, my mother didn't even know she existed. I was ashamed, I was embarrassed, and, at times, I was suicidal. I was asking myself, what was I doing? Where was I going wrong? I never wanted the stigma or label of what some identified as the stereotypical "black father." So: absentee, confrontational, combative, not present. It took a lot of work, time, energy and effort for us to finally realize that maybe co-parenting for us didn't need to mean a shared household and wedding bells, that maybe, just maybe, the way we showed up as co-parents lay not only in the layered nuances of our partnership but the capacity within our hearts to tend to a human that we helped create together. (Applause) It would involve love in a nurturing and safe environment that would feed Lilah long after we both left this earth. Fast-forward four years, and Lilah is now in pre-K. She loves gummies, and she says things like, "My heart is filled with love." She's the most loving, compassionate, empathetic human being I know, and the reason I get to tell you all of this is because she's back in the Bronx with her mother. You see, this is co-parenting, and in an ideal world, my mother would have had a co-parent, too. She would have had support, someone to show up and give her a break, a time off. In an ideal world, every parent is a co-parent. In an ideal world, both parents share the weight of the work appropriately. Lilah's mother and I have a schedule. Some days, I leave work and pick Lilah up from school, some days I don't. Lilah's mother gets to go rock climbing or study for the LSAT, and I get to stand in a room full of bold, dynamic and powerful women and talk about dad stuff. It is work, it is beautifully hard work dismantling the systems that would have us believe a woman's primary role is in the kitchen, tending to all things domestic, while the hapless dad fumbles all over himself whenever he has to spend a weekend alone with the kids. It is work that needs to happen right now. You see, far too often, what it seems like is when both parents are working, one parent is typically tasked with organizing the household and keeping the home running. That person is typically a woman or someone who identifies as such. Far too often, those who identify as mothers and as women have to sacrifice their dreams in order to appease the standard. They have to sacrifice their dreams in order to ensure that motherhood takes precedence over all else. And I'm not here to say that it doesn't, but what I am here to say is, as equal partners and co-parents, it is our duty to ensure that our co-parenting partners don't have to put their passions, their pursuits and their dreams to the back burner just because we're too self-absorbed to show up as allies. Co-parenting makes the space possible for everybody. As a co-parent, the time I've gotten to share and spend with Lilah is time I appreciate, the time that has allowed me to be fully present for my child, removing the notion that the emotional labor required to raise a child is a woman's work. As a co-parent, Lilah and I have built snowmen, we've played with acorns, we've rapped to the soundtrack of "Moana," I know you have, too. She's sat with me while I've led workshops at Columbia University, when I talk about the intersections of poetry, hip-hop and theater. We get to talk about her emotions and her feelings because we have exclusive time together, and that time is planned time, it's organized around not just my schedule but her mother's. Both of us, as co-parents, have unique parenting styles. And we may argue at times, but what we can always agree on is how to raise a human -- our human. I will never fully understand or comprehend what it means to hold a child in my body for 10 months. I will never be able to understand the trials and tribulations of breastfeeding, the work that it takes, the emotional, physical, psychological and emotional toll that carrying a human can have on the female body. What co-parenting does is say, we can create balance, a more balanced home and work life for everyone involved. Co-parenting says that while parenting may involve sacrifices, yes, the weight of that sacrifice is not solely resting on one parent alone. No matter your relational dynamic, no matter how you identify as a human being -- he, she, they, ze -- co-parenting says we can create space and equity, better communication, empathy, I hear you, I see you, how can I show up for you in ways that benefits our family? My goal: I want more fathers to embrace co-parenting as a model for a better tomorrow, a better today for ourselves, for our co-parenting partners, for our families, for our community. I want more fathers talking about fatherhood openly, candidly, honestly, lovingly. Right? I want more people to recognize that black fathers in particular are more than the court system, more than child support and more than what the media might portray us to be. Our role as fathers, our role as parents, our value as parents is not dependent on the zeroes at the ends of our checks to show up for our families, for the people we love, for our little ones. Being a father is not only a responsibility, it's an opportunity. - What Are The Financial Complexities Of Co-parenting Multiple Households?: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] What are the financial complexities of co-parenting multiple households? Imagine trying to keep to different homes running smoothly for your children while balancing your own budget. That's what many fathers face when co-parenting across multiple households. It's a challenge that involves more than just splitting time. It's about managing money in a way that keeps everyone stable. When parents separate, they often need to support to homes instead of one. This means paying for rent or mortgage payments, utilities, furniture, and household supplies for each place. For example, if a father lives in a rental apartment and the children stay there on weekdays, he might pay around $500 a month for rent and another hundred for utilities. Then when the children are with him on weekends, he has to cover food, clothing, and activities which can add up quickly. Budgeting becomes more complicated because income can change after separation. Some fathers might have a lower income or face new expenses like legal fees or child support payments. Creating a new financial plan that fits these changes is essential. It's important to be honest about what can be afforded so that the children's needs stay a priority without causing financial stress. Sharing costs for children is another tricky part. Expenses like doctor visits, school supplies, clothing, and extracurricular activities need to be divided fairly. If a child needs a new backpack costing $20 or sports fees of $50 a season, parents should agree on who pays what. Custody arrangements influence this division and sometimes one parent pays more if they have the children more often or earn more money. Long-term costs like saving for college or private school also add to the financial puzzle. If a father wants to contribute to a college savings plan, he might set aside $50 a month in a savings account. But if the other parent prefers to save four private school tuition, disagreements can happen. Clear agreements on these contributions help avoid misunderstandings later. Medical expenses are another part of the picture. If one parent has better health insurance, it might make sense for the children to be covered under that plan. But if coverage changes because of employment issues, both parents need to communicate quickly to find the best way to handle bills for doctor visits, prescriptions, or dental work. Supporting to households also means planning four unexpected costs. Emergency expenses like urgent medical care or replacing broken furniture can come up suddenly. Having a financial cushion or emergency fund can help manage these surprises without disrupting the children's stability. For fathers, balancing these financial responsibilities requires open communication with the co-parent. Talking about expenses regularly, planning ahead for big costs like school trips or medical bills, and seeking advice from financial professionals can make the process easier. It's all about working together to keep the children's lives steady and secure. In the end, managing money across multiple households involves careful planning, honest conversations, and shared responsibilities. Fathers who stay organized and cooperative can help create a supportive environment for their children, even amid the financial complexities of co-parenting.

- Co-parenting: How To Set Consistent Rules Across Two Homes? - Fatherhood Journey Network: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Co-arenting. How to set consistent rules across two homes. Have you ever wondered how to create a sense of stability for children living in two homes? Setting consistent rules across co-parenting arrangements is key to helping kids feel secure. When children know what to expect, they can transition between homes more smoothly. This is especially important for blended families where multiple parents may be involved. To start, open communication between both parents is essential. Regular discussions can help you agree on important areas like curfews, homework expectations, and screen time limits. It is important to compromise and find common ground. This way, children experience continuity in their daily lives regardless of which home they are in. Creating a shared list of rules can be a great tool. This list should include expectations like chores and curfews along with responsibilities for each parent. By clarifying roles, you reduce the chances of conflict. Both parents should support each other's authority. It is vital to avoid undermining rules set by the other parent as this can confuse children. Maintaining similar routines is another effective strategy. Consistent bedtimes, homework times, and meal schedules across both homes help children adjust more easily. When routines differ greatly, children may struggle with transitions and may act out. Here are some practical steps to consider. Regularly discuss and update rules as children grow and their needs change. Keep communication focused on the child's well-being. Avoid making assumptions about the other parents household. Coordinate schedules to allow children to continue extracurricular activities and maintain friendships. Use tools like shared calendars or co-parenting apps to keep track of schedules and commitments. Be reliable in pickup and drop off times to build trust and security. From a fatherhood perspective, setting consistent rules across to homes shows a commitment to providing a stable environment. Fathers can play a vital role by actively participating in discussions about rules. It is important to model respect for the co-parents household and reinforce agreed upon boundaries. This approach helps children feel valued and secure knowing both parents are working together for their well-being. Overall, consistent rules and routines across to homes requiring cooperation, flexibility, and ongoing communication between parents. Keeping the child's best interests at heart will guide you through this process.

- What Steps Help Dads Rebuild Social Life After Divorce? - Fatherhood Journey Network: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] What steps help dads rebuild social life after divorce? Imagine standing at a crossroads where your social world feels smaller than it used to be. After a divorce, many dads find their social circles shrink and reconnecting can seem daunting. But rebuilding your social life is not only possible, it's essential for your happiness and your children's well-being. Let's explore some clear steps to help you get back out there and create a vibrant social life again. First, focus on healing and emotional support. Divorce can stir up feelings of grief and anger. Talking to friends, family members, or a therapist can give you a safe space to process these emotions. It helps you move forward with a clearer mind. Next, reconnect with friends you may have drifted from during your marriage. Reach out with a simple message or invite them for coffee or a walk. Spending time with familiar faces can boost your confidence and make you feel less alone. Then consider meeting new people by joining activities that match your interests. Whether it's a sports league, a hobby group, or a local class, these are great ways to find like-minded individuals. For example, signing up for a cooking class or a running club can introduce you to new friends. Volunteering for community service is another excellent step. It allows you to give back while meeting people who care about similar causes. Look for local charities or neighborhood projects that need help. If you have children, participating in school events, sports teams, or parent groups can be especially helpful. These activities let you connect with other parents who understand your situation. Handling social situations can sometimes feel uncomfortable, especially if you run into your exartner. Focus on your well-being and remember that your priority is your happiness. Practice being present and positive during social events. Lastly, keep open communication with your children. Let them know about your new social plans in a way that makes them feel secure. When they see you happy and engaged, it helps strengthen your bond. Rebuilding your social life after divorce takes time, but taking these steps can help you create a fulfilling, supportive environment for yourself and your children. Remember, every small effort counts toward a brighter, more connected future.

- Divorce Lawyers Give Relationship Advice | Glamour: Kind: captions Language: en-US - Marriage is like the lottery. You're probably not gonna win. But if you win, what you win is so great that it's worth trying, it's worth buying the ticket and taking the ride. (soft plucked string music) You know, 53% of marriages end in divorce in New York state. So if I said to you there's a 53% chance that when you walk out the front door today, you're gonna get hit in the head with a bowling ball, you would either stay inside or at a minimum you would wear a helmet. You buy a house, they make you fill out 50 forms explaining that you understand the lead paint disclosures, you understand the loan, you understand everything. You get married, you don't even get a pamphlet. You don't even get a piece of paper that says, "Oh, by the way, you just did "the most legally significant thing, "other than dying, you'll ever do." The fact that you're entering into this significant legal contract but most people have zero idea what's actually involved in that contract, and spend a lot more time figuring out what kind of cake we should have at the event is a really strange thing to me. Take marriage seriously. (bell dings) - If you want to be with a person who is social, and very outgoing, then don't marry someone who is a homebody. - "This is who I want, this is what I expect. "If this doesn't work for you, then you may not be "the right guy for me." It's critical that those expectations are communicated early. - You're not going to change all that much just because you're getting married, and the person that you're marrying is most probably not going to change all that much either. - I always tell clients, "You divorce the person you were married to." Because clients come in and they'll say, you know, in this shocked way, say like, "I can't believe he's being so vindictive!" And I'll say, "Well, what was he like during the marriage?" And they say, "Well, he was vindictive." You know, "I can't believe he's nickel-and-diming me "on all the finances." And you say, "Well, what was he like with money "during the marriage?" And they say, "Well, he was the kind of person "that would nickel-and-dime everybody." "Okay, well why would you think he'd be different "during the divorce?" - I think one of the most important conversations you need to have with your partner before getting married is about your finances. Doing a prenup forces the couple to have a conversation. With a prenuptial agreement, there is a mandatory financial disclosure. Most people are out there making their own money, and by the time they get married they have their own bank accounts, and you know, student loans or credit card debts, and those are all sort of important things to know about the person that you are going to marry. - I think what a lot of us are looking for in someone to date is very different than what we're looking for in someone to marry. You know, I've had so many clients who, for example their boyfriend or girlfriend has a job that is so cool to have a boyfriend or girlfriend doing. A musician, a chef, there's something very exciting about dating someone in that position. But think about what it's going to be like to be married to someone in that position. A chef who works really long hours, late into the night. A person who travels for business that is on the road 45 weeks of the year. What makes for a good boyfriend or girlfriend and what makes for a good spouse or partner, especially if you want to have kids, is a tremendously different thing much of the time. When you represent people in divorce, you see good people at their worst. So I've seen, you know, stay at home moms, who, in the middle of a courtroom, throw something at their spouse. - The biggest mistake that people make when they're getting divorced is to make this into the winner and loser situation. They get so caught up into standing their ground that they lose focus of what really matters and the collateral damage that's happening around them. The key here is to keep your eye on the prize. It's my job to take your hand and walk you through this path, which is very scary, but the goal is to get to the other side, not to get stuck in the middle. I don't think that you have as much drama, unpredictable day-to-day experiences that you have in divorce and family law. - Being a divorce lawyer has helped me figure out in my own marriage what I'm fighting for and what I really don't want to lose. - I've been a divorce lawyer for 17 years, I still get teary-eyed at weddings. If I was to say to people what they should try to hold on to, is that sense of being this other person's cheerleader and being connected to them.

property hmrc issued Discovery assessments underpaid sdlt leading to a legal dispute the first year tribunal dismissed the appeal stating that there had not been full disclosure of facts and the schemes are found to be ineffective the appellants have 56 days to appeal stamp Duty land tax sdlt commonly known as stamp duty is a government tax imposed on property buyers in England and Northern Ireland the tax has a rich history dating back to its origins in Venice in 1604 where it was later reintroduced by the Spanish in the 1610s however stamp Duty as we recognize it today made its way to England on June 28th 1694 during the reign of William and Mary its introduction aimed to fund the war with France and initially applied to various documents including checks property and land transactions and receipts the success of stamp Duty in increasing the Crown's treasure led to its persistence for centuries over time the scope of the tax evolved eventually resulting in the introduction of a new type of land transfer tax in 2003 at London property our handpicked legal and tax experts who specialize and stamp Duty can help you make informed decisions when investing in UK property take advantage of stamp duty exemptions that may suit your investment plans email us at ask london. co.uk to start the conversations subscribe to our newsletter to receive our weekly bulletins and stay ahead of what's shaping the super Prime Property Market

blame for a lot of these rents going up for years now tech companies like real page yardi and their competitors have been selling a special piece of software to private landlords that allows them to aggregate all sorts of information about the apartments they own using that data collected from thousands of landlords and significant chunks of Any Given area's rental market real Pages algorithm recommends things things like what landlords should charge and specific occupancy rates most of the time pushing those two things in opposite directions take the occupancy rate for example landlords used to shoot for around 97 to9 98% occupancy obviously right you have Apartments just lease them out landlording is not a real job this shit's easy anyway maximizing the number of people occupying the places you own seems obvious and even lines up with what landlords lie and say their job is providing people with housing in recent years though landlords have found that this sort of quote unquote obvious logic doesn't hold up according to propublica real pages software has recommended property managers reduce their occupancy down to about 94% units stay empty but thanks to the algorithm's calculation and its ability to push out the same recommendation to a ton of property managers at once landlords actually see a net revenue increase of about 3 to 4% when they empty units and raise rents on those they keep occupied same goes for turnover using real Pages software landlords found that if they increase turnover by about 15% meaning kicking out tons of people as soon as their leases end or being more liberal with evictions they can raise rents quicker and this has real consequences consequences that the CEO of real page knows because last year he told a trade publication quote the net effect of driving revenue and pushing people out was $10 million in income I think that shows keeping the heads in the beds above all else is not always the best strategy this isn't normal rhetoric it's cruel calculated and basically automated violence especially given that hundreds of thousands of people in the US are homeless and a majority can't afford the place they live real Pages software which unfortunately gets better at this extortion racket the more signups it gets rigs the market even more in favor of landlords than it already was by letting landlords coordinate their price hikes without actually needing to collude just following an algorithm's recommendations real pay P's approach kicks more people out of their homes leaves more Apartments vacant and allows property managers sitting on hundreds of units to get a bigger chunk of people's dwindling incomes this single example shows that the price of rent has nothing to do with the number of livable homes and everything to do with how comfortable property managers are using various price scouching techniques in a for-profit business they're going to be very comfortable but underneath this thin layer of dystopian Tech there's a deeper core of private Finance that's really driving all this a big part of the landlording that exists today comes from smalltime landlords Mom and Pop Style setups where people buy an extra property with the express purpose of leasing it out but over time we've seen this smallscale approach complemented by huge interest from private Equity firms Apollo Global Management the Carlile group and maybe most famously Blackstone to name just three big ones in a sea of private Equity firms have all recently upped their investments in real estate in the first quarter of 2022 nearly a third of all single family homes sold in the US were bought by private investors and that's already not great but some areas are seeing even more dramatic numbers not only that real estate markets that investors used to not touch are seeing the same Trend nearly 25% of mobile home purchases in 2020 and 2021 were made by private Equity groups meaning some 21 million people living on very tight budgets saw their housing get bought up on mass and for some of them their rents go up as much as 60% by making cash offers and bypassing a lot of steps someone who would actually live in a house would take like an appraisal private Equity has completely taken over real estate swooping in and raising housing costs for everyone along the way renters and homeowners alike but why right sure profit is an easy answer but it's not really an explanation for why it's so profitable for so much Capital to go into real estate so much Capital actually that in the US out of roughly $178 trillion of National Assets almost half about 84 trillion is in real estate in the words of Robert Schiller the Nobel prize winning Economist quote far more important to the world's economies than the stock stock markets our wage and salary incomes and other non-financial sources of livelihood such as the economic value of our houses and apartments this is where the bulk of our wealth is found that's a relatively new thing and there's three parts to how we got here government policy generational wealth gaps and Capital's demand for liquidity since about the80s governments all over the world including the US have changed the way they operate pretty drastically where once the New Deal and the Keynesian era of the 20th century meant governments trying to run an economy with decent jobs that paid pretty well paired with decent government services Reagan's turn to neoliberalism meant pretty much gutting all that and replacing it with personal debt it's around this time that credit scores appeared and mortgage loans exploded this new governmental approach has a name house price keynesianism neoliberalism means lower real wages non-existent public service and weaker working protections it sucks but you can't jump straight into it to compensate workers for the fact that wealth was being siphoned to the top 1% much faster than before the 80s marked a radical shift towards Consumer Debt specifically mortgages basically while everything was being slashed and home budgets took a hit credit card debt and easy access to ultimately more expensive mortgages meant you could still live a version of the American dream including the all essential home ownership part but this has a few consequences for one it means people are now on the hook for a lot more debt is a form of public control and accountability there's this huge push for financial literacy programs to get people to take on more debt and most importantly get it through their heads that they have to pay these debts back and it's a moral failing if they don't ensuring this compliance when wages are declining is critical why because governments begin to facilitate using this housing debt as a financial instrument and integrate it into the broader economy housing debt becomes an asset that investors can start trading through a process called securitization if you haven't heard about this before basically you start with a mortgage in the primary Market the bank gives you a loan you pay the bank back seems simple enough starting in the 80s though the US government wanted to create a secondary market for all this debt for a free guide that can put you on the path to home ownership call The Fanny May Foundation we're showing America a new way home in 1981 Fanny May the Federal National Mortgage Association starts issuing mortgage-backed Securities 2 years later Congress lets Freddy Mack the federal Home Loan mortgage Corporation issue the first collateralized mortgage obligation and in 1984 Congress passes the secondary mortgage Market enhancement act which quote removes statutory restrictions on investments in private mbss by federal Charter depository institutions and then you have the tax reform Act of 1986 you know what it doesn't need to be this complicated the point is the government created a market for investors to bet on Mortgage Debt it allowed Banks to take all the mortgages they gave out pull them together slap a label on them so different mortgage pools could be compared and essentially sold them to investors all over the world it takes the illiquid physical home that someone actually lives in in and turns it into a liquid easily tradable valuable asset that investors can pour money into this was useful because back then capitalists had no idea what they were going to do with all the money they were making from not paying their workers fairly you can't leave that money just sitting there it'll lose some of its value and without money circulating in the economy the risk of an economic crisis keeps growing capitalism is constantly running towards this very precarious situation where money stops moving and suddenly people are left holding the bag for obligations they can no longer actually fulfill in David Harvey's words then you need a fix somewhere to fix and put money in and keep it moving and what could be better than real estate you have too much money you use it to create an asset of equal worth that you'll be able to sell at some point and you've saved your cash from sitting in the bank the problem with real estate is that it takes time to design build and sell homes like this for a profit luckily for capitalists though now that there's this huge secondary Market in mortgage back Securities thanks to the government everything moves a lot faster there's more debt and therefore a place to put your money if you have a lot of it that'll keep it circulating and growing this way real estate becomes not just a good investment for people looking to make more money but a source of liquidity for capitalist markets that are terrified of drying up with no more places to invest the money they've stolen and for the rest of us this means more expensive homes now that housing costs are no longer related to any sort of Supply or how useful they are for the people who actually live in them but rather how valuable they can be made as stores of value and sources of liquidity their prices reflect the power of capitalists over that process and their ability to enforce debt repayment from homeowners and landlords real estate becomes just another overflow tank that's meant to protect capitalists from rushing into the next Crisis this also means that governments are now desperate to keep this cycle going mortgages are inflating house prices because families can now borrow way more than they can afford mortgage back Securities inflate home prices because their value as a source of liquidity brings more money into the world of real estate and because home ownership is now people's Main Avenue for Building Wealth in a world of declining real wages keeping the value of these assets going up becomes a huge Obsession for governments desperately trying to avoid a return to a time when people were paid more fairly so cities weaken protections for renters to attract more corporate landlords neighborhoods gentrify to accommodate more sites for Capital to find its fix and public housing developments slow down everywhere and even start to be demolished to make room for privately funded construction some Twisted reading of this might make it seem like a win-win more money going into real estate means more or Better Homes right not really for this system to continue year after year you need two things to happen housing prices need to constantly keep Rising meaning buying a house or staying in the rental market is almost always guaranteed to be more unaffordable from one year to the next and people need to keep taking on more debt and being forced or guilted into paying it back two things that feed off each other and temporarily Stave off capitalist crises but make them way more destructive when they do hit so I think this is the most significant financial crisis in the post-war period the phone say a lot of their customers are freaked out waiting to see how low the Dow will go they're so while governments find new ways to create liquid assets and investors keep finding new ways to take advantage of that liquidity to grow their Capital the rest of us are left holding the bag taking on debt that we can't afford to live with or live without staying in homes that get more expensive than we can keep up with parasitic landlords using every trick in - Voice of the Child of Divorce: Kind: captions Language: en Dear Mom and Dad, I know that you are hurting. I'm hurting, too. I feel and feed off your tension, fear and shock. Although I'm young and cannot express verbally what is happening in our lives, I'm still feeling the impact. My heart is broken every time I have to give up a parent. My sense of security is lost. Please don't assume that I am resilient. Please don't assume that my life will be exactly as it was and that I will continue to feel the same love from both of you. I am a human being just like you. My needs are just like yours. I need love, attention, nurturing, stability, consistency, affection, understanding, patience and mostly to be wanted. When you fight over me or put me in the middle of your argument, you are sending me the message that winning with each other is more important than my life. I am learning from you that is better to be right than to be loved. You are teaching me that I came from a person who is unlovable and wrong, and that I am somehow wrong, too. When you confide your hurt in my heart, you are storing up adult pain and robbing me of my childhood. You are taking away my belief that love is unconditional and replacing it with a message that tells me to become hard and not to love because I will get hurt and not be able to recover. You may not understand this today, and I am so small that you are not thinking about my future, but you are putting me at a greater risk of getting a divorce myself. At times you are risking my safety to fill a void in your heart. My safety is your job. Without you and your protection I am unshielded from the world. This will manifest in irrational fears for me, because I will stay in a state of fight or flight for most of my life. Someday this initial shock will wear off, but how you choose to parent me through this crisis will never wear off. I will either feel your sense of selflessness, support and protection, or I will have a scar on my heart with a message that reads, "Good things happen to good people. I must be bad." Thoughtfully, The Child of Divorce

- How Can Parents Manage Inconsistent Rules Between Two Households After Divorce?: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] How can parents manage inconsistent rules between two households after divorce? Imagine your child feeling confused every time they switch homes because the rules are different. It's a common challenge after divorce, but there are ways to make transitions smoother. When parents focus on clear communication and working together, children feel more secure. Start by talking openly about what rules matter most. Agree on key areas like bedtimes, screen time, and discipline. Using tools like co-parenting apps can help keep everyone on the same page and avoid misunderstandings. It's okay if some rules differ, but try to find common ground on core values. For example, both homes should promote respect, safety, and responsibility. If one parent encourages polite manners, the other can support that, even if routines differ. Consistent consequences for behavior around homework or chores also help children know what to expect, no matter which house they are in. It's important not to criticize or contradict the other parents rules in front of the child. Supporting each parents authority helps children feel safe and reduces their stress. Visual aids like posted schedules or clear expectations in each home can serve as gentle reminders for children. Praising them when they follow rules reinforces positive behavior and helps them adapt more easily. Flexibility is key. Regularly check in to see how the rules are working and be open to making small adjustments. If disagreements become difficult, seeking help from a family therapist can provide guidance. Listening to your child's feelings about rules and routines is also vital. If they express stress or confusion, consider minor changes to ease their worries. Maintaining predictable routines in both homes helps children feel secure. Simple things like consistent bedtimes, meal routines, and homework schedules create a sense of stability. When parents work together on these strategies, children's emotional needs are better met. They can navigate life between two homes with less anxiety and more confidence.

- How to Handle Your Finances After a Divorce: Kind: captions Language: en - So the hard thing about life is that stuff happens that you don't plan for. And one of those is divorce, and it's extremely stressful. It's extremely heartbreaking for everyone involved. It's one of the most stressful, sad things that you can go through in life, and unfortunately it affects your money. And so a lot of the time I get questions about this. "Hey, Rachel. I'm getting divorced. What do I do?" And so you have to realize that a lot of things can't be guaranteed, but there are two things you need to remember. Number one, you will survive this, that you can move past it. It's heartbreaking, it's excruciating, but you can survive this. And number two, it doesn't have to cause a financial fallout. You can take the steps to be wise with your money and be smart for the future during this time. So there are five steps I want you to make to ensure that financially you're gonna be okay so that you can focus mainly on what matters. So step one, the first thing you wanna do is get a team together. You wanna get trusted family members and friends around you for emotional support. It's gonna be extremely hard and to have someone to talk with you through this time is very important. You're going to want to get a divorce attorney who will listen to you and provide you some really sound legal advice during this time. You're gonna wanna contact a CPA or a financial planner to make sure all your investments, taxes, everything like that, the big stuff is going through well. And someone that really has walked with other clients through divorce. And again, they can help you make some really sound decisions with your money You also wanna contact an ELP, one of our Endorsed Local Providers, when it comes to insurance. So there's different options for insurance like home, life, auto, any other policies that you might need going forward. And I would also recommend contacting a counselor, a pastor, again, someone that has more of a professional side to this, that can walk you through the emotions of it cause it is extremely painful. It can be extremely wounding and damaging and making sure you're taking care of not just your money, but your emotional side as well. Alright, step two, you need to know your numbers. So gather all the financial information you can. So I'm talking about tax returns, real estate paperwork, retirement, investment accounts, I mean anything around that, you need to know that you have those statements and you can see the numbers, and then check with your attorney about opening a new checking and savings account without your spouse's name on them. So make sure that they are removed from those accounts. You also wanna update insurance policies and change the beneficiaries. You wanna make or update your will. This is especially important if you have children. So if you have kids, make sure you update your will. Step three, create an emergency fund. This is going to be your safety net. So during a time like this of crisis, and I say this, if it's divorce, if there's a death close to you and your family, if there's times of that type of grief, you just wanna pause everything. Don't make any huge moves right now with your money, but make sure that you are saving. Having that safety net as much cash as possible is going to give you that peace of mind. Step four, make adjustments to your budget. So make sure you have a budget in place and then ask yourself the big questions. Are you going to be selling your home? Do you think you need to downsize? And make sure you make a budget and you tell every single dollar where it's going. And remember, right now, your main focus is to stockpile cash. So anywhere you can cut back on your lifestyle is really wise. And again, this is for the short term to get some cash in place, and then in the future, you can add some money back to your budget. And step five, last but not least, take a deep breath and just stay focused. For me, journaling really helps during times of grief and sadness, or even times of joy. There might be moments that you see that light of joy. Write stuff down so you can look back and you can see God's faithfulness through this journey, even though it's extremely painful, but journaling sometimes helps. And I would say start small wherever you are financially, whatever that emergency fund is you're putting in place. Start small, those little steps you take in life, will get you moving forward. So just know that again, extremely painful and I'm so sorry for going through it, but you have this and you are not alone. Everything you do now, the decisions you make now are really gonna help you build a new future ahead of you and you're gonna get there. So if you're going through this hard time, click the link below cause we have our divorce checklist and it's really tactical, but I think it's a very, very helpful tool if you're walking through this process to give you the direction that you need. And also if you go check out the official Rachel Cruze Facebook group, there are people in there right now, literally going down this path. They are in the exact same spot as you and finding people that are going through it for you, just for support to talk to cause they know exactly what's happening, how you're feeling. Go in there and talk to them. Find that community, even if it's people you don't know in person again, have those trusted friends and family that can be with you in person, but sometimes that online community can help you and support you you as well. multif family homes with low interest interest mortgages which create a financial dilemma if they decide to separate selling the home might mean facing a high tax burden while keeping it could lead to financial challenges due to potentially higher mortgage rates let's take a short break for a quiz consider the financial issues when selling a multif family home during a divorce in high cost areas is video the correct answer is D all of the above selling a multif family home during a divorce in high-cost areas can lead to high tax burdens loss of valuable rental income and potentially facing higher mortgage rates when purchasing a new property the key challenges in navigating divorce with multif family homes involve dividing equity and preserving favorable mortgage terms buying out a spouse's share can involve refinancing and selling might eliminate valuable rental income making it expensive to buy a new home next we'll look at potential solutions to navigate these challenges successfully let's explore potential Solutions first is mortgage assumption involving consultation with legal and mortgage professionals negotiated Equity splits can reduce the need for refinancing deferred buyouts allow co-ownership with a structured payment plan co-ownership with rental agreement ments allows for income generation lastly selling the property offers a clean break but involves losing the current mortgage's benefits to implement these Solutions you'd want to consult with a mortgage professional about eligibility for assuming the mortgage legal council is crucial for drafting any agreements like negotiated Equity splits or deferred buyouts make sure there's thorough documentation for any co-ownership and rental agreements if selling the property carefully consider the tax I grew up on a farm and so I helped with the lamming and out of that I ended up having three of my own because I they were lambs that needed feeding and I couldn't say goodbye to them. So I have in the field up the back three sheep and I of course in the summer you take their wool off and so their wool is actually packed around the bath. Three there are three fleces packed around the bath for insulation. So that keeps it so the water stays really warm for a long time. >> Oh, that is just such a magical addition to the home.   20. @andreirodin2061: In a capitalist society the focus is on teaching kids how to save. 
In other world: on how to earn the money.
Teaching a child how to save shouldn’t come before learning how to earn! 
So, here’s an idea: teach your child how to earn the $$ first, so then they will have intrinsic motivation to save what they earned.🤷🏼‍♂️ 
Does money come free to adults? How? 
Teach that☝🏻 
You can earn the $$, you can steal it, you can win it, or you can inherit it. 
Once you introduce this idea, and practice EARNING and (yes!) winning (lottery, tv shows, bets), then you can move on to saving it. 
At what age should you start?🤔 
Consider the child’s brain, not adult.
Young children have very short attention spans and memory: what happens in AM rarely matters in PM🤷🏼‍♂️ 
Teaching a young child how to save is a totally ridiculous point: they lose their interest very quickly for long-term projects. This is such a basic fact. 
WHY would anyone start teach a little kid how to save? 
Especially, how to save that is not earned? 

If you think I’m wrong, read up on stats about people who win big $$ in lottery 🤷🏼‍♂️ 
“Easy comes - easy goes”🤷🏼‍♂️🤣🤣 
Did any millionaire become rich by saving? Or did they have to learn how to EARN and INCREASE their capital?!
So, let’s stop putting the cart in front of the horse: teach the money value through EARNING IT, and then - saving it up.

- What Are the Legal Considerations in Co-Parenting Strategies? | Family Law Gurus News: Kind: captions Language: en What are the legal considerations in co-parenting strategies? In recent days, co-parenting has become a hot topic. As more families navigate the complexities of shared parenting after separation or divorce, many parents are searching for clear guidance on how to manage their responsibilities legally and effectively. Understanding the legal considerations in co-parenting strategies is essential for ensuring the well-being of children involved. Co-parenting involves much more than just sharing time with children. Family law outlines specific legal expectations that shape how parents should approach their shared responsibilities. A primary legal consideration is the requirement to prioritize the child's best interests. Courts are mandated to make decisions based on what benefits the child, not the parents. This means parents should support their child's relationship with the other parent and avoid actions that could harm the child emotionally or physically. Clear communication is another key legal expectation. Parents are encouraged to keep discussions focused on their children's needs and maintain respectful dialogue. Keeping written records of agreements and any changes to parenting plans is crucial, especially when disputes arise or travel plans are made. Parenting plans are often legally required. These plans detail how parents will share time with their children, including schedules for school days, holidays, and vacations. Courts expect parents to adhere to these plans unless both parents agree to changes in writing. Violating a court-hordered plan can lead to legal consequences. Flexibility in co-parenting is allowed, but only with mutual agreement. If parents wish to change the schedule, both must consent and document it in writing. If they cannot agree, the original court order remains in effect. Temporary changes can be made for short-term issues like medical emergencies, but these should be documented and if necessary, approved by the court. Bird nesting is a co-parenting strategy where children remanges in one home while parents take turns living there. This arrangement requires detailed written agreements covering schedules, finances, household rules, and exit strategies. Bird nesting may not be suitable for high conflict situations or where there is domestic abuse. Courts may support bird nesting if it serves the child's best interests and both parents are committed to making it work. Child support calculations can become complicated with co-parenting strategies like bird nesting as traditional formulas assume separate households. Parents should consult family lawyers to establish fair financial arrangements reflecting shared housing and parenting responsibilities. As children grow older, their views should be considered in custody decisions. Child lawyers can help ensure that children's voices are heard while protecting them from feeling responsible for the outcome. In disputes, courts evaluate parental cooperation, financial stability, and the impact on the child's welfare before approving or modifying co-parenting arrangements. Legal guidance is recommended for parents needing help negotiating or documenting changes to their co-parenting plans.

- What Role Does Communication Play in Co-Parenting Strategies? | Family Law Gurus News: Kind: captions Language: en What role does communication play in co-parenting strategies? Breaking news in family law. The question on many parents minds today is what role does communication play in co-parenting strategies? As co-parenting arrangements become more common, understanding effective communication is vital. Many parents are searching for ways to improve their co-parenting relationships, especially after recent discussions in the media about family dynamics. Effective communication is essential for successful co-parenting. It allows parents to address issues quickly and avoid misunderstandings. This ongoing dialogue helps coordinate parenting styles and decisions regarding children. When parents communicate openly, they create a stable environment for their children even while living apart. In co-parenting arrangements like bird nesting, where children stay in one home and parents wrote hate living there, communication becomes even more critical. These arrangements requiring detailed agreements that cover schedules, finances, and household rules. Without clear communication, coordinating these logistics can become challenging. This may lead to conflicts that can negatively impact the children involved. Successful co-parenting relies on parents ability to cooperate and share decision-making. Honest and respectful communication is the foundation for this cooperation. This is especially true in amicable divorces where parents prioritize their children's well-being. When communication breaks down, it often results in increased conflict, making arrangements like bird nesting impractical. Family courts generally favor co-parenting plans where communication and cooperation are evident. These factors contribute significantly to children's welfare. Clear communication also helps negotiate financial responsibilities and resolve disputes without needing court intervention. Communication plays a central role in co-parenting by enabling parents to coordinate effectively reduce conflict and maintain a stable environment for their children. It is the foundation upon which successful co-parenting strategies are built. As parents navigate their new roles, focusing on communication can make a significant difference in their children's lives.
