- Divorce के बाद ₹0 और 2 बच्चे, Single Mother से Millionaire बनने की कहानी | Priya's Inspiring Story: Kind: captions Language: en When I got there, I saw my father's dead body and I could n't understand what to do. So, everything was gone. Everything was gone except our house. This big article came out and overnight people started calling me. [Music] Hello, my name is Priya Khanna and today I am going to tell you the entire story of my life from beginning to end. My story started. Now we reached 10th standard. That is, in 10th class, my father asked me if I would pass. Why did he ask because in my entire life I never passed any class. I always failed in some subject or the other. My mother was a teacher in the same school. The problem was that I was made to stand outside the class. My mother used to come a lot and ask what is happening to her. She is always having problems. So I told my father that the day I take this exam, I will either pass in first class or fail. I passed in first class. Now comes my college life. First class is great, there is no problem. After a few days, college gets over. My parents get me married. The marriage happened and I left. Good. The family was good, everything was good but now a twist came and the twist was that my husband was a little different, he was a little older than me and he thought that brother I am strong, so that story of I am strong did not sit in my mind, so I tried for a while, we had a daughter, then he got posted and we went to Dubai city, now my second chapter begins where I had to handle household work, outside work, job, child and a lot of ups and downs of husband, I was not able to handle it, it was very difficult, still I did it with laughter, in this way a little situation got out of hand, so I told my father that you tell me what should I do, then he said that no problem, let's try to make him understand or calm him down or something, the whole family got together, some talks happened, some new things happened, nothing worked out, then what my father said was that look you are just 28 years old, by then I had two children, a son and a daughter, in 28 years it is not the right way to handle each problem or one by one. A marriage in which there is constant humiliation, which means whatever you can understand, whatever problems can happen, you are facing them in life, they educated you, made you so big, then why did they do something and I will support you, so they got me divorced saying that if there is cancer in any part of the body, it is not necessary that we fill the whole body with cancer, that part should be cut off, so they got that part cut off, the part was cut off, now I don't understand what to do, two small children, one two years old and one four years old, what will we do with them, no problem, let's move ahead, it was just four-five months ago and my father died suddenly in Dubai, I am in Delhi, I mean my father, he is in Dubai, our mother, both my children are with my father, I was not able to understand what to do, I ran and took an overnight flight to Dubai, reached there and saw father's dead body and I was not able to understand what to do, so within 24 hours I took the children, closed the house and brought father's body back, I did not go back here for a whole year. I was taking care of my mother and my home. During this time, you can say another huge earthquake came in my life. In 1995, there were huge floods in Kullu Manali. Such huge floods that we lost everything except our house. Everything else was gone. I was at a loss as to what to do. My mother was sitting there, I was sitting there with two children. Anyway, I gathered courage and left the children with my mother and went to Dubai. Without a job, without anything, without a house, nothing. After going there, I searched for a job again. I got a very small job. I started living with someone and doing that job. Then with great difficulty, when she took me back with the children, I spent a year and a half with great difficulty. In these last few years, I started studying side by side. First I studied Vastu, then astrology, then after that I studied Feng Shui. One day, I had a friend who worked in a newspaper. I was telling her that I have studied this, such and such, all this. She said, oh this is a very good thing, this is a very wonderful thing. If they had written an article on you, then in the next week's newspaper, he wrote an article about me, two pages long, it was the year 2000, such a big article came out and overnight people started calling me and asking me how do they do it, how do they do it, tell me this, tell me that, 37 years ago there was no such thing as divorce and in my seven generations, not even in 70 generations, no one got divorced, only I got divorced and I could n't understand what wrong I did because I did everything right, I cooked, looked after the house, looked after the kids, looked after the family and because we belonged to a decent family, we respected everyone but why did this happen, it bothered me deeply because I am very logical and I don't understand that when I did nothing wrong then why was this wrong done to me, they started harassing me on the point that I had taken five masters degrees, this was just happening on the side, I was doing the same job. I was living there with great difficulty, meaning I did n't have even 50 dirhams extra to spend on anything, and 50 rums, which would be around a thousand rupees today, were a big thing for me, very big, meaning if the kids made a request, it would take me a few months to fulfill it, and even today I feel a little bad thinking about it. I taught my kids myself, and I had no support from anywhere, absolutely no support. When the kids were first going to school in Dubai, the school I put them in, because I did n't have that much money. The school I put them in had children from all grades. My younger child would come back learning that I would drive because his father was a driver, and I would work at home because his family was like that. So, they've seen so much, and because of all this, my kids have become such that they have a deep affection for everyone. My boss doesn't know what happened. Overnight he told me that we don't need you, even though I was doing a very good and important job in the office and was also making money for the company. But he said, I do n't have a place for you. You can leave next week. Now the house also belonged to the company. The children were small and I was alone. What could I do? Well, God had made a plan. It started with the factory calls. So I thought, let me do it. So what I did was, I said, yes, if you want to get the flooring done, just let me know. I will come to your house. I still remember a person who was the owner of a big company. He called me to get his office flooring done. He gave me 1000 dirhams. 1000 dirhams was a lot of money even at that time. I got it, and I was so happy that I can't even describe it. Now, I mean, my life has become a reality. That happened in one day, and after that, gradually, sometimes it started getting 200, sometimes 500. 400 rupees like this, I started getting work and when I say with great difficulty, I am right. With great difficulty, I collected 2000 rupees and with that I went to China. With great difficulty, I collected goods, little by little, and I brought it and set up a shop. Before opening the shop, our turnover in the year had become over 2 million dirhams. Imagine such a turnover in a year. When we started with literally 000 rupees, our annual turnover was 2 million dirhams. 2 million dirhams in a year, I myself could not believe it. When I looked at the numbers, I was surprised, how did this happen? What was happening? I mean, where was all this money coming from? People were calling and work was coming. So, I don't know what I thought at that time. I thought that everything is fine, the money is coming. Well, the biggest problem was the money, it came, God gave it to me. After that day, I never looked back. No, the day I understood this, I understood another thing that I am not doing it, the one who does it is sitting above, the one who takes it is sitting here, I am just a conduit, I am a medium, that is, a channel through which this work was to be done, like a doctor. Today, God has blessed me a lot, I have opened nine shops all over Dubai and have done many consultations with big people, big ministers, big people here, not to have any influence. I have never blamed anyone, I have never said to anyone that because of you, because of this, because of that. Even today, the father of my children is a very good friend of ours, isn't it surprising, and I say thank you to him because if I had not faced the situation in which I studied and if he had not put me in so many troubles, then I would not be standing here today and I would not have reached this height, so who should get the credit for all this? It goes to those who put me in this situation and to my thinking which my parents gave me. So I know sewing, knitting, embroidery, everything, I also know how to cook, I still do that and what I learned from my father's side was that never given means nothing is impossible. The word ' possible' doesn't exist in my dictionary because my father used to say that this cannot happen. If one path is closed, another will open. So look from the other. Don't say that you ca n't go here. So this is my story and I hope that all of you who are here, who are listening and watching this, understand this. Please do n't look back. Everything is ahead. Time doesn't go backwards. You too move forward and think new. Whatever you think will happen. Whatever you don't think can never happen. Isn't that right? Thank you so much. And before I leave, I thank all our Josh Talks team and Josh Talks channel for giving me this opportunity and inviting me here. Thank you very much for this. - Single Mom Life 💪 10 Brutal Lessons I Learned the Hard Way After Divorce: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Today I want to pull back the curtain&nbsp; and talk about the lessons that I've learned&nbsp;&nbsp; as a single mom. If this is something that&nbsp; you're currently going through, you're either&nbsp;&nbsp; contemplating divorce, you're right smack dab in&nbsp; the middle of a divorce, or you're post divorce,&nbsp;&nbsp; hopefully some of these lessons that I'm&nbsp; going to talk about will resonate with you,&nbsp;&nbsp; and you can either save yourself some of the grief&nbsp; that I went through, or perhaps they resonate with&nbsp;&nbsp; you and you can comment and help others that are&nbsp; going through this. My name's Tammy. I'm a divorce&nbsp;&nbsp; mortgage specialist and more importantly, a single&nbsp; mom of two young men. They're 18 and 20 now. when&nbsp;&nbsp; I got divorced 10 years ago, they were eight&nbsp; and 10. And I have learned some pretty impactful&nbsp;&nbsp; lessons. And I thought that this video would be&nbsp; helpful to others that may be going through this&nbsp;&nbsp; of just some really great life lessons that I've&nbsp; learned. One is no is a full sentence. I used to&nbsp;&nbsp; think that I could had to say no and then follow&nbsp; it up with all of these reasons why I was saying&nbsp;&nbsp; no. But now I just say no thank you. If I don't&nbsp; want to do something, I just don't do it. It's&nbsp;&nbsp; no thank you. And that is perfectly okay. You&nbsp; don't have to say no thank you and then give the&nbsp;&nbsp; 10 reasons why you're saying no thank you. So that&nbsp; was really empowering once I realized that I could&nbsp;&nbsp; just say no. Hopefully you can take that away if&nbsp; you're a people pleaser. It's a really hard um&nbsp;&nbsp; thing to do. The next one is silence is an answer.&nbsp; That one I learned about maybe five years ago. I'm&nbsp;&nbsp; 56 now. So I didn't learn it until I was in my&nbsp; 50s. And that I don't have to explain myself.&nbsp;&nbsp; I don't have to defend myself. That silence is an&nbsp; answer and it can be a very powerful answer. And&nbsp;&nbsp; if somebody is trying to get more out of you&nbsp; or if somebody is trying to create conflict,&nbsp;&nbsp; they won't like silence. They won't like the fact&nbsp; that you have not responded. But yes, silence&nbsp;&nbsp; can be an answer, too. So you don't have to do a&nbsp; really long text explanation about why somebody is&nbsp;&nbsp; wrong or somebody didn't interpret the way that&nbsp; you reacted in a certain way. You can just be&nbsp;&nbsp; silent. The next thing is you don't have to follow&nbsp; the crowd. You don't have to do things that you&nbsp;&nbsp; never really wanted to do to begin with. I used&nbsp; to do a lot of things living in a community where&nbsp;&nbsp; everybody was trying to keep up with the Joneses.&nbsp; Everybody was having these big elaborate parties&nbsp;&nbsp; for their children and everybody was having big&nbsp; parties for whatever the occasion was and we were&nbsp;&nbsp; doing the same thing and it was very expensive and&nbsp; it was very timeconuming and sometimes at the end&nbsp;&nbsp; of it we'd have these big events or big parties&nbsp; and I'd think why did I do that? I didn't even&nbsp;&nbsp; have fun. I didn't even get to enjoy myself. And&nbsp; so you don't have to do those things. You don't&nbsp;&nbsp; have to keep up with other people. You don't&nbsp; have to do activities that aren't necessarily&nbsp;&nbsp; in alignment with who you are. And it's okay. And&nbsp; when you are free from that, it's a very freeing&nbsp;&nbsp; place to be because you don't feel like you have&nbsp; to keep up with everything else that everybody&nbsp;&nbsp; else is doing. You don't have to be everything to&nbsp; everybody. It's really hard when you're divorced&nbsp;&nbsp; to try to keep up with all the expectations. Now&nbsp; you're wearing all of the hats. And so it's okay&nbsp;&nbsp; if your house is messy. When I was married, we&nbsp; had a housekeeper. I couldn't afford that when&nbsp;&nbsp; I was divorced. So I didn't have a housekeeper&nbsp; anymore. And it's okay that your house is messy&nbsp;&nbsp; and everything's not organized. And you want to&nbsp; spend some time with your children instead of&nbsp;&nbsp; spending all weekend cleaning your house because&nbsp; you work the rest of the time. All that is okay.&nbsp;&nbsp; It doesn't matter. At the end of the day, what's&nbsp; important is that you find a place that you're&nbsp;&nbsp; comfortable with and a level of um cleanliness&nbsp; that you're comfortable with and organization that&nbsp;&nbsp; you're comfortable with and that your children are&nbsp; comfortable with and that you have an environment&nbsp;&nbsp; that feels safe and feels secure and it feels&nbsp; like home to you and everything doesn't have to&nbsp;&nbsp; be in the exact right place. If somebody were&nbsp; to walk in the door and your house is messy,&nbsp;&nbsp; you don't have to feel shamed or feel shameful&nbsp; because you didn't keep up and have your house&nbsp;&nbsp; in perfect order when it when they're walking in&nbsp; the door. Your peace matters more than perfection.&nbsp;&nbsp; And sometimes that means letting go of things and&nbsp; sometimes that means not necessarily keeping the&nbsp;&nbsp; household running in the way that you might have&nbsp; been able to when you were married because there&nbsp;&nbsp; was two of you taking care of things. There was&nbsp; a divide and conquer situation going on. And one&nbsp;&nbsp; person maybe had a list of things to do that&nbsp; had nothing to do with what the other person&nbsp;&nbsp; was doing, but it took two to run a household and&nbsp; took two to make the money and make sure there was&nbsp;&nbsp; food in the refrigerator and keep the house clean&nbsp; and make sure the yards were taken care of and&nbsp;&nbsp; make sure the kids schedules were done and make&nbsp; sure the kids were doing their homework on time.&nbsp;&nbsp; There's a lot there's a lot to life in general&nbsp; and so letting go of some things is okay and that&nbsp;&nbsp; is just the way that it's going to be. financial&nbsp; clarity. Really, when you get a handle on your own&nbsp;&nbsp; personal finances, it can be very empowering.&nbsp; And once you understand your finances and you&nbsp;&nbsp; understand what the money is that's coming in&nbsp; versus the money that's going out and you can get&nbsp;&nbsp; a handle on that for yourself and understand what&nbsp; you can say yes to and what you have to say no to.&nbsp;&nbsp; Because sometimes you may want to do something.&nbsp; You may want to go on that trip. You may want to&nbsp;&nbsp; go out to dinner. You may want to do things, but&nbsp; you don't have the money for it. And that's okay.&nbsp;&nbsp; It's okay to say no. And it's okay to say I can't&nbsp; afford that or I can't go on that trip because&nbsp;&nbsp; I don't have the budget for it. And that is&nbsp; perfectly okay. And nobody is going to blame you&nbsp;&nbsp; for that or not be your friend or, you know, feel&nbsp; that you're less of a person because you cannot&nbsp;&nbsp; afford to do something. There'll be a time and a&nbsp; place you will be able to do those things. But if&nbsp;&nbsp; it means like really getting your feet underneath&nbsp; you and figuring out where your budget is and what&nbsp;&nbsp; your standards need to be, then that's okay. This&nbsp; is a really big one. A lesson that I learned from&nbsp;&nbsp; making a huge mistake was your kids are watching&nbsp; you. They're watching everything you do. They're&nbsp;&nbsp; watching the way that you co-parent with their&nbsp; other parent. They're watching the way that you&nbsp;&nbsp; respond to people out and about in the community.&nbsp; They're watching your emotions. They're watching&nbsp;&nbsp; all of the things that you do, how you're handling&nbsp; situations. And I've made some huge mistakes over&nbsp;&nbsp; time. And one that I really was uncomfortable&nbsp; with was once when I was in a traffic situation.&nbsp;&nbsp; I was in the wrong. I knew I was in the wrong and&nbsp; somebody followed me to confront me about it. I&nbsp;&nbsp; wasn't paying attention. I think I cut somebody&nbsp; off and this kid followed me and confronted me&nbsp;&nbsp; and I was afraid of what he was going to do. I&nbsp; didn't know what how I was going to handle it.&nbsp;&nbsp; My kids were young and they were in the back seat&nbsp; and I really had to get a grip on my emotional&nbsp;&nbsp; intelligence and, you know, try to be kind and&nbsp; respectful, but at the same time, I was angry,&nbsp;&nbsp; right? I was angry, I was embarrassed, I was&nbsp; all the things. And my kids were watching every&nbsp;&nbsp; step I made. And so that was something that I was&nbsp; like, "Wow, my kids are really watching me." and&nbsp;&nbsp; you know, they're watching you when you're on the&nbsp; phone with their other parents and you're they're&nbsp;&nbsp; listening for things and they're paying attention&nbsp; to your energy and they're really in tune to how&nbsp;&nbsp; things are being handled. And that is something&nbsp; where emotional intelligence really comes into&nbsp;&nbsp; play. And keeping your cool when you may not feel&nbsp; cool inside, keeping your cool on the outside can&nbsp;&nbsp; be really powerful, make a big difference for how&nbsp; your children are perceiving you and how they're&nbsp;&nbsp; perceiving. Because you got to remember that&nbsp; they're your co-parent is half of your children.&nbsp;&nbsp; So you don't want to badmouth the other person&nbsp; or talk badly about them or disrespect them just&nbsp;&nbsp; because you think that the other person isn't&nbsp; listening. Your children are listening and and&nbsp;&nbsp; they are going to feel badly about themselves if&nbsp; you're talking badly about their father or their&nbsp;&nbsp; mother or their other the other parent. So be&nbsp; very careful in those situations. You're going&nbsp;&nbsp; to grieve your old life, the life that you thought&nbsp; that you were going to have. It is going to take&nbsp;&nbsp; you time to get past the thoughts that you were&nbsp; having when you were walking down the aisle and&nbsp;&nbsp; getting married. Those that life you thought of&nbsp; and that you were trying to build is no longer an&nbsp;&nbsp; option. And it's going to take a long time for you&nbsp; to get through those emotions and get through the&nbsp;&nbsp; thought of that life that doesn't exist anymore.&nbsp; And it's okay to grieve that. And it is divorce&nbsp;&nbsp; is a grieving process. You're grieving even if you&nbsp; were the one that asked for the divorce. It still&nbsp;&nbsp; takes time to get through that and to understand&nbsp; that it's a grieving process that you have to go&nbsp;&nbsp; through. And there's five stages, I think, of&nbsp; grief. And you're going to go through every&nbsp;&nbsp; single one of them. And you have to understand and&nbsp; give yourself the space, give yourself the time,&nbsp;&nbsp; and give yourself the ability to process all of&nbsp; that emotion that you're going to go through. And&nbsp;&nbsp; that is what the div process of divorce itself is&nbsp; so broken because a lot of times people are asking&nbsp;&nbsp; you a million questions and you're having to make&nbsp; a million decisions and you're going through a&nbsp;&nbsp; grieving process. Well, if somebody dies, you&nbsp; know, a lot of times if somebody dies in your&nbsp;&nbsp; family, you're not having to make decisions.&nbsp; And a divorce is the opposite. When you're&nbsp;&nbsp; going through a divorce, you're grieving what you&nbsp; thought was going to be and you're having to make&nbsp;&nbsp; a lot of very important life decisions. And so&nbsp; you have to give yourself the space and you have&nbsp;&nbsp; to give yourself the ability to get through&nbsp; that. The next one is boundaries will save&nbsp;&nbsp; you. Boundaries are really an important lesson to&nbsp; have. I know when I first went through my divorce,&nbsp;&nbsp; there was a lot of gray area as to what boundaries&nbsp; we needed to come up with because we went through&nbsp;&nbsp; a very amicable divorce. And so we were trying&nbsp; to figure out how to reinvent our relationship.&nbsp;&nbsp; And we had to have a lot of conversation about&nbsp; boundaries such as if you're going to drop off&nbsp;&nbsp; the kids over to the other person's house,&nbsp; you can't necessarily just walk in the door&nbsp;&nbsp; and follow them into the house the way you would&nbsp; if you're married. Being respectful of somebody&nbsp;&nbsp; else's space when they don't have the children&nbsp; because they're trying to build a new life for&nbsp;&nbsp; themselves. And that was really hard and some hard&nbsp; conversations especially when somebody gets into&nbsp;&nbsp; potentially a new relationship or has or is trying&nbsp; to date or whatever. And boundaries are really&nbsp;&nbsp; important in all aspects of life. But in a divorce&nbsp; situation, sometimes those are also conversations&nbsp;&nbsp; that you need to have. And boundaries aren't&nbsp; necessarily just a given that people were going&nbsp;&nbsp; to understand where your boundaries are. And so&nbsp; being respectful and having those conversations&nbsp;&nbsp; is okay. and you can say, "Hey, look, I would&nbsp; prefer it if you gave me a heads up if you're&nbsp;&nbsp; planning on dropping off the backp packs over at&nbsp; the house on this specific day rather than just&nbsp;&nbsp; knocking at the door and expecting me to answer&nbsp; it because whatever. You may be dancing in the&nbsp;&nbsp; living room naked. Who knows? I don't know." I learned&nbsp;&nbsp; how to meditate. I learned how to clean up my food&nbsp; intake. Really understanding the foods that I was&nbsp;&nbsp; eating, what was in them, not eating processed&nbsp; foods and eliminating alcohol in my life. That was&nbsp;&nbsp; a big one. - How to Predict a Divorce with 91% Accuracy: Kind: captions Language: en There is a world-renowned marriage counselor named John Gottman. And John Gottman has been studying marriages his entire career. That's all he does is study marriages. He has a whole team of people that studies them. And he claims that he can p he can predict whether or not a couple is going to get divorced with a 91% accuracy rate within the first 10 minutes of meeting them if they show the signs of fruitless fighting. Do you want to know what those signs are? It's scientifically proven that if you show all these signs, your marriage is in trouble. Well, the first sign is what we call harsh startup. Harsh startup means we need to talk. The harsh startup is you did it again. The harsh startup is beginning the conversation already abruptly, already negatively, and already with a hostile attitude. Well, the next sign is actually four things. A Gottman calls it the four horsemen. It's defensiveness, contempt, which we've already we've already talked about, criticism, and stonewalling. Those are the four horsemen that uh together make up the next sign of divorce. Now, what is stonewalling? Stonewalling, which is is when one person in the couple is just not responding. Stonewalling is Yeah. Mhm. Okay. Yeah. Sure. maybe looking out the window. You're having a conversation, but they're not looking at you. They're not really telling you how you feel, how they feel. You don't really know what's going on inside of them. Appears like that person doesn't even care. The next sign is very much related to the the previous four. It's called flooding. Flooding is when you have so many feelings that you can't even speak them. So, they're so flooded with feelings, they can't tell you how they feel. It's not that they don't have a feeling, they've got so many they can't pick one. And flooding is very dangerous because it leads to the next sign. The next sign is physiological distress. If you've been allowing the conflict in your marriage to go on for so long, the anger to not get resolved for so long, you're going to start having physical problems. You're going to start having ulcers, backachches, necks. You're going to start drinking more. You're going to start having physiological problems. You're going to start failing from the inside. The last two signs of marriages that are in distress that leads towards divorce are bad memories and failed repair attempts. What does bad memories means? What it means is you interpret history negatively. You're only remembering the negative things from the past. It's like, "Yeah, I remember our wedding. Yeah, he was late. He was late to that. He's still late today." You forget all the good things and you remember all the bad things. You're reinterpreting history negatively. Bad memories, bad sign leading to bad things in your relationship. And the last thing is failed repair attempts. This is really critical. Everybody fights. Even good marriages fight. Fighting in a marriage is not a problem. The problem is what are you going to do about it? And the really critical thing is to be able to repair what you did. Fighting isn't a problem. not being able to repair it is. And repair attempts can be simple. It can be as simple as coming to a common understanding. It can be as simple as uh communicating that you're on the same side. But you've got to have the ability to get back on the beam. You got to have the ability to get back on track again. You got to have the ability to repair. Most marriages never really solve their problems. Yeah, I know that's sad news, but it's true. Gottman has discovered this that you will spend most of your marriage fighting about the same issues over and over and over again. You're never going to see child rearing the same way. You're never going to see your in-laws the same way. You're never going to manage money the same way. You're never going to do lots of things the same way. You'll come to a a common enough agreement to live with the difference between the two of you. But for most things that you fight about, you never do come to a common understanding of them. So why do people fight? You fight because you want to be connected. You feel disconnected and you're fighting to be reconnected. You think you're fighting about money. You think you're fighting about her parents, but you're not. You're fighting because I need to feel connected to you when I don't. And if you feel reconnected to each other, then the subject that you're fighting about doesn't become as important. In psychology, we say the thing is never the thing. And what I mean by that is you think you're fighting about something. You're not. You're fighting about whether or not we feel connected. And once we do feel connected, the thing all of a sudden doesn't seem so important.

- The Top 3 Mistakes That Dads Make in Child Custody: Kind: captions Language: en Today we're going to talk about the top three mistakes that dads make in child custody. Now yesterday we talked about moms and so obviously I don't want the moms to feel solely called out. There are things that recur on both sides. But today I'm going to focus on the things that I see repeat with my dad clients that are kind of the common mistakes that they make when they're going through their custody journey. So before we dive into this, let me just remind you if you like the content and find it helpful, don't forget to hit like on this video. Also, subscribe to the channel so that you get notified as new videos are released. And if you know another dad that is struggling with this same process like you are, feel free to share this video with him because we want to avoid these mistakes. The first thing that a new dad client will say to me, I just want what's fair. Okay, fair is a four-letter word in court. I hate to say it, but it is. And it's sad because we think of the court as the justice system, right? So, we want what's just, we want what's fair. And that's our perspective on it. But in family court, when you're dealing with child custody, fairness is not the call of action for the court. Fairness suggests between two people, right? So, you're saying between me and mom, I just want what's fair. I want us both to have what's fair for us. Here's the problem with that argument. The court's focus is not on you, mom, or the fairness. The court's focus is on the children. So, as soon as the word fair or equal or any of those things leave your mouth, what you're saying to the court, whether you realize it or not, or the or or what I what I should say is the way the court's interpreting what you're saying, whether this is what you intend to mean or not, is that your focus is on you and what's fair to you and the other parent. Okay? And you know, frequently we say things and intend them in a certain way and they're not interpreted that way by the other person. That's why we have arguments in our relationships because we have breakdowns around what the meaning of things is. And so it's real important for you to understand when you're in front of that judge and you're saying certain things, you're conveying underlying messages that you may not be intending to convey because of the way that they're interpreting it. And fairness is just one of those words. The way the court tends to look at this issue is what parenting schedule, not percentage of time, not equal, not fair. What parenting schedule is in your child's best interest and why? And so I have other videos where I talk about common parenting schedules. There's everything from every other weekend and a weekday overnight. There's 223, 2255, week on week off. I'm not going to dive deep into what all those mean, but you can find that information in other videos on the channel, but that's the terms that the court thinks of it in. If you say week on week off is best for my child because they're a little bit older. They can be away from each parent for that length of time, it gives us the ability to establish household routines in our homes. It gives us the ability to fully participate in all aspects of parenting. um it creates a lot of stability for the child because there's not a lot of exchanges, right? And you have to choose plans that are age appropriate like a week on week off. The court will generally want the child to be at least 10 most of the time before we're we're looking at that kind of thing. Parents can agree on that sooner, but if it's up to the court, they're usually not going to do it before that age. But when I'm talking about it in terms of that schedule, see, I'm not saying, well, I want week on week off because it's fair, because it's equal. Because again, those things go back to how it impacts you and mom. That's not the question. The question is, what does it do for the kid? That's where you want to put your argument, okay? Not on fairness or equality. The second thing that I see is being too aggressive or too assertive. Okay? Now, let me just say that my late husband used to I used to tell him he was passive aggressive sometimes and he would say, "Yeah, well, you're aggressive aggressive." And so, I'm aggressive aggressive. Okay, you guys can probably tell I have a pretty strong personality. The way you see me on the video, my friends, my family, people will tell you this is me. This is who I am. I don't sugarcoat things. I'm pretty straightforward. But we have to remember that when we're coming into this system, there is some male female dynamic there that I think is often held against dads in my experience. And you know, I don't want to say that the system is inherently biased against dads. I think it's getting better. I'm in California. Obviously, we're a 50/50 state. We're much more progressive than other states. I understand that. I grew up in Kentucky back east, not so progressive, more mom focused, right? So, I get that there are still inherent biases in a lot of um jurisdictions in kind of like those male female roles. And I think particularly with younger children um again I think we're getting better. I think we're getting more aware and we're more appreciative and and more um understanding of how important dad's role is for a child. And you know, I think children need both parents. They need moms and dads ideally. Sometimes you have safety issues and reasons that can't happen. But on the whole, all things being equal, children need both parents. And but I think what happens is dads that particularly if you're the kind of dad that's very sort of in his masculine and very intense maybe is a word that I like to use to describe my my dad clients sometimes. and they just kind of present in that very matter-of-act kind of boom boom this is it kind of way which again I love and I love that whole masculine thing and I appreciate it but we're in a system where we're kind of in this day and age being taught gentle parenting and all these types of things. And so when you come across in sort of that very intense kind of dominating way, especially if you have somebody on the other side that's accusing you of being controlling and all those types of, you know, abusive, verbally abusive, all of these things, you want to dial back that intensity. And one of the key ways that I tell my dads to do this is to when you're sitting in front of an expert like a therapist or a custody evaluator or a parenting coordinator or, you know, attorney for the children or whatever that expert is. One of the ways that you can do this is by letting that person have control of the conversation, right? just sort of not trying to go down the list of all the things you want to tell them, but sort of dialing back and when they say, you know, what is your main concern about mom's parenting? I feel like um mom is a little overprotective and I'm concerned that that may cause the child to not develop the level of independence that they really need to be able to be, you know, uh to stand on their own two feet in the world, right? Let's and so and so I gave the reason right but I also gave what how that affects the child which is very very important and I'm not just saying oh mom's too coddling she babies them she you know I'm not making it about mom I'm saying well mom can be overprotective and here's my concern of how that might um impact the child and then you just stop right and you don't say anything else and you wait and that person nine times out of 10 when I have people do this. That expert comes back and says, "Can you give me an example of that?" And then you go, "Well, sure." Uh, when I take the child to the swimming pool and they get close to the edge, a mom starts screaming for them to be, you know, for me to grab them or pull them back or whatever and I encourage them to jump off the edge and into my arms so that they learn to take calculated risk and they learn to overcome their fears and they learn to trust that I'm going to catch them. and it improves our bond and our relationship. You know, again, I'm giving child focused reasons for why for what my example is. But I don't give that example right away. I let the other person have that control and let them ask for that example. Just by way of the fact that that other person has to kind of pull that out of you and ask for the example sort of creates the dynamic of you not being like, "Here's all the things mom does, right? Let me just go down the list." And it and it dials back that intensity, right? Because you're just relaxing. You're letting that other person have control and that type of thing. So then when mom comes in and she's like, "Oh, he's controlling. He's this. He's that." Then that expert is going, "Huh? You know, that wasn't my experience of this person at all." Okay? So just being aware of that. And I'm not saying again, not all dads have that, right? But if you're kind of a a you know a man's man and you really kind of stand in that masculine again, which I love. I think is great. I'm not complaining about it from a woman perspective. I'm just telling you from a court perspective, that is something you have to be aware of and try to dial back. You have to know your audience. You have to know who you're talking to. That's so important. Let's say you you sit down and you have minors counsel and it is a man and it is somebody that's very masculine like you and all that, they can probably handle your level of intensity, right? But if you have a young female newly out of school, just learning about therapy, whatever, she's not going to be able to handle it as well. She's going to see you as overpowering, intense, all those things. So knowing your audience ahead of time and knowing who you're dealing with is important. The third thing is kind of tied in with the second thing, but pushing your agenda. Men are very problem-solving, right? It's one of the things that we love about you is that we got a problem, we can come. You are all about coming up with all the different ways that we can solve that problem. And that particular aspect is good when you're dealing with this and you can throw out multiple options of, hey, let's brainstorm. How could we solve this problem? But when you have the list of, okay, here's the problem, here's the solution, here's this problem, here's the solution, here's this problem, here's the solution, and then you're like, okay, we need to move. We need to get this done and push all these things through. Then you're trying to push your agenda, right? And a lot of times the harder we try to push the agenda, the more it backfires on us. Again, I find if you can kind of sit back and let things unfold, then eventually if mom is crazy and difficult, you know, I see all kind borderline bipolar, narcissistic, whatever it is, if you're dealing with a crazy chick, okay, on the other side, the best way to show that is to step back and let her be crazy. Let the spotlight shine on that crazy. And when you're in there and you're pushing your your agenda and you're trying to make things happen, then it becomes well both of these people are engaging in the dance of conflict and then both of you look bad. And so if again stepping back, not having that need to push your agenda and just letting things unfold in the natural course. I know it's hard to wait because you just you want things to happen now. You want time with your kids now. You want the divorce over now. You want to be done with all this now. But I am telling you, every time I see a client try to push things through and do that, normally they come out with the the short end of the stick. And if you if you can just sit in the uncertainty and let things unfold, then normally what will happen is over time different incidences will happen that will show that mom's interfering in your parenting or that she's withholding the child or whatever the things are that are happening. You know, there's a great quote from Tony Robbins where he says, "The quality of your life is in proportion to the amount of uncertainty that you can reasonably tolerate." And every year that I live, I find that to be more and more true. Learning to just let things happen and trust that it the process is going to do what it should do and figure it out on the other side. But it's that fear that the experts or the court or whoever won't figure it out that drive us to push that agenda. Just state what you're observing. You know, the behaviors, the actions, things you can witness with your five senses. The person said this, did this for her and the kids, whatever that is. and then let these experts piece together the reasoning of why it's happening, what the agenda is of mom, how it's impacting the kids, all of those things. That's the expert's job to piece all that together. Your job is to just give input on what you're observing in this process. I hope that's helpful to you. There, of course, these are not the only three. There is all kinds of things that recur that I see that are the mistakes that dads make. So, if you'd like more help sorting through those and maybe deciphering what your tendency is and which mistakes you're making, you can go to divorceuniversityonline.com/vip-coaching. There's information on that page on my group coaching programs for for dads, for moms as well, but you probably don't need that if you're watching this video. And also information on my premium VIP one-on-one coaching services. You know, if you're just in up to your eyeballs, you're at the very beginning of your case, you've got DVs, custody evals, all kinds of craziness going on, then those premium services are probably something that would be very, very supportive of you. So, thanks for joining me. I will see you guys next time. See the horizon turn us to thousands.

- What Representing Men in Divorce Taught Me About Fatherhood | Marilyn York | TEDxUniversityofNevada: Kind: captions Language: en Transcriber: Morgane Quilfen Reviewer: Tanya Cushman I'm six years old, and all I can think about is getting the pink Barbie Corvette! I need five more dollars. Luckily for me, it's Easter, and I know that my dad always hides one coveted five-dollar egg. I also know the best egg is the hardest to find. This year, I'm ready! Before long, I spot it, right in the middle of my sweet '70s swing set pole! You know, the one that runs along the entire top of the set. I scramble to get the ladder and the yardstick and duct tape it to a broom handle. I fish it into the pipe, and I shove at it hard. It flies out the other side, and by the time it hits the ground, I'm waiting above it like an expectant father. The egg cracks open and inside ... is the very opposite of my grand prize. Instead, a perfectly formed dog turd rolls out. (Laughter) I burst into loud hysterics. At the same time, my father explodes with laughter. I run as fast as I can to my room, but he's not far behind. It's time for one of his talks. "Honey, it was clear that you already learned the important life lesson: the harder you work, the better the payoff. So it was time that you learned another valuable lesson: sometimes, no matter how hard you work, you just end up with shit!" And who better to teach just this sort of hard-hitting, direct, and painful life lesson to six-year-old me than my father? I'm a divorce attorney. I've been practicing for over 20 years. I began in Los Angeles, but I've owned my firm in Nevada since 2001. My firm has a particular sub-specialty: men's rights. My nine female employees and I specifically represent men in divorce and custody battles. And guess who runs the business end of my law firm? My father. In my practice, we've represented over 2,000 men, 650 of whom are fathers. My expertise not only comes from my career but just as much from my personal life. I'm a mother. My children are 23, 15, 12, and barely 3. They come from two different mothers and three different fathers. Hooker! Let me explain. I helped my ex-husband raise our 23-year-old son, whom I didn't birth, from age 5. I share custody of my 15-year-old daughter with the same father. My 12-year-old son sadly lost his father to suicide when he was just 7. And my 3-year-old is being raised at home with his father and me. I literally live my work every day! So, what has 20 years of representing men in family law while living my own reality show as a wife, mother, and daughter taught me about fatherhood? Allow me, if you will, to start with the second thing I learned about fatherhood: men parent differently than women. Big surprise! But their influence is crucial in the development of their children. Do you know any mom in the world who would put dog shit in an Easter egg? Okay, maybe that's a good thing. Let me better demonstrate this point from my legal experience. When getting my father clients ready for court, I prepare them for this kind of interrogation: "Who's your children's doctor or dentist?" "What's the name of your school principal or even their teacher?" "What grades did they get on their last report card?" Nine times out of ten, they miss the majority of these questions. Seriously. Does this mean they don't care or love their children as much? I bet it makes you wonder. But please, hold your judgement. Here are the questions that my father clients can easily answer: "If your son could be a superhero, what would his power be?" "What kind of monsters do your kids fear?" "How high does your daughter feel comfortable flying in a swing?" "What makes your son feel defeated?" Yet, in my experience cross-examining hundreds of mothers in family court, these are the harder questions for them. Most of us know, motherhood brings with it a sixth sense and an unspoken bond to our children. But what about fathers? Even fathers feel insecure about this reality. After representing 650 fathers, I can count on one hand those that felt secure in their instinctive role and significance to their children. What's interesting is my anecdotal legal experience suggests otherwise. In 20 years of practice, I've had over 100 men take a paternity test - like Jerry Springer. Do you know how many were wrong in predicting their biological relation to the child? Two. This shocked me and taught me my third lesson: fathers, too, have a genetic bond and instinct about their children from infancy. Forget where you are for a moment, seriously, and close your eyes. I'd like to ask you to feel. Think about your childhood. Picture your father, his smiling eyes, his strong hands. Hear his deep voice. What did you love about your dad? Did he throw you high into the air? Teach you to ride a bike? Carry you home when you got hurt? Push you when you tried to give up? What did it feel like to have a daddy? Secure? Fun? Challenging? Now, go back in your memories and erase your father from every scene. This is what the other 40 percent of people's childhoods look like. Just under half the people listening to me, including my own 12-year-old son, felt sad, angry, or blank during the peak of your joy, while playing along. Eye-opening, isn't it? Nearly two of every five children in America are growing up without their fathers, or 17 million, according to the 2016 census. Other sources estimate as many as 30 million. In 2011, I joined the board for a local charity called Nevada Youth Empowerment Project, or NYEP. NYEP is a housing program for homeless girls ages 18 to 24. As board president of this small charity, I've been closely involved and gotten to know the girls and their tragic stories over the years. Hundreds of otherwise homeless girls have come to our program. Their backgrounds and what they have endured would haunt you. Do you know the one thing all of these girls have in common? They all come from fatherless homes. Sadly, these girls aren't the exception; they're the rule. My fourth lesson about fatherhood came from the data. According to the Center for Disease Control, children from fatherless homes account for 90 percent of all homeless and runaway kids, 71 percent of high school dropouts, and 63 percent of youth suicides. While you listen to me speak, you have to be wondering, "What makes fathers so crucial?" Honestly, the answer is complex and better explored by psychologists. What I can tell you is that the data unequivocally tells us fathers are vital and yet laws and society undervalue their importance, making it harder for them to be in their children's lives. Even fathers underrate their own value. I know this data upsets a lot of mothers, me included. But advocating for fathers isn't about diminishing mothers. While children deserve both parents whenever possible, this crisis is specific to fathers. The occurrence of fatherlessness is epidemic, the effects are catastrophic, and the causes are male gender specific. Nearly 30 years ago, leading child psychologist Michael Lamb reminded us: "Fathers are the forgotten contributors to child development." Yet, researchers have found that children with involved fathers have stronger cognitive and motor skills, elevated physical and mental health, become better problem solvers, and are more confident, curious, and empathetic. Sadly, we've had this data for 30 years, and fatherlessness has only continued to rise during this time. The main contributors to fatherlessness are divorce and out-of-wedlock births. Every 13 seconds, someone in America gets divorced. That equates to almost 2.5 million divorces a year. Lucky me! Currently, more than 40 percent, or 1.5 million babies, are born out of wedlock each year in the US. And this brings me back to the first and most significant thing that my career has taught me about fatherhood: family court is one of the critical places where fathers are disadvantaged, and this hurts kids. Historically, this maternal preference was solidified in the tender years doctrine, which mandated custody of children under age four be awarded to mothers. This doctrine was in use until the 80's. As the laws progressed, visitation for fathers improved, but it took a lot of years before the law was finally gender equal. In fact, it wasn't until 2017 that Nevada finally adopted a presumption for joint physical custody. When I began my practice, and until 10 years ago, the best my father clients could expect was every-other-weekend visitation and maybe a dinner on the off week. While significant legal progress has been made, this long-standing bias against fathers still occurs in the enforcement of custody orders, in child support rulings, and it exists in paternity laws. All the while, the number of kids growing up without dads continues to rise. Between 1960 and 2016, the percentage of children growing up with just mothers nearly tripled, from 8 to 23 percent. Paternity laws desperately need more reform to protect the 40 percent of children born out of wedlock each year in the US. Right now, once custody has been ordered, it's illegal to remove a child from their father - usually a felony. But it's perfectly legal, in all 50 states, for a woman to conceal her pregnancy, leave the father's name off the birth certificate, and never tell him he has a child, ever! How is this not kidnapping? Just as horrible, a woman can knowingly list the wrong father in a child's birth certificate, deceive him, and a short while later, in many states, the wrong man becomes that child's legal father forever. He's obligated to a child that isn't his. And that child just lost their real father with little to no recourse. This is a betrayal of the worst kind. And the law not only allows it, it creates the opportunity. This is what we know. Every bit of data we have tells us children need their fathers! The law, its application, and society at large disfavor fathers. The law is improving, but the statistics are not. So, what can you do? We are the change makers, all of us. If you're a father, make the effort, do everything you can to be in your children's daily lives. If you're a mother, encourage and facilitate the relationship between your children and their father instead of trying to interfere or control it. If you're a child, spend time with your dad, ask him to do something, seek his advice and guidance. If you're an employer, grant the fathers you employ the ability to be at their children's events, to help in their schools, to take sick days to care for their kids. If you work in the legal field, help us continue to progress, change the laws, and ensure that they're enforced to protect fathers and their children. The importance of this pursuit cannot be overstated. The fate of nearly half of America's children depends on it. I'd like to close by asking all of you to do one final thing. Please, stand if you are able or raise your hand - I'm serious, please - if you grew up without a father, if you raised or are raising a child without a father, or if you are a father who's been separated from your child. Now, look around: the people really affected by fatherlessness. Really, look. Those of you standing and raising your hands aren't numbers. You're real living and feeling humans. You're the children scarred by fatherlessness. Now let me tell you who can't stand. The 1,000 fatherless children who were murdered last year. The 3,000 fatherless children who died from drugs. The 3,200 fatherless children who committed suicide last year. And the 14,000 fatherless children who were incarcerated. Everyone, please, stand for them! And do everything you can to help the remaining 17 million fatherless children avoid these fates. Thank you. (Applause and cheers)

I know the statistics of father abandoning their children is the bigger issue and that cannot be solved by what this lady is saying. I think it's a bit misleading to say all those fatherless people that died are because of the system putting down fathers when the truth is most of those fatherless people died due to the father going to get the milk and not coming back. This is biology and this is how men are they always have to be on the go. AGAIN I know there system is against fathers I have experienced that through the lens of an old friend. Her father was great and he raised them only to loose custody. However this is not as common. We have to look at the whole picture and I don't like painting the picture as only the system being against them when The system mirrors how the people are and the reality is fathers tend to leave while mothers are left with the kids. Maybe the real issue is fixing the psychology of men and educating men better. I really don't know what to do about that but lets gather a bigger picture not just look at it from one angle that ISN'T EVEN THE MAJORITY OF CASES.

Nonetheless I support the notion of providing an equal opportunity for father's being a part of their kids lives and it's unfortunate with the case of my friends father that he lost his kids after raising them BUT STATISTICALLY SPEAKING (MAJORITY OF CASES) the reason why those fatherless people/children/teens have died is because their fathers left them. I won't enable painting a picture that isn't the driving force for why these people die.

- 5 Tips for EVERY Dad fighting for Custody in Court: Kind: captions Language: en You've probably heard that Fathers can&nbsp; ONLY get custody if the child's Mother&nbsp;&nbsp; is absent or a drug addict, right? WRONG!&nbsp; I'm sick and tired of Fathers giving up&nbsp;&nbsp; on custody because they just presume&nbsp; that Mothers are always going to win.&nbsp; Here's the ONE question that the Courts care&nbsp; about when it comes to awarding custody:&nbsp;&nbsp; What is in the best interest of your child?&nbsp; Contrary to popular belief, winning the&nbsp;&nbsp; custody battle doesn't require slinging mud at&nbsp; your child's mother. Remember, blowing out her&nbsp;&nbsp; candle doesn't make your candle burn any brighter. Let me explain how you can prove that YOU are in&nbsp;&nbsp; fact the parent who should get custody because&nbsp; it is in the best interest of your child. If you want to win the custody battle,&nbsp; you're going to have to prove that you're&nbsp;&nbsp; actively involved in your child's life. One thing that attorneys LOVE to do when&nbsp;&nbsp; Fathers are seeking custody is to interrogate&nbsp; you on the stand with rapid-fire questions&nbsp;&nbsp; about all of the teachers, doctors,&nbsp; and counselors in their child's life.&nbsp; "Who is your child's Dentist?" "Who is your child's homeroom teacher?"&nbsp; "How about Primary Care Provider?" "Who is your child's best friend?"&nbsp; "Who is your child's bully?" For Fathers who are heavily involved&nbsp;&nbsp; in their child's day-to-day care, they ace this&nbsp; pop-quiz and leave the attorney looking like a&nbsp;&nbsp; jerk. However, this round of questioning becomes&nbsp; excruciating, and the silence becomes deafening,&nbsp;&nbsp; for Fathers who are seeking custody but&nbsp; can't answer these simple questions.&nbsp; Not only do you need to know who these&nbsp; professionals are in your child's life,&nbsp;&nbsp; but you should also be prepared&nbsp; to answer questions about the&nbsp;&nbsp; last time that you spoke to each of&nbsp; them as well as the topics discussed. In your custody battle, demonstrating your&nbsp; ability to provide a safe, stable, and supportive&nbsp;&nbsp; environment is crucial in Court. This involves&nbsp; showcasing not just the physical safety of your&nbsp;&nbsp; home, but also the emotional and developmental&nbsp; support essential for your child's well-being.&nbsp; Start by ensuring your living space is conducive&nbsp; to your child's needs. This means a clean,&nbsp;&nbsp; well-maintained home in a safe neighborhood.&nbsp; Your child should have adequate personal space,&nbsp;&nbsp; such as their own bedroom, to provide&nbsp; comfort and security. It's also important&nbsp;&nbsp; to have safety measures in place, like&nbsp; working smoke detectors, secure locks, and&nbsp;&nbsp; childproofing if necessary, to prevent accidents. Document your physical safety and stability with&nbsp;&nbsp; pictures of all of those items in your home,&nbsp; your living spaces, and your child's bedroom.&nbsp; A supportive environment goes beyond&nbsp; physical safety; it also encompasses&nbsp;&nbsp; emotional and psychological well-being. As a&nbsp; father, you should foster open communication,&nbsp;&nbsp; allowing your child to express their feelings&nbsp; and concerns freely. Be an active listener&nbsp;&nbsp; and validate your child's emotions by offering&nbsp; reassurance and guidance. Consistent routines and&nbsp;&nbsp; discipline, balanced with love and understanding,&nbsp; contribute to a sense of security and belonging.&nbsp; Document your emotionally supportive environment&nbsp; by saving the chore list your child keeps. Also,&nbsp;&nbsp; keep a journal of the conversations&nbsp; that you have with your child where you&nbsp;&nbsp; discuss fears and provide your fatherly advice. Engage in your child's education by participating&nbsp;&nbsp; in school activities, monitoring their academic&nbsp; progress, and providing a quiet, designated space&nbsp;&nbsp; for homework and study. Encourage extracurricular&nbsp; activities that align with their interests,&nbsp;&nbsp; fostering social skills and personal development. Document your educational and developmental&nbsp;&nbsp; support by showing the emails between you and&nbsp; your child's teacher, pictures of you and your&nbsp;&nbsp; child at extracurricular activities, and ask&nbsp; the other parents of your child's friends if&nbsp;&nbsp; they would testify about your involvement. Prioritize your child's physical health by&nbsp;&nbsp; providing nutritious meals, encouraging regular&nbsp; physical activity, and ensuring timely medical&nbsp;&nbsp; care. Mental health is equally important, so&nbsp; be attentive to signs of stress or emotional&nbsp;&nbsp; distress and seek professional help if needed. Document your healthy and nutritious environment&nbsp;&nbsp; with pictures of your stocked&nbsp; fridge and cabinets and emails&nbsp;&nbsp; between your and your child's pediatrician. Maintain a positive environment that encourages&nbsp;&nbsp; relationships with family and friends, which&nbsp; provides a sense of community and belonging.&nbsp;&nbsp; Remember, all of the branches of you and your&nbsp; Ex's family trees converge on your child. Just&nbsp;&nbsp; because your relationship has ended doesn't&nbsp; mean that they aren't family anymore. Extend&nbsp;&nbsp; an olive branch to your Ex's parents to set up&nbsp; visits, video calls, and coordinate holidays.&nbsp; Document the social and family connections in&nbsp; your home by showing pictures of your child&nbsp;&nbsp; with extended family, even those&nbsp; that you don't especially like.&nbsp; Children are expensive (even with child&nbsp; support) so you need to show that you&nbsp;&nbsp; have financial stability and are able to meet&nbsp; your child's needs. You don't have to have a&nbsp;&nbsp; lot of money or have large savings. You just&nbsp; need to show that you have steady employment,&nbsp;&nbsp; flexibility to attend your child's appointments,&nbsp; and enough income to cover daily living expenses.&nbsp; Document your financial stability by showing&nbsp; your monthly budget for the last year, your&nbsp;&nbsp; consistent paychecks, and demonstrating that you&nbsp; have the financial means to care for your child.&nbsp; This is not an exhaustive list of the ways&nbsp; that you can show that you are fully committed&nbsp;&nbsp; to your child's overall well-being and&nbsp; best interests. What are some of the&nbsp;&nbsp; ways that you've seen fathers demonstrate their&nbsp; ability to act in their child's best interest? I can't tell you how many times I've told&nbsp; my clients the value of co-parenting and&nbsp;&nbsp; communicating, just for them to respond&nbsp; with, "There's no way I can communicate&nbsp;&nbsp; or co-parent with MY Ex, she's crazy!" Here's&nbsp; the thing though, even if she isn't willing to&nbsp;&nbsp; co-parent and communicate with you, that&nbsp; doesn't stop you from making the effort.&nbsp; It is really simple if you just take a&nbsp; little time. Here's what that looks like.&nbsp; Any time there is a decision to be made for&nbsp; the child, like administering medication,&nbsp;&nbsp; making medical appointments, or even discipline,&nbsp; make a concerted effort to reach out to your Ex to&nbsp;&nbsp; tell them your plan and to ask for their input. It really doesn't have to be complicated,&nbsp;&nbsp; but it does have to be frequent. Here's an example: Your daughter starts&nbsp;&nbsp; running a mild fever. You send a text message&nbsp; to her Mother saying, "Hey, she's running a&nbsp;&nbsp; mild fever so I have a cold rag on her forehead.&nbsp; I'm about to give her some Motrin, but wanted to&nbsp;&nbsp; check with you first to see if you've seen any&nbsp; allergic reaction to it that I should know about." That is the perfect formula&nbsp; for co-parenting text messages:&nbsp; Issue plus Proposed Plan plus&nbsp; Seeking Input equals Co-Parenting You don't have to do what they say, but you should&nbsp; at least consider it and then thank them for&nbsp;&nbsp; their input. Even if your child's other parent&nbsp; doesn't respond, I can't tell you how valuable&nbsp;&nbsp; those text messages are in trial because now YOU&nbsp; are the peacemaker, communicator, and co-parent. "Intellectual growth should commence&nbsp; at birth and cease only at death."&nbsp; If you want to win the custody battle, you're&nbsp; going to have to demonstrate that you have&nbsp;&nbsp; great parenting skills. Obviously, a lot of&nbsp; your parenting skills can be seen from your&nbsp;&nbsp; actions, but what if you haven't had as many&nbsp; opportunities to act as the primary caregiver?&nbsp; Military fathers who have spent a lot&nbsp; of time away, fathers of newborns,&nbsp;&nbsp; and fathers who have been intentionally kept away&nbsp; from their children by a spiteful ex may not have&nbsp;&nbsp; much of a track record to demonstrate their&nbsp; parenting skills. Active pursuit of parenting&nbsp;&nbsp; classes can overcome some of this. Also,&nbsp; pursuit of parenting classes shows that you're&nbsp;&nbsp; devoted to improving your parenting skills. Some states require parents to take a Parent&nbsp;&nbsp; Education and Family Stabilization class, but&nbsp; that isn't enough. You should seek out additional&nbsp;&nbsp; classes to go the extra mile. For example: Anger Management is helpful for learning&nbsp;&nbsp; conflict resolution. And taking this class&nbsp; isn't an admission that you have an anger issue.&nbsp; Parenting Skills Course is helpful for learning&nbsp; recent trends in parenting and child development.&nbsp; First Aid Training Class is an&nbsp; often-overlooked class. Knowing&nbsp;&nbsp; how to treat your child's physical injuries&nbsp; is obviously an important part of parenthood.&nbsp; These are just a few classes, but the sky is the&nbsp; limit. Here's a link for some additional courses&nbsp;&nbsp; that we've found helpful, but make sure that you&nbsp; take notes so that way you can explain to the&nbsp;&nbsp; Judge what you learned in each of these classes&nbsp; and how they have made you a better parent. Winning custody of your child is a difficult&nbsp; task. It isn't difficult because you're a man,&nbsp;&nbsp; but because there are a lot of rules&nbsp; of civil procedure, rules of evidence,&nbsp;&nbsp; and statutes that you must know at the drop of a&nbsp; hat in Court. Also, all of the evidence that we&nbsp;&nbsp; just now discussed, you'll need to have prepared&nbsp; correctly so that way it is admissible in trial.&nbsp; Rather than making this video any longer than&nbsp; it already is, you can learn more about How&nbsp;&nbsp; to Pick the Right Attorney, and then check&nbsp; out this Playlist of courtroom procedures&nbsp;&nbsp; and techniques with body language tips, guidance&nbsp; for testimony, and even how to dress for Court.

- How Much Should Rental Property Maintenance Reserves Be? - Home Investing Experts: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] How much should rental property maintenance reserves be? Imagine waking up one morning to find your rental property has a leaking roof or a broken water heater. Unexpected repairs can hit hard if you're not prepared. That's why setting aside maintenance reserves is a smart move for any real estate investor. These funds are meant to cover ongoing and surprise repair costs, keeping your property in top shape and your cash flow steady. But how much money should you actually reserve? That depends on several factors. A common rule of thumb is to allocate between 5% and 15% of your gross rental income each year. For example, if your property earns $60,000 annually in rent, setting aside between $3,000 and $9,000 makes sense. This range covers routine repairs, small replacements, and unexpected issues that pop up. If your property is older or has more wear and tear, you might want to lean toward the higher end of this range. Conversely, newer properties or those recently renovated might only need reserves closer to 5% to 10%. Some investors prefer a different approach. They might set aside a fixed dollar amount per property, like $250 to $500, to simplify budgeting. Others reserve enough to cover 3 to 6 months of fixed expenses, including mortgage payments, property taxes, insurance, and management fees. This helps cover periods when the property might be vacant or tenants are moving out. Another method involves estimating large future expenses. List major appliances and systems such as the heating, ventilation, air conditioning system, roof, or water heater. Calculate their remaining lifespan and replacement costs. Then save accordingly. This way, you're prepared for big expenses without draining your cash reserves. From a practical point of view, keeping these reserves in a separate account is best. It prevents accidental spending on daily operations and keeps your financial picture clear. Maintaining enough reserves protects your investments value, avoids cash flow interruptions, and keeps tenants happy because repairs are done promptly. In the end, the exact amount varies based on your property and comfort level. Starting with about 10% of your annual rental income is a good baseline. Adjust this amount based on the age, condition, and location of your property. This approach balances being ready for maintenance costs without tying up too much capital. Remember, being prepared with proper reserves is a key part of smart real estate investing.

- What Routine Maintenance Counts As An Operating Expense For Landlords? - Rental Property Gurus: Kind: captions Language: en What routine maintenance counts as an operating expense for landlords? Imagine keeping your rental property in top shape without breaking the bank. That's where understanding what counts as routine maintenance for operating expenses comes in. When you manage rental properties, you need to know which costs are part of your regular expenses. These are the tasks that keep the property safe, functional, and appealing for tenants. Routine maintenance includes things like cleaning common areas such as lobbies, hallways, stairwells, and parking lots. You might hire janitorial services or schedule regular cleaning. Landscaping is another key part like mowing lawns, trimming bushes, removing snow, and maintaining outdoor lighting. These tasks are ongoing and necessary to keep the property looking good and functioning well. You also need to keep building systems in good shape. This means fixing minor plumbing leaks, replacing filters in heating, ventilation, and air conditioning systems, and servicing electrical equipment. Regular checks and repairs help prevent bigger problems later on. Landlords also maintain appliances they provide, like refrigerators, stoves, ovens, and washing machines. Servicing these appliances regularly extends their lifespan and keeps tenants happy. Pest control is another expense that counts as routine maintenance. Scheduling periodic pest treatments prevents infestations and promotes a healthy living environment. Security system maintenance such as testing alarms, replacing batteries, and checking cameras and locks is also part of routine upkeep. Small repairs like patching drywall, fixing loose railings, adjusting doors, or replacing light bulbs are included, too. Shared amenities like laundry rooms, fitness centers, and community rooms need regular cleaning and maintenance. Smoke detectors and carbon monoxide alarms should be inspected and serviced to ensure they work properly and meet safety codes. Plumbing maintenance, including drain cleaning and fixing leaks, helps prevent backups. Roof and gutter cleaning are necessary to avoid water damage and ensure proper drainage. Touch-up painting and surface repairs also fall under routine maintenance. It's important to remember that these tasks are different from major upgrades or capital improvements. Operating expenses are recurring costs needed to keep the property running smoothly. Tracking and budgeting for these expenses helps landlords plan financially, avoid costly repairs, and keep tenants satisfied. Regular maintenance not only saves money in the long run, but also helps maintain the property's value over time. [music]

- Can I Rent To Family With Section 8? - America First Democrats: Kind: captions Language: en Can I rent a family with section 8? Have you ever wondered if you can rent to family members if you are part of the section 8 housing program? This is a common question and it is important to understand the rules surrounding this topic. section, also known as the housing choice voucher program, is designed to assist lowincome families, seniors, and individuals with disabilities in finding affordable housing. Local public housing agencies manage this program, paying a portion of the rent directly to landlords, while tenants pay the remaining amount based on their income. When it comes to renting to family members under section 8, the general rule is that tenants cannot rent from close relatives. This includes parents, children, siblings, grandparents, grandchildren, and spouses. The reason for this restriction is to prevent any misuse of the program and to ensure that all participants have fair access to housing resources. Both the landlord and tenant must confirm that no familial relationship exists. Providing false information can lead to serious consequences, including legal issues and loss of benefits. However, there are exceptions to this rule, especially for tenants with disabilities. If a tenant needs special accommodations that are not available elsewhere, such as wheelchair accessibility or living close to a family caregiver, the housing authority may allow renting from a family member. This flexibility shows a commitment to supporting vulnerable individuals in suitable living situations. Another important point is that renting through entities like trusts or limited liability companies can also be permissible. If the landlord is a trust rather than an individual relative, renting to family members may be allowed since the trust is not considered a family member. Additionally, renting from family, friends, or more distant relatives who do not fall under the definition of immediate family is generally acceptable from a progressive and democratic viewpoint that values social justice and racial equity. These rules aim to maintain the integrity of the section 8 program while ensuring vulnerable populations have access to safe and affordable housing. Allowing exceptions for disabled tenants reflects a commitment to inclusivity. At the same time, preventing rentals from close family members helps protect public resources and promotes equitable access for all eligible families. In summary, renting from immediate family members under section, it is usually not allowed to prevent abuse of the program. Exceptions exist for disabled tenants needing special accommodations. Renting through trusts or entities may be allowed and renting from non-family landlords, including family friends, is permitted. These rules support fairness and access to housing for lowincome and vulnerable populations, aligning with democratic values of equity and justice.

- Hindu Undivided Family (HUF): Legal Issues & Other Challenges For Formation & Dissolution Explained: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] in our last video we introduced you to the concept of the hindu undivided family and why it exists we also listed the many benefits that come with an huf structure but today we explore the flip side some practical hurdles which may arise with the formation and dissolution of an huf let's take a look at some of these number one transfer of property one of the greatest disadvantages clothed as an advantage of the structure is that all members have a right to the properties and other assets of an huf such joint assets cannot be sold without the consent of all the members of the huf this means that every time one buys a property in the name of the huf it will belong to every member of the huf and not just to the person purchasing it this rule creates several issues where the family grows and expands into multiple branches which may also lead to disputes among members as a member owes his or her right to an huf by birth he or she cannot bequeath their share to anyone also a member by marriage is only entitled to maintenance and gets no share of the huf any individual born in an huf automatically becomes a co-parsoner second if the huf is the owner of a property the asset can only be alienated or sold in certain circumstances this can be either by the consent of all co-partners of the huf or by the karta who is typically the eldest member to meet a legal necessity or for the benefit of the huf in which case consent of all the co-partners is not required yet a co-partner can challenge such a decision the property can also be alienated or sold by disposing of the assets of the huf in case of a sole surviving co-parsoner if a minor child is involved in the huf permission of a court is required to create any third party rights to any of the assets of an so when a property is held in the name of an huf one has to be mindful of the restrictions on its transfer and on the tax implications arising out of it number three there's no universal recognition an hof is not recognized in any other country as it's a purely indian concept income assessment can therefore become a challenge for members who are moving abroad or obtaining citizenship of other countries also an huf cannot get private equity funding for a business conducted by it an huf may need to be dissolved in case of death of members or in case of a partition between the members once an hof is closed and dissolved its assets and properties need to be distributed among all its members which can be challenging the partition can either be total or partial and the property can be partitioned through a physical division a total partition amounts to dissolution of the huf the entire joint family property is divided among all core personnels and the family ceases to exist as an huf in a partial partition one co-partner or more and their branch of the family may separate from the others the remaining co-partners may continue to be an huf in such a scenario the share in the huf properties of the person leaving the huf is determined and handed over to him or her only co-partners of an huf are entitled to carry out a partition any co-parsoner can through a written notice dissolve the hul an huf owning immovable properties can be dissolved by executing a partition deed or by filling of a partition suit among all the co-parsoners if only one or more core partners of the huf but not all agree to the partitions to a partition deed it cannot be called disillusion of the huf as the deed only extinguishes the core partners rights in an issue by detaching their interest in the joint property and the remaining continue as an hos so how much does an huf really help well mitchuf has both advantages and drawbacks benefits from huf can be multiplied and it can serve as a useful planning tool that can be used to maximize your benefits keeping in mind the interests of the family however it needs to be strategically planned with a long-term perspective while creating an huf may be useful for tax planning and investments one has to be mindful that it entails issues of joint families it poses legal challenges in dissolution and partitioning of the entity as the family grows the dissolution process becomes more tedious and even more complicated it not only gets emotionally taxing but also legally challenging to dissolve and distribute and huf's assets on that note we're wrapping up the video hope this was helpful please don't
